courses.title: College Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: First-Year Writing Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: African Literature in Translation
courses.description: Continental African literature. Content varies. May be repeated for credit with different topic. Taught with COMP_LIT 270-0; may not receive credit for both courses with same topic.

courses.title: Culture, Language, and Identity in South Africa
courses.description: Introduction to South African populations and cultures through direct experience and representations in history, art, literature, languages, and customs. Discussions of South African cultures and ethnicity, language use and policy, and identity. Restricted to students in Northwestern's South Africa program.

courses.title: Topics in African Studies
courses.description: A general examination of topics relevant to African studies. May be repeated for credit with change of topic. Restricted to students in Northwestern's South Africa program.

courses.title: Herskovits Undergraduate Research Award
courses.description: 2-course sequence required for recipients of the Herskovits Undergraduate Research Award. Consecutive enrollment required in AFST 392-2.

courses.title: Herskovits Undergraduate Research Award
courses.description: 2-course sequence required for recipients of the Herskovits Undergraduate Research Award.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: AFST 392-1.

courses.title: Senior Research Seminar
courses.description: Capstone seminar addressing both techniques of research and the substance of a significant issue in African studies. Students develop skills at formulating a research topic and organizing research.

courses.title: Independent Study
courses.description: May be repeated for credit with change of topic.

courses.title: Seminar for Majors
courses.description: Set of required courses structured to share a broad comparativist or internationally oriented theme, integrating methods and materials from different disciplines. Change of instructor each quarter; change of theme every year. Limited to 20 students.

courses.title: Seminar for Majors
courses.description: Set of required courses structured to share a broad comparativist or internationally oriented theme, integrating methods and materials from different disciplines. Change of instructor each quarter; change of theme every year. Limited to 20 students.

courses.title: Seminar for Majors
courses.description: Set of required courses structured to share a broad comparativist or internationally oriented theme, integrating methods and materials from different disciplines. Change of instructor each quarter; change of theme every year. Limited to 20 students.

courses.title: Studies in American Culture
courses.description: Readings and discussions of topics in American cultural life-for example, law in 20th century America or television news in contemporary US culture. Limited enrollment with emphasis on student participation. Prerequisites vary. May be repeated for credit with consent of program director.

courses.title: Senior Project
courses.description: Thesis or field study. Required of majors.

courses.title: Senior Project
courses.description: Thesis or field study. Required of majors.

courses.title: Independent Study
courses.description: This course is for American Studies majors doing independent research or reading with a faculty member. Must be approved by American Studies Director.

courses.title: College Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: First-Year Writing Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: Culture & Society
courses.description: Introduction to the comparative study of culture, exploring different types of social organization and their economic and political correlates in the context of contemporary globalization.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Human Origins
courses.description: Emergence of the human species through the process of organic evolution, emphasizing genetics, the fossil record, and comparison with our nearest living relatives.

courses.title: Archaeology: Unearthing History
courses.description: The evolution of culture from its earliest beginnings through the development of urbanism and the state. Principles of archaeological research.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: The Study of Culture through Language
courses.description: The scope of linguistic anthropology, from the study of language as an end in itself to the investigation of cultures through the medium of human languages.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Social and Health Inequalities
courses.description: Bidirectional relationship between social (e.g., class, gender, and racial/ethnic) and health inequalities, including institutional/structural, individual/family/psychosocial, and biological mechanisms.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Myth and Symbolism
courses.description: Introduction to different approaches to the interpretation of myth and symbolism, e.g., Freudian, functionalist, and structuralist.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Language in Asian America
courses.description: Survey of linguistic anthropological topics relevant to Asian American communities, including bilingualism, code switching, language socialization, language shift, style, sociolinguistic variation, indexicality, media, and semiotics. ANTHRO 235-0 and ASIAN_AM 235-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Food in Culture & Society
courses.description: This class explores food in all its cultural and social dimensions: deep history and origins; social, economic and political organization of the modern food industry; and cultural significance. Individual research projects allow in-depth study of one food; speakers, videos and experiential assignments take students out of the classroom to meet the people who sell, make, and grow our food.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Anthropology of Money
courses.description: A survey of cultural and ethnographic approaches to money and finance. Topics of investigation include “primitive money,” the uses of money in religious and ritual practices, social and cultural meanings of numbers, mobile money, crypto-currency and other alternative currency systems, and the politics of central banking.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: None.

courses.title: Porous Borders? Geography, Power and Techniques of Movement
courses.description: At the advent of globalization scholars have argued that the movements of capital, commodities and people across nation-states have rendered their borders increasingly porous. The death of the nation-state was announced elsewhere. Yet, in the epoch of offshored refugee processing centers and border walls, this assumed porosity of borders begs a reexamination of broader geographies of power and techniques of movement.

courses.title: Contemporary African Worlds
courses.description: Use of key anthropological insights about value judgments and cultural relativism to examine the survival strategies and turbulent histories of contemporary African societies.

courses.title: Introduction to Forensic Anthropology
courses.description: This course provides an introductory overview of forensic anthropology by reviewing a range of issues associated with human skeletal identification including recovery techniques, time since death, biological profile development, trauma analysis, mass disasters investigation, and ethical consideration in forensic anthropology. These will serve as a model for understanding the broader aspects of the interaction between anthropology and the medicolegal system.

courses.title: Topics in Anthropology
courses.description: Intermediate work in areas of developing interest and special significance. May be repeated for credit with different topic.

courses.title: Anthropology of Peace
courses.description: Cultural and ethnographic approaches to peace, peace building and peace activism. Topics of investigation include the concept of "peaceful societies," cultural mechanisms for conflict resolution, truth and reconciliation, the relationship between peace and commerce, and the role of literature, art and material culture in peace activism.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: None.

courses.title: Human Osteology
courses.description: Introduction to human skeletal anatomy and biology. Identification and classification of human bones through hands-on dry-lab-based analysis.

courses.title: Evolutionary Medicine
courses.description: In this course we explore how evolution has made our bodies susceptible to the role of environments and social forces, including factors like stress, discrimination and inequality. We consider fields at the interface of the social and biological, including genetics, epigenetics and social epidemiology.

courses.title: Archaeological Survey Methods
courses.description: Unique contributions of archaeological surveys to research about past peoples and places. Course uses geospatial technologies, such as shallow geophysics and GIS.

courses.title: The Maya
courses.description: The archaeology of the Maya in Latin America; life and society in pre-Columbian Maya civilization.

courses.title: Archaeology and Nationalism
courses.description: The course explores the role of archaeology in the creation and elaboration of national identities (18th C century to present): the institutionalization of archaeology; development of museums and practices of display/interpretation;archaeological sites as national monuments and tourist destinations; and cultural property legislation and artifact repatriation. HUM 329-0 and ANTHRO 329-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: GIS for the Social Sciences
courses.description: This course introduces Geographic Information Systems (GIS) as a tool for analyzing spatial phenomena in the social sciences. Students will explore the fundamental principles of GIS, learn to handle spatial data, and apply spatial analysis to address questions in the social sciences. Through seminar, labs, and projects, students will develop skills in spatial analysis, visualization, and critical interpretation of geographic data. Additionally, the course combines practical GIS skills with foundational knowledge of research design, equipping students to conceptualize, execute, and critically evaluate GIS-based studies. Students will learn to integrate spatial data with social science questions, explore analytical methods, and navigate ethical and methodological challenges in research.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Visual Anthropology of Africa
courses.description: Anthropological analysis of techniques, visual rhetoric, and narrative strategies embedded in images of Africa and Africans in a variety of contemporary and digital media. Course includes instruction in video production.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: 200-level social science or African studies course or consent of instructor.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: 200-level social science or African studies course or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Anthropology of Race
courses.description: Anthropological approaches to the analysis of race, racialization, and antiracism. Human variation, space, segregation, comparative analysis, and language ideologies.

courses.title: Black Queer Diaspora
courses.description: This course explores Black LGBTQ+ sexual cultures and social movements around the world. Across shared and divergent histories, Black LGBTQ+ people have been shaped by and continuously reshape conceptions of race, gender, and sexuality. Focusing on ethnography, Black queer theory, music, movies, photography, and other scholarly and artistic works, we study social practices and activism oriented towards freedom and flourishing.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Shady Business: Informal Economies in Contemporary Capitalism
courses.description: Taking stock of world economic changes such as the collapse of socialism, the advent of globalization as well as the intensification of transnational labor migration, this course aims to reveal the categorical distinctions drawn between formal and informal economies in contemporary capitalism as historically situated and politically charged constructs.

courses.title: Biocultural Perspectives on Water Insecurity
courses.description: Explore many ways that water impacts humans around the world through the lens of anthropology, religion, biology, environment, and politics. Why do individual’s experiences with water differ? How does water insecurity manifest? How do we measure it? How do we solve it? ANTHRO 357-0 and GBL_HLTH 357-0 are taught together; students may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Primate Behavior and Ecology
courses.description: This course provides an introductory overview of non-human primate behavior and ecology, covering topics nutrition, cognition, sociality, and conservation. No prerequisites.

courses.title: The Human Microbiome and Health
courses.description: Discussion-based analysis of cutting-edge research on the microbes associated with the human body and their impacts on health. Consideration of historical, social, and political influences on observed patterns.

courses.title: Language, Race, & Ethnicity in the U.S.
courses.description: Analysis of connections between language ideologies, language use, and meanings of race and ethnicity. Bilingualism, immigration, identity, accented English, African American English, language policy, English only movement, education, social change. ANTHRO 365-0 and ASIAN_AM 365-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.

courses.title: Latina and Latino Ethnography
courses.description: Sociocultural analysis of US Latina/o communities. Examines ethnographies by and about Latina/os based in the United States. Draws on a broad disciplinary basis to critique and elaborate on ethnographic methods and epistemologies.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: ANTHRO 211-0 or consent of instructor.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Contemporary Immigration to the U.S.
courses.description: Major theories in immigration studies; contemporary processes of immigration and immigrant "community building" in the United States.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: 300-level course in anthropology or sociology.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: 300-level course in anthropology or sociology.

courses.title: Advanced Methods in Forensic Anthropology
courses.description: This course provides a review of advanced methods in forensic anthropology. We will discuss the full range of issues associated with human skeletal identification from biological profile construction to trauma analysis. This class will include discussion of relevant literature in forensic anthropology and hands-on, dry lab activity to better appreciate the reality of practitioners in the field.

courses.title: Traveling While Muslim: Islam, Mobility, and Security after 9/11
courses.description: What are the stakes of traveling while Muslim in that post 9/11 era of racing Islam? How do we come to understand such mobility? In probing these questions, amongst others, in this seminar we aim to examine the interlocked relationship between Islam, mobility and security.

courses.title: Senior Seminar
courses.description: Supervised group discussion of research during preparation of the senior thesis project.

courses.title: Independent Study
courses.description: Open with consent of department to juniors and seniors who have completed with distinction at least 2 courses or the equivalent in anthropology. Under direction of individual members of department.

courses.title: Arabic I
courses.description: Three-course introduction to listening, speaking, reading, and writing Arabic. The course follows the integrated approach which blends the standard Arabic, Fuṣḥā (reading and writing) and the dialect/colloquial language, āmmiyya (speaking and listening) in a way that reflects the authentic practice of native Arabic speakers.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: None or placement exam results.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: None or placement exam results.

courses.title: Arabic I
courses.description: Three-course introduction to listening, speaking, reading, and writing Arabic. The course follows the integrated approach which blends the standard Arabic, Fuṣḥā (reading and writing) and the dialect/colloquial language, āmmiyya (speaking and listening) in a way that reflects the authentic practice of native Arabic speakers.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: ARABIC 111-1 or equivalent.

courses.title: Arabic I
courses.description: Three-course introduction to listening, speaking, reading, and writing Arabic. The course follows the integrated approach which blends the standard Arabic, Fuṣḥā (reading and writing) and the dialect/colloquial language, āmmiyya (speaking and listening) in a way that reflects the authentic practice of native Arabic speakers. Prerequisite: None or placement exam results.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: ARABIC 111-2 or equivalent.

courses.title: Conversation and Culture in the Arab World
courses.description: This course focuses one of the main dialects of Arabic (Egyptian, Levantine, Moroccan). This course is strongly recommended for students, undergraduate or graduate, who wish to further explore a spoken variety of Arabic. The course uses a communicative, proficiency-oriented approach with fully integrated audiovisual media to help students acquire a solid background on how to communicate using a dialect.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: 121-2.

courses.title: Arabic II
courses.description: Further development of grammar knowledge, reading, writing, speaking, and listening skills. Completion of at least this level is recommended for students seeking functional proficiency for study abroad.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: ARABIC 111-3 or equivalent.

courses.title: Arabic II
courses.description: Further development of grammar knowledge, reading, writing, speaking, and listening skills as well as cultural knowledge. The course follows the integrated approach which blends the standard Arabic, Fuṣḥā (reading and writing) and the dialect/colloquial language, āmmiyya (speaking and listening) in a way that reflects the authentic practice of native Arabic speakers.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: ARABIC 121-1 or equivalent.

courses.title: Arabic II
courses.description: Further development of grammar knowledge, reading, writing, speaking, and listening skills as well as cultural knowledge. The course follows the integrated approach which blends the standard Arabic, Fuṣḥā (reading and writing) and the dialect/colloquial language, āmmiyya (speaking and listening) in a way that reflects the authentic practice of native Arabic speakers.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: ARABIC 121-2 or equivalent.

courses.title: Media Arabic
courses.description: Introduction to vocabulary, expressions, and terminology used in Arab print and broadcast media.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: ARABIC 121-2 or equivalent.

courses.title: Arabic III
courses.description: Continued skills development of grammar knowledge, reading, writing, speaking, and listening skills as well as cultural knowledge. The course follows the integrated approach which blends the standard Arabic, Fuṣḥā (reading and writing) and the dialect/colloquial language, āmmiyya (speaking and listening) in a way that reflects the authentic practice of native Arabic speakers.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: ARABIC 121-3 or equivalent.

courses.title: Arabic III
courses.description: Continued skills development of grammar knowledge, reading, writing, speaking, and listening skills as well as cultural knowledge. The course follows the integrated approach which blends the standard Arabic, Fuṣḥā (reading and writing) and the dialect/colloquial language, āmmiyya (speaking and listening) in a way that reflects the authentic practice of native Arabic speakers.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: ARABIC 211-1 or equivalent.

courses.title: Arabic III
courses.description: Continued skills development of grammar knowledge, reading, writing, speaking, and listening skills as well as cultural knowledge. The course follows the integrated approach which blends the standard Arabic, Fuṣḥā (reading and writing) and the dialect/colloquial language, āmmiyya (speaking and listening) in a way that reflects the authentic practice of native Arabic speakers.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: ARABIC 211-2 or equivalent.

courses.title: Language and Culture
courses.description: In this course students will study important cultural themes in Arab society. In-depth exploration of a variety of historical and cultural topics from various sources help students build a comprehensive vocabulary as well as expand their reading knowledge and cultural understanding. The course will engage both, the MSA “frame” of narration as well as the spoken Arabic dialogue.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: ARABIC 121-3 or permission of the instructor.

courses.title: Current Events in the Middle East: Arab Society through Online News Media
courses.description: This course focuses on current events in the Middle East through in‐depth exploration of online News Media sources such as foreign newspaper articles and videos. Students will gain respect for alternative ideas and diversity of views as expressed in Arab news. The course focuses on reading, listening, discussion as well as building a comprehensive vocabulary in MSA and dialect.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: ARABIC 121-3 and ARABIC 125-0 or permission of the instructor. May be repeated for credit with different topic.

prereqSegments.value: or permission of the instructor. May be repeated for credit with different topic.

courses.title: Translation in Practice: A Bridge to the Future
courses.description: This unique foundational courses introduces students to translation skills to provide them with a competitive edge on the job market. Honing grammatical as well as stylistic facility in Arabic, the course promotes higher-level linguistic analysis and interpretation by following a problem-solving approach in translation.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Strong performance in two or average performance in three 200-level Arabic courses, or instructor permission.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: Strong performance in two or average performance in three 200-level Arabic courses, or instructor permission.

courses.title: Reading Arabic Poetry
courses.description: Advanced class reading, analyzing, and discussing G18 Arabic Poetry. Content varies. May be repeated for credit with different topic. Open to heritage speakers.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: 211-3 or equivalent.

courses.title: Reading Classical Arabic Texts
courses.description: Advanced class reading, analyzing, and discussing Classical Arabic texts. Content varies. May be repeated for credit with different topic. Open to heritage speakers.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: ARABIC 211-3 or equivalent.

courses.title: Reading Modern Arabic Prose
courses.description: Advanced class reading, analyzing, and discussing Modern Arabic prose. Content varies. May be repeated for credit with different topic. Open to heritage speakers.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: ARABIC 211-3 or equivalent.

courses.title: Independent Study
courses.description: For students who have advanced with distinction beyond the regular course offerings in Arabic.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: consent of instructor.

courses.title: College Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: First-Year Writing Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: Introduction to Drawing
courses.description: Introduction to basic drawing techniques and problems in line, space, perception, and the expressive use of various graphic media. No previous experience necessary.

courses.title: Introduction to Painting
courses.description: Introduction to problems in painting and visual thinking. Includes surface preparation, color mixing, and composition. No previous experience necessary.

courses.title: Introduction to Time Based Arts
courses.description: Introduction to a wide range of time-based art practices as used in the visual arts, including performance, sound, and video. No previous experience necessary.

courses.title: Introduction to Sculpture
courses.description: Introduction to basic sculptural materials and techniques and issues of three-dimensional form. No previous experience necessary.

courses.title: Introduction to Photography
courses.description: Extensive darkroom instruction focusing on aesthetic problems and the production of high-quality black-and-white prints. No previous experience necessary.

courses.title: Studio Practice
courses.description: Exploration of varied techniques and strategies geared toward the development of an individualized and self-directed studio practice.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: junior standing in the major, or junior or senior standing in the minor.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: junior standing in the major, or junior or senior standing in the minor.

courses.title: Contemporary Art Survey
courses.description: Forms and concerns of art from the 1960s to the present, introduced in slide lecture format.

courses.title: Critical Methods for Contemporary Art
courses.description: Introduction to basic key terms, concepts, and analytical categories of theoretical discourses relevant to an informed and critical engagement with contemporary art.

courses.title: Senior Critique
courses.description: Students complete a body of work to be shown in the senior exhibition, develop their critical skills, and learn to give articulate verbal and written expression to the concerns their art explores.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: ART 260-0 and Junior or Senior standing in major.

prereqSegments.value: and Junior or Senior standing in major.

courses.title: Independent Study
courses.description: For advanced majors pursuing projects outside the context of regularly offered courses.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: consent of department chair and director of undergraduate studies.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: consent of department chair and director of undergraduate studies.

courses.title: College Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: First-Year Writing Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: Introduction to African Art
courses.description: Introduction to art making in Africa; historical periods and regions of focus may vary depending on instructor.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Black Art in the TransAtlantic World
courses.description: Introduction to the visual arts and art history of the African diaspora, including the Caribbean, Brazil, and the United States, from the 19th century to present day.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Introduction to Ancient Art
courses.description: Introduction to the art and architecture of the ancient Near Eastern, Egyptian, Aegean, Greek, and Roman worlds.

courses.title: Introduction to Medieval Art
courses.description: Introduction to the art and architecture of Europe and the Middle East from the third to fifteenth centuries CE.

courses.title: Art and Visual Culture of the Islamic World
courses.description: An introduction to the art and architecture of the Islamic world from the seventh century to the present.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Introduction to Art of the United States
courses.description: Survey of the art and architecture of the United States in its cultural context, from the art of conquest to contemporary production.

courses.title: Introduction to the History of Architecture: 1400 to Present
courses.description: The theory and history of architecture in relation to cities and landscape; historical periods and regions of focus may vary depending on the instructor.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Introduction to Latin American Art
courses.description: Survey of the work of artists and groups from throughout the various countries of Central and South America from colonial times to the present.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Introduction to Asian Art
courses.description: Introduction to the art and architecture of Asia from ancient cultures to contemporary developments, including religious, court, and popular genres. Depending on the instructor, this course covers South/Southeast Asia or East Asia.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Introduction to Early Modern European Art
courses.description: Leading centers and artists of Europe from the later Middle Ages to the 19th century, with attention to their global context. Architecture, sculpture, painting, and graphic arts in relation to their social and cultural settings.

courses.title: Introduction to Modernism
courses.description: Conceptual introduction to modernism, covering art and visual culture from the late-19th century to the mid-20th century, with a focus on Europe and the United States.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Introduction to Contemporary Art
courses.description: Conceptual and thematic introduction to art since the 1960s, with attention to the impact of new technologies, social and political change, globalization, and the ongoing transformation of artistic production and distribution.

courses.title: Topics in Art, Revolution and Empire
courses.description: This course closely examines specific cases to consider how the politics of empire and revolution intersect with artistic, architectural and aesthetic forms and theories. The precise time period and locations considered vary depending on instructor.

courses.title: Exhibiting Antiquity: The Culture and Politics of Display
courses.description: Examination of the construction of Mediterranean antiquity through modes of reception since 1750. Analysis of programs of collecting and display and the intersection of institutional and scholarly agendas. ART_HIST 318-0, CLASSICS 397-0 and HUM 397-0 taught together; may receive credit for only 1 of these courses.

courses.title: Special Topics in Ancient Art
courses.description: Content varies depending on the expertise of the instructor and may engage with exhibitions and museums in Chicago and beyond. Past topics have included monsters and civilization; monument and commemoration in antiquity; narrative in ancient art; and the Roman provinces.

courses.title: Medieval Art: Byzantine
courses.description: Art and architecture of the Byzantine (Eastern Roman) empire in its larger Mediterranean, Middle Eastern and Eastern European context from the fourth to fifteenth century.

courses.title: Medieval Art: Late Medieval
courses.description: Art and architecture of Europe from the twelfth to fifteenth centuries.

courses.title: Medievalism in Video Games: Art, Culture and Theory
courses.description: Examination of the reconstruction of the medieval world in modern video games. Analysis of how real medieval environments and objects are imagined within digital formats and introduction to key methods of video game analysis.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Baroque Art: Italy & Spain 1600–1800
courses.description: Painting and sculpture in Italy, Spain, and Latin America from 1600–1800.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Eighteenth-Century European Art
courses.description: Survey of European art, architecture, decorative arts, and material culture of the “long eighteenth century” (ca. 1670 to ca. 1815 CE) with a specific attention paid to Europe’s colonial and imperial legacy during the period.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: 20th Century Art
courses.description: Painting, sculpture, architecture, design, and visual culture of the 20th century.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Art of the United States
courses.description: Survey of the arts and visual culture in the United States, encompassing architecture, painting, sculpture, photography, prints, film, and popular culture.

courses.title: Special Topics in Modern Art
courses.description: Content varies-for example, art of the Russian Revolution; the avant-garde; totalitarian art; art during war; modernism and its discontents; art and decolonization; medium specificity.

courses.title: Media Theory
courses.description: Comprehensive introduction from a humanistic perspective to theories about the nature of media and the role of technology in modern culture.

courses.title: Black Visual Culture: Race and Representation
courses.description: Examination of how visual representations and technologies of vision have been used to create, transform, or destabilize the idea of race as it pertains to people in Africa and/or the African diaspora at specific historical moments.

courses.title: Special Topics in the Arts of Africa
courses.description: Content varies depending on the expertise of the instructor and may engage with exhibitions and museums in Chicago and beyond. Past topics have included contemporary African art and African photography.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Undergraduate Seminar
courses.description: Intended for advanced reseach and writing in art history. Past topics have included art and ecology, Japanese prints, and Ottoman architecture in Egypt.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: 300-level art history course.

courses.title: Art Historical Methods Seminar
courses.description: Introduction to the historiography of art history and to the different methodological approaches to the study of art and visual culture.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: 300-level art history course.

courses.title: Museums
courses.description: Museum studies seminars. Content varies-for example, the history of museums, their ethical basis, community responsibilities, educational prerogatives, and future directions.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: 300-level art history course.

courses.title: Internship in the Arts
courses.description: Direct participation, with oversight by the director of undergraduate studies, in the curatorial/educational activities of an established arts organization. By petition, on a limited basis; may be taken only once.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: 300-level art history course or consent of instructor.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: 300-level art history course or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Independent Study
courses.description: Special projects involving reading and conferences with a supervising professor. Arranged in exceptional circumstances. Two quarters required for students writing a senior thesis in art history.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: 300-level art history course.

courses.title: College Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: First-Year Writing Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: Topics in Asian American Social and Cultural Analysis
courses.description: Issues and themes in Asian American society and culture. Recent topics include the Arab American studies, student protests, and minority conservatisms. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Introduction to Asian American Studies
courses.description: Origins of the field, emerging trajectories, core concepts, theories and methodologies. Analyzes race, gender, immigration, diaspora, class, labor, and sexuality as primary subjects of the field.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Asian American History
courses.description: Introduction to the history of Asians in the United States, with a focus on their impact on American society as well as their experiences within the United States. ASIAN_AM 214-0 and HISTORY 214-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.
distroTags: U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Global Asians
courses.description: Survey of Asian diasporas in the United States and elsewhere in the 19th and 20th centuries, emphasizing causes of migration, process of settlement, relations with other ethnic groups, and construction of diasporic identities. ASIAN_AM 216-0 and HISTORY 216-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Asian/Black Historical Relations in the U.S.
courses.description: Comparative historical analysis of relations of these groups in the United States, including racialized and sexualized discourses structuring interracial relations and social, political, and economic location. Slavery, immigration, model minority myth, cross-racial politics. BLK_ST 218-0 and ASIAN_AM 218-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Topics in History
courses.description: Exploration of theme, event, region, or historical period, with emphasis on historical interpretation. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.

courses.title: Asian American Communities
courses.description: Critical examination of post-1965 Asian American communities in light of demographic, social, racial, and economic trends in the United States and Asia.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Language in Asian America
courses.description: Survey of linguistic anthropological topics relevant to Asian American communities, including bilingualism, code switching, language socialization, language shift, style, sociolinguistic variation, indexicality, media, and semiotics. ANTHRO 235-0 and ASIAN_AM 235-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Asian Americans and Popular Culture
courses.description: Examination of the varied roles of Asian Americans in U.S. popular culture, from representations to cultural production, historically and today.
distroTags: U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Introduction to Critical Mixed Race Studies
courses.description: Exploration of demographic trends in interracial and interethnic marriages to highlight the complexity of the American experience. Special attention to mixed-race experiences portrayed in film and novels. BLK_ST 251-0 and ASIAN_AM 251-0 are taught together; students may not receive credit for both courses.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Introduction to Asian American Literature
courses.description: Introduction to Asian American literature from the late 19th century to the present, covering a range of genres and ethnicities. ASIAN_AM 275-0 and ENGLISH 275-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.
distroTags: U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Topics in Literary and Cultural Studies
courses.description: Close study of Asian American literary and cultural texts within a theme, genre, or other organizing criterion. May be repeated for credit with a different topic. ASIAN_AM 276-0 and ENGLISH 276-0 are taught together.
distroTags: U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Advanced Topics in Social and Cultural Analysis
courses.description: Detailed exploration of an issue and its ramifications in Asian American society and culture. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Asian American Women's History
courses.description: Exploration of race, gender, and the contours of US history from the perspective of Asian American women's experiences. Considers migration, exclusion, labor, marriage, family, sexuality, and cross-racial alliances. ASIAN_AM 304-0 and HISTORY 304-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.

courses.title: Contemporary Asian Black Relations
courses.description: Divides between these groups, as well as areas of positive cross-cultural collaboration. Historical analysis of reparations, the 1992 Los Angeles riots, and affirmative action. Cross-racial exchange in youth expressions, popular culture, hip-hop. BLK_ST 310-0 and ASIAN_AM 310-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.

courses.title: Advanced Topics in History
courses.description: Close study of Asian American history within a theme, event, or other organizing criterion, with emphasis on primary documents, historical interpretation, and research. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.

courses.title: Asian/American Gaming
courses.description: Critical examination into the global circuits of gaming with an emphasis on production, content, aesthetics, and sociality.

courses.title: Asian American Religions
courses.description: Analysis of the role of religion in Asian American communities; how experiences as immigrants and as racial and ethnic minorities shape religious practices, communities, theologies, and identities.

courses.title: Advanced Topics in Sexuality, Gender, and Race
courses.description: Exploration of the intersections of gender, race, and sexuality, the construction of masculinity and femininity, and the role of gender and sexuality in the life experiences of Asian Americans. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.

courses.title: Language, Race, and Ethnicity in the U.S.
courses.description: Analysis of connections between language ideologies, language use, and meanings of race and ethnicity. Bilingualism, immigration, identity, accented English, African American English, language policy, English only movement, education, social change. ASIAN_AM 365-0 and ANTHRO 365-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.

courses.title: Asian American Sexualities
courses.description: Advanced exploration into queer theory and sexuality studies in Asian America, with an emphasis on the politics, ethics, and power of racialized sexualities. Examination of how sexuality impacts the lived experience of immigration, race, gender, class, and disability among Asian Americans.
distroTags: U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Advanced Topics in Diaspora
courses.description: Exploration of the ideas of diaspora and homeland and their implications for rethinking immigration and migration as they relate to the experiences of Asian Americans. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.

courses.title: Advanced Topics in Literature and Culture
courses.description: Close study of Asian American literary and cultural texts within a theme, genre, or other organizing criterion, with an emphasis on theory and its application. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.
distroTags: U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: War and Empire
courses.description: Examination of US-Asia connections through history and representations of US wars in cultural and historical texts.

courses.title: Advanced Topics in Arts and Performance
courses.description: Analysis of Asian American contributions to the art, visual culture, and film of the U.S. Exploration of the dynamics of race, gender, and class in Asian American dance, theater, film, art, and visual culture. May be repeated for credit with different topic.

courses.title: Advanced Seminar for Majors and Minors
courses.description: Seminar on a topic in areas related to Asian American social structure and culture. May be repeated for credit with different topic.

courses.title: Independent Study In Asian American Studies
courses.description: Readings and conferences on special subjects for students pursuing areas of interest in Asian American studies.

courses.title: College Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: First-Year Writing Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: Introductory Topics in Chinese Literature and Culture
courses.description: Introduction to topics in Chinese literature and culture, from precolonial to contemporary periods and contexts. May be repeated for credit with change of topic. No prerequisites.

courses.title: Introduction to Modern Chinese Literature and Culture
courses.description: Introduction to topics in the history of cultural production in modern China between the 19th century and present day. May be repeated for credit with change of topic. No prerequisites.

courses.title: Modern Chinese Popular Culture I
courses.description: Introduction to the history of modern Chinese popular cultural production between the mid-19th century and 1949. The course is designed around the introduction and adaptation of four media technologies: photography, film, mass print culture, and sound recording. No prerequisites.

courses.title: Modern Chinese Popular Culture II
courses.description: Introduction to popular cultural production in Mainland China, Taiwan, and Hong Kong between 1949 and the present. ASIAN_LC 204-0 is recommended. No prerequisites.

courses.title: Introductory Topics in Japanese Literature and Culture
courses.description: Introduction to topics in Japanese literature and culture, from premodern to contemporary periods. May be repeated for credit with change of topic. No prerequisites.

courses.title: Introduction to Premodern Japanese Literature and Culture
courses.description: Introduction to Japanese literature and culture from the earliest writings to the dawn of the modern era (8th-19th c.): poetry, narrative fiction, essays, diaries, theater. Focus is on reading literary texts in historical context. May be repeated for credit with change of topic. No prerequisites.

courses.title: Introduction to Modern Japanese Literature and Culture
courses.description: Introduction to Japanese literature, thought, and culture from the mid-19th to mid-20th century: fiction, poetry, theater, essays. Focus is on reading literary texts in relation to historical context. May be repeated for credit with change of topic. No prerequisites.

courses.title: Introduction to Contemporary Japanese Literature and Culture
courses.description: Introduction to Japanese literature, thought, and culture from the mid-20th to early 21st century: fiction, poetry, theater, essays, animation, manga. Focus is on reading literary texts in relation to historical context. May be repeated for credit with change of topic. No prerequisites.

courses.title: Introduction to Japanese Film, Media, and Visual Culture
courses.description: Introduction to Japanese media and visual culture. Topics may include: pre-modern visual narrative, pre-modern print culture, cinema, animation, manga, video games. Focus is on reading media and visual culture in relation to historical context. May be repeated for credit with change of topic. No prerequisites.

courses.title: Introductory Topics in Korean Literature and Culture
courses.description: Introduction to topics in Korean literature and culture, from precolonial to contemporary periods and contexts. May be repeated for credit with change of topic. No prerequisites.

courses.title: Introductory Topics in South Asian Literature and Culture
courses.description: Introduction to topics in South Asian literature and culture, from precolonial to contemporary periods and contexts. May be repeated with change of topic. No prerequisites.

courses.title: South Asian Popular Cultures
courses.description: This course offers an introduction to the intersecting worlds of popular literature, digital media, folk arts, performance, and politics of South Asia with a focus on the relationship between artistic expression, social reform and political change. May be repeated for credit with change of topic. No prerequisites.

courses.title: South Asian Cinemas
courses.description: Narrative and aesthetic approaches to understanding popular cinemas in South Asia with special attention to historical, local, and global contexts. May be repeated for credit with change of topic. No prerequisites.

courses.title: Introductory Topics in Asian Languages and Cultures
courses.description: Content and prerequisites vary. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.

courses.title: Advanced Studies in Asian Film, Media, and Visual Culture
courses.description: Advanced topics in Asian film, media, and visual culture. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: ASIAN_LC 202-0 or ASIAN_LC 204-0 or ASIAN_LC 205-0 or ASIAN_LC 224-0 or ASIAN_LC 265-0 (C- or better); or consent of instructor.

prereqSegments.value: (C- or better); or consent of instructor.

distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Research Seminar
courses.description: A research seminar in Asian Languages and Cultures, usually taken in the junior or senior year.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: consent of the instructor.

courses.title: Independent Study
courses.description: The independent study is designed for advanced undergraduates interested in studying topics not otherwise covered in normal ALC courses.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: consent of director of undergraduate studies and instructor.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: consent of director of undergraduate studies and instructor.

courses.title: Modern Cosmology
courses.description: Modern views on the structure of the universe, its past, present, and future. For nonscience majors who want to take a more detailed course after completing ASTRON 120-0.

courses.title: Milky Way Galaxy
courses.description: Structure of the galaxy, star formation, interstellar clouds and dust, star clusters, neutron stars and black holes, the galactic center. For nonscience majors who want to take a more detailed course after completing ASTRON 120-0.

courses.title: Solar System
courses.description: The planets and their moons, the sun, comets, asteroids. For nonscience majors who want to take a more detailed course after completing ASTRON 120-0.

courses.title: College Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: First-Year Writing Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: A Brief Journey Through the Invisible Universe
courses.description: A conceptual course exploring the invisible radio universe. Topics include: the historical development of the radio sky, how radio telescopes are fundamentally different than optical telescopes, numerous discoveries with multiple Nobel Prizes over the last century (pulsars, quasars, the Big Bang cosmic microwave background radiation, organic molecules, shadows of supermassive black holes) and search for radio signals from alien civilizations.

courses.title: Introduction to Astrobiology
courses.description: The modern scientific perspective on the question of life elsewhere in the universe. The prospects for life on Mars. The discovery of extrasolar planets and the search for extrasolar biospheres.

courses.title: Highlights of Astronomy
courses.description: Acquaints students with modern ideas about the solar system, stars, galaxies, and the universe. Emphasizes fundamental principles and underlying concepts.

courses.title: Introduction to Astrophysics I: Life Cycle of Stars and Planets
courses.description: The course will explore the origin and evolution of star/planet systems, focusing on underlying physical processes and observational techniques. We will discuss the recent discovery of thousands of planets orbiting stars other than our Sun - a.k.a. "exoplanets". We will also discuss stellar remnant black holes. (Prerequisites: PHYSICS 135-1, PHYSICS 135-2 (concurrent registration in PHYSICS 135-2 is acceptable).

courses.title: Introduction to Astrophysics II: Galactic Evolution and Cosmology
courses.description: The course will explore modern cosmology, including dark matter, the Big Bang, curved space-time, the origin and evolution of the first stars and galaxies, cosmic acceleration, and dark energy. In all cases, the focus will be on the underlying physical processes and the observational techniques used. (Prerequisites: ASTRON 220-1 or all of PHYSICS 135-1, PHYSICS 135-2, and PHYSICS 135-3.)

courses.title: Basics of Radio Astronomy
courses.description: Radio astronomy is the study of natural radio emission from the sky, providing important clues about the history of the universe. Topics include how a radio telescope receives invisible signals, how dramatically the radio sky differs from the optical sky, and interferometry.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PHYSICS 135-1, PHYSICS 135-2, PHYSICS 135-3 (or equivalent) or PHYSICS 332-0; MATH 220-2 or equivalent.

courses.title: Planetary Astrophysics
courses.description: Methods of exoplanet detection. The observed architecture of exoplanetary systems. Formation and evolution of planetary systems. Modeling exoplanet interiors and atmospheres. Exoplanet habitability and the search for bio-signatures.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PHYSICS 330-1 and PHYSICS 330-2, or equivalent.

courses.title: Observational Astrophysics
courses.description: Geometric optics applied to design of optical and x-ray telescopes; diffraction and the Airy disk; radio and optical interferometry and aperture synthesis; adaptive optics; recent developments in detector technology; quantum and thermal noise in astronomy. Independent research projects using the CCD camera and 18-inch refractor in Dearborn Observatory.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: ASTRON 220-1 or ASTRON 220-2.

courses.title: Stellar Astrophysics
courses.description: Physics of stellar interiors, stellar atmospheres, and star formation. Specific topics include simple stellar models, nuclear energy generation, overview of evolutionary phases, white dwarfs, neutron stars, interstellar gas and dust grains, gravitational collapse.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: ASTRON 220-1 or ASTRON 220-2.

courses.title: Extragalactic Astrophysics and Cosmology
courses.description: Big bang cosmology, thermal history of the universe, primordial nucleosynthesis, microwave background, dark matter, largescale structure, galaxy formation, spiral and elliptical galaxies, groups and clusters of galaxies.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: ASTRON 220-1 or ASTRON 220-2.

courses.title: Astrophysics for ISP
courses.description: Stellar structure and evolution: nucleosynthesis, supernova phenomena, white dwarfs, neutron stars, and black holes. Limited to students enrolled in ISP or with consent of the physics department.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PHYSICS 135-1, PHYSICS 135-2, & PHYSICS 135-3, or equivalent.

courses.title: Current Topics in Astronomy
courses.description: Explores in detail an area of current research interest in astrophysics. Contact the department or instructor for specifics. May be repeated for credit with change of topic. Prerequisites vary.

courses.title: Independent Study
courses.description: Opportunity to study an advanced subject under the individual direction of a faculty member. Open to all advanced students. Consent of instructor required.

courses.title: Introduction to Biological Sciences at Northwestern
courses.description: For participants in Bio&ChemEXCEL summer program. An overview of recent advances in biological research and leadership within the field of biology. Taken with CHEM 100-0.

courses.title: College Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: First-Year Writing Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: Diversity of Life
courses.description: Comparative survey of organisms, emphasizing adaptation and phylogenetic relationships. Particular emphasis on animals.

courses.title: Plant-People Interactions
courses.description: Biology and history of the interaction of humans and flowering plants.

courses.title: The Nature of Plants
courses.description: Plant adaptations for growth, survival, and reproduction. Plant defense against herbivory, pollination, and seed dispersal.

courses.title: College Seminar
courses.description: For participants in the NUBioscientist program. Biological Thought & Action; preparatory to BIOL_SCI 116-6.

courses.title: First-Year Writing Seminar
courses.description: For participants in the NUBioscientist program. Science Research Preparation; follows BIOL_SCI 115-6.

courses.title: Human Genetics
courses.description: Basic principles of human inheritance and genetic variation.

courses.title: Basic Genetics and Evolution
courses.description: Principles of inheritance as they apply to evolution. May not receive credit after taking BIOL_SCI 203-0.

courses.title: Molecular Biology
courses.description: This course focuses on how information is stored and propagated in DNA, and used and regulated to generate proteins at the proper time and location. It also applies this information to understanding fundamentals of biotechnology.

courses.title: Mentored Study Program
courses.description: Study sessions facilitated by undergraduate peer leaders, for students enrolled in BIOL_SCI 201-0. Meets weekly in small groups to collaboratively review material, solve practice problems, clarify concepts, and enhance study strategies. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U.

courses.title: Peer-Guided SG: Molecular Biology
courses.description: Meets weekly in small groups, along with a peer facilitator, to collaboratively review material, work through practice problems, and clarify course concepts. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U. Co-requisite: BIOL_SCI 201-0.

courses.title: Cell Biology
courses.description: This course covers how biomolecules function together to generate the complexity of cells, and how cells behave collectively to communicate with each other and to enact key decisions, such as proliferation and cell death.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Students must have completed, with a C- or better, BIOL_SCI 201-0 to register for this course. Must be taken concurrently with BIOL_SCI 232-0.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: Students must have completed, with a C- or better,
to register for this course. Must be taken concurrently with

courses.title: Peer-Guided SG: Cell Biology
courses.description: Meets weekly in small groups, along with a peer facilitator, to collaboratively review material, work through practice problems, and clarify course concepts. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U. Co-requisite: BIOL_SCI 202-0.

courses.title: Genetics and Evolution
courses.description: This course provides an analytic framework for studying the flow of biological information across generations, and understanding how phenotypes reveal biological mechanisms. This framework is applied to development, cancer, the history of life, and mechanisms governing the evolution and distribution of organisms over time.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Students must have completed, with a C- or better, BIOL_SCI 202-0 to register for this course. Must be taken concurrently with BIOL_SCI 233-0.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: Students must have completed, with a C- or better,
to register for this course. Must be taken concurrently with

courses.title: Peer-Guided Study Group: Genetics and Evolution
courses.description: Meets weekly in small groups, along with a peer facilitator, to collaboratively review material, work through practice problems, and clarify course concepts. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U. Co-requisite: BIOL_SCI 203-0.

courses.title: Undergraduate Teaching Assistant
courses.description: Prerequisite: consent of instructor.

courses.title: Molecular and Cellular Processes Laboratory
courses.description: Laboratory techniques and experiments in fundamental aspects of cell and molecular biology. Must be taken concurrently with BIOL_SCI 202-0 or BIOL_SCI 240-0.

courses.title: Genetics and Molecular Processes Laboratory
courses.description: Laboratory techniques and experiments in fundamental aspects of transmission genetics and molecular biology.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Students must have completed BIOL_SCI 232-0. Must be taken concurrently with BIOL_SCI 203-0 or BIOL_SCI 241-0.

courses.title: Investigative Laboratory
courses.description: A culminating life-science laboratory experience.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Students must have completed BIOL_SCI 233-0.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Biological Mechanisms
courses.description: A mechanistic view of molecular biology, emphasizing experimental approaches. Students will be introduced to empirical methods and the logical framework employed to generate our current understanding of molecular biology. Key concepts from chemistry, biochemistry and genetics will form the foundation for discussing molecular biology. For each topic, key papers that led to the discoveries in the textbook will be examined.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM 151-0 or CHEM 171-0. Enrollment by successful score on the annual BioSci placement exam or permission of instructor.

prereqSegments.value: . Enrollment by successful score on the annual BioSci placement exam or permission of instructor.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Cellular Processes
courses.description: This course focuses on how biomolecules function together to generate the complexity of cells, and how cells behave collectively to communicate with each other and enact core behaviors such as cell secretion, intracellular transport, proliferation, motility, and cell death.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: BIOL SCI 239-0, co-requisite BIOL SCI 232-0.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: BIOL SCI 239-0, co-requisite BIOL SCI 232-0.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Multicellular Complexity
courses.description: This course explores the developmental and evolutionary mechanisms that led to the emergence and complexity of multicellular life in animals. Topics include the evolution of multicellularity, cell communication, morphogenesis, and the genetic and molecular mechanisms that drive complexity. We will also examine examples of developmental complexity across major animal phyla and discuss current research in evolutionary developmental biology (evo-devo).
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: BIOL SCI 240-0; co-requisite BIOL SCI 233-0.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: BIOL SCI 240-0; co-requisite BIOL SCI 233-0.

courses.title: Principles of Biochemistry
courses.description: Biochemical processes.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: Students must have completed BIOL_SCI 201-0 or BIOL_SCI 239-0, and CHEM 210-1 or CHEM 212-1 or CHEM 215-1 or CHEM 217-1 to register for this course.

courses.title: Peer-Guided Study Group: Principles of Biochemistry
courses.description: Peer-guided study group for students enrolled in BIOL_SCI 301-0. Meets weekly in small groups, along with a peer facilitator, to collaboratively review material, work through practice problems, and clarify course concepts. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U.

courses.title: Molecular and Structural Neurobiology
courses.description: Exploration of the overlap between neurobiology and molecular biology.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Students must have completed BIOL_SCI 301-0 to register for this course.

courses.title: Brain Structure, Function, and Evolution
courses.description: An overview of the evolution of the nervous system and cognition, from the origin of neurons to the structure and function of the human brain. No P/N.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Students must have completed BIOL SCI 202-0 or BIOL_SCI 240-0, 203-0 or BIOL_SCI 241-0, and BIOL_SCI 301-0; or NEUROSCI 206-0 in order to register for this course.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: Students must have completed BIOL SCI 202-0 or
in order to register for this course.

courses.title: Human Physiology
courses.description: An exploration of the functions of the human body at the tissue, organ, and organ system level. Emphasis on homeostatic mechanisms and interdependence within organs and organ systems and the influence of modulatory systems. Topics will include, but are not limited to: nervous, cardiovascular, respiratory, and renal systems.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: Students must have completed BIOL_SCI 201-0 or BIOL_SCI 239-0, BIOL_SCI 202-0 or BIOL_SCI 240-0, and CHEM 132-0, CHEM 152-0, or CHEM 172-0.

courses.title: Virology and Antiviral Immunity
courses.description: Virus structure, synthesis of viral nucleic acids and proteins, the interaction of the viral and cellular genomes.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: Students must have completed BIOL_SCI 202-0 or BIOL_SCI 240-0, BIOL_SCI 203-0 or BIOL_SCI 241-0, and BIOL_SCI 301-0 to register for this course.

courses.title: Animal Physiology
courses.description: Physiological principles and mechanisms responsible for the ability of animals to regulate variables in the steady state. Prerequisite: Students must have completed BIOL_SCI 310-0 to register for this course.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Students must have completed BIOL SCI 202-0 or BIOL_SCI 240-0, 203-0 or BIOL_SCI 241-0, and BIOL_SCI 301-0 to enroll.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: Students must have completed BIOL SCI 202-0 or

courses.title: Conservation Genetics
courses.description: Critical issues in the management and understanding of endangered populations.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Students must have completed BIOL_SCI 203-0 or ENVR_SCI 202-0 to register for this course.

prereqSegments.value: or ENVR_SCI 202-0 to register for this course.

courses.title: Plant-Animal Interactions
courses.description: Plant-animal interactions, and their consequences for individuals, populations, ecological communities, and ecosystems. Examination of how these interactions are responding to ongoing global factors such as anthropogenic habitat destruction and climate change.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Students must have completed BIOL_SCI 203-0, or BIOL_SCI 241-0, or BIOL_SCI 339-0, or BIOL_SCI 341-0, or BIOL_SCI 342-0, or ENVR_SCI 202-0 to register for this course.

prereqSegments.value: , or ENVR_SCI 202-0 to register for this course.

courses.title: Spring Flora
courses.description: Life cycles, vegetative and reproductive structures, and adaptations for pollination and fruit and seed dispersal of the wildflowers, trees, and shrubs of oak woodland.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Students must have completed BIOL_SCI 203-0, BIOL_SCI 241-0, or BIOL_SCI 339-0, or BIOL_SCI 341-0, or BIOL_SCI 342-0, or ENVR_SCI 202-0 to register for this course.

prereqSegments.value: , or ENVR_SCI 202-0 to register for this course.

courses.title: Biostatistics
courses.description: Approaches, methods, and techniques for analyzing datasets in ecology and conservation biology.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: BIOL_SCI 201-0, or BIOL_SCI 239-0, or ENVR_SCI 202-0, and MATH 218-3 or MATH 220-2.
distroTags: Empirical and Deductive Reasoning Foundational Dis

courses.title: Critical Topics in Ecology and Conservation
courses.description: Seminar discussing historical and modern publications in the field.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Students must have completed BIOL_SCI 203-0, or BIOL_SCI 241-0, or BIOL_SCI 341-0, or BIOL_SCI 342-0, or ENVR_SCI 202-0 to register for this course.

prereqSegments.value: , or ENVR_SCI 202-0 to register for this course.

courses.title: Evolutionary Processes
courses.description: Evolutionary mechanisms (natural selection, genetic drift), evolutionary history (speciation, phylogenetics), and adaptations (sex, cooperation, aging, life history).
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: Students must have completed BIOL_SCI 203-0 or BIOL_SCI 241-0, and BIOL_SCI 337-0 or another course in statistics to register for this course.

prereqSegments.value: or another course in statistics to register for this course.

courses.title: Community & Population Ecology
courses.description: Abundance, distribution, diversity, and scaling in plant communities in space-time.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Students must have completed BIOL_SCI 203-0, or BIOL_SCI 241-0, or BIOL_SCI 339-0, or BIOL_SCI 341-0, or BIOL_SCI 342-0, or ENVR_SCI 202-0 to register for this course.

prereqSegments.value: , or ENVR_SCI 202-0 to register for this course.

courses.title: Plant Evolution and Diversity Lab
courses.description: Introduction to the diversity and evolutionary history of land plants.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Students must have completed BIOL_SCI 203-0, or BIOL_SCI 241-0, or BIOL_SCI 339-0, or BIOL_SCI 341-0, or BIOL_SCI 342-0, or ENVR_SCI 202-0 to register for this course.

prereqSegments.value: , or ENVR_SCI 202-0 to register for this course.

courses.title: Principles of Cell Signaling
courses.description: Emphasis on principles, components, and logic that are common to different cell signaling systems. Modern experimental strategies for studying cellular signaling as well as the implications of disrupting cell communication pathways in disease will be described.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: Students must have completed BIOL_SCI 202-0 or BIOL_SCI 240-0, and BIOL_SCI 203-0 or BIOL_SCI 241-0 to register for this course.

courses.title: Biophysics
courses.description: Protein interaction with small molecules; protein tertiary structure determination.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: Students must have completed BIOL_SCI 202-0 or BIOL_SCI 240-0, BIOL_SCI 203-0 or BIOL_SCI 241-0, and BIOL_SCI 301-0 to register for this course.

courses.title: The Human Microbiome
courses.description: Course explores different communities of microorganisms in the human body – the gut, urogenital, oral, and skin microbiota, and how these communities contribute to or are altered in health and disease. Topics will include but are not limited to: the contribution of these communities to digestion and gut health, mood, obesity, the immune system, fertility and pregnancy, and neurological disorders.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: BIOL_SCI 201-0 or BIOL_SCI 239-0, BIOL_SCI 202-0 or BIOL_SCI 240-0, and BIOL_SCI 301-0 to register for this course.

courses.title: Proteomics and Disease
courses.description: Homeostasis of the proteome, and how breakdown of homeostatic mechanisms leads to disease. Topics include gene expression, the biochemistry of protein folding, the various pathways that ensure proper protein folding, and the destruction of mis-folded proteins and aggregates. Reading and discussion of primary literature; worksheets and problem sets in a collaborative learning environment.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: BIOL_SCI 203-0, BIOL_SCI 234-0, and BIOL_SCI 301-0.

courses.title: Biology of Cancer
courses.description: The disease of cancer: causation at the cellular and molecular levels; treatment.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: Students must have completed BIOL_SCI 202-0 or BIOL_SCI 240-0, BIOL_SCI 203-0 or BIOL_SCI 241-0, and BIOL_SCI 301-0 to register for this course. to register for this course.

prereqSegments.value: to register for this course. to register for this course.

courses.title: Stem Cells and Regeneration
courses.description: Developmental and molecular biology of tissue regeneration, with regard to regeneration from embryonic or adult stem cells. Discussion of conserved developmental pathways necessary for regeneration. Applications in regenerative medicine.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: Students must have completed BIOL_SCI 202-0 or BIOL_SCI 240-0, and BIOL_SCI 203-0 or BIOL_SCI 241-0 to register for this course.

courses.title: Morphogenesis
courses.description: Development of overarching principles alongside classic readings of experiments exploring key concepts in developmental biology.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: Students must have completed BIOL_SCI 202-0 or BIOL_SCI 240-0, BIOL_SCI 203-0 or BIOL_SCI 241-0, BIOL_SCI 234-0, and BIOL_SCI 301-0 to register for this course.

courses.title: Human Genomics
courses.description: This course will examine how the analysis of the human genome and its variation provides insight into diversity, human health and our evolutionary history.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: BIOL_SCI 203-0 or BIOL_SCI 241-0.

courses.title: Evolution and Diversity: Mushroom Genetics and Genomics
courses.description: The occurrence of natural genetic variation is the raw material with which evolution has sculpted every species that has ever existed. In this laboratory-based course, students are immersed in the world of a widespread and biologically famous mushroom-forming fungus.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: Students must have completed BIOL_SCI 202-0 or BIOL_SCI 240-0, BIOL_SCI 203-0 or BIOL_SCI 241-0, and BIOL_SCI 301-0 to register for this course.

courses.title: Senior Thesis Colloquium
courses.description: Supervision while writing a Senior Thesis. Discussion of students' research. Instructor feedback on thesis drafts. Continued student research. Enrollment limited to Senior Biological Sciences majors hoping to graduate with Program Honors and/or to produce a Senior Thesis. Registration required for all Honors candidates.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: BIOL_SCI 398-0 or BIOL_SCI 399-0, plus permission of instructor.

courses.title: Tutorial in Biology
courses.description: Supervised reading and discussion or supervised laboratory work. P/N only.

courses.title: Independent Research
courses.description: Supervised independent research project.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: BIOL_SCI 398-0 or previous BIOL_SCI 399-0.

courses.title: College Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thrive at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: First-Year Writing Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: Introduction to African American Literature
courses.description: Literature of Black people in the United States from slavery to freedom. Works of major writers and significant but unsung bards of the past.
