ENGLISH 266-0 and BLK_ST 210-0 are taught together; may receive credit for only 1 of these courses. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.
distroTags: U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Literatures of the Black World
courses.description: Introductory survey of fiction, poetry, drama, folktales, and other literary forms of Africa and the African diaspora. Texts may span the precolonial, colonial, and postcolonial periods and cover central themes, such as memory, trauma, spirituality, struggle, identity, freedom, and humor.

courses.title: Introduction to African-American History: Key concepts from 1700-1861
courses.description: African origins, the slave trade, origins of slavery and racism in the United States, life under slavery in the North and the South. BLK_ST and HISTORY 212-1 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.
distroTags: U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Introduction to African American History: Emancipation to Civil Rights Movement
courses.description: Emancipation to the civil rights era. Reconstruction, rise of legal segregation, strategies of resistance, migration, and urbanization. BLK_ST 212-2 and HISTORY 212-2 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.
distroTags: U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: History of the Black World
courses.description: Introductory survey of the history of Africans and their descendants across the globe. African civilizations prior to European colonialism, encounters between Africa and Europe, movements of "Africans" to the Americas and elsewhere, and development of Black Introductory survey of the history of Africans and their descendants across the globe. African civilizations prior to European colonialism, encounters between Africa and Europe, movements of "Africans" to the Americas and elsewhere, and development of Bblack communities in and outside Africa.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Comparative Race and Ethnic Studies
courses.description: Problems and experiences of racialized minorities: Blacks, Native Americans, Asian Americans, and Latina/o peoples. Comparison of their relationships with each other and with the majority society. Historical Studies Distro Area Social Behavioral Sciences Distro Area Social and Behavioral Science.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Introduction to Black Social & Political Life
courses.description: Analysis of class, gender, sexuality, immigrant status, and ethnic origin in Black society and politics. Focus on demographic trends, lived experiences, and ideological debates.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Asian/Black Historical Relations in the U.S.
courses.description: Comparative historical analysis of relations of these groups in the United States, including racialized and sexualized discourses structuring interracial relations and social, political, and economic location. Slavery, immigration, model minority myth, cross-racial politics. BLK_ST 218-0 and ASIAN_AM 218-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Civil Rights and Black Liberation
courses.description: The Northern and Southern Civil Rights Movements and the rise of Black nationalism and feminism, 1945-72.
distroTags: U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: African-American Culture
courses.description: Survey of African American culture from slavery to the present. Relation of African American culture to African and Euro-American cultures, the Black Atlantic as a unit of analysis, representations of Blackness in the public imagination.

courses.title: Introduction to Black Studies
courses.description: Introduction to the discipline of Black Studies using key historical and theoretical texts.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: The Black Diaspora and Transnationality
courses.description: Examination of events, movements, theories, and texts that have shaped development of the African diaspora. Topics include slavery, abolitionism, pan-Africanism, the culture-politics nexus, hip-hop, AIDS, and linkages among gender, sexuality, and diasporic sensibilities.

courses.title: Black Life. Trans Life.
courses.description: This course will introduce students to the parameters and textures of Black life, Trans life, and Black Trans life. Popular discourse has either depicted Black Trans people as glamorous superstars or always and already predisposed to death. This course, then, seeks to usefully complicate these narratives and focus on Black and Trans *life*.
distroTags: U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Race, Class and Gender
courses.description: Introduction to scholarship and key theories that treat race, class, and gender as intersecting social constructs. Race, class, and gender in work, family and reproduction, education, poverty, sexuality, and consumer culture. How race, class, and gender inform identity, ideology, and politics to incite social change.

courses.title: Introduction to Critical Mixed Race Studies
courses.description: Exploration of demographic trends in interracial and interethnic marriages to highlight the complexity of the American experience. Special attention to mixed-race experiences portrayed in film and novels. BLK_ST 251-0 and ASIAN_AM 251-0 are taught together; students may not receive credit for both courses.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Introduction to African-American Drama
courses.description: Thematic and historical survey of African American drama. Sociopolitical context, the aesthetic reflected in the work, impact on African American and general theater audiences.

courses.title: Queer Literatures in the African Diaspora
courses.description: Advanced introduction to critical theories of race, gender, and sexuality in the African diaspora from the 19th century to today.

courses.title: Introduction to Black Religions: The North American Experience
courses.description: Introduces students to the variety of Black religions that developed during and after the Atlantic slave trade up to the present. Explores these traditions as continuities/changes of West African religious cosmologies. Examines the interplay between religion, politics, and the constructions of racial identities within various forms of Christianity, Islam, and other expressive cultures. RELIGION 262-0 and BLK_ST 262-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Africans and African Americans: Cultural Entanglements
courses.description: Students will explore how the afterlives of colonialism and slavery have shaped the contemporary relationships between Africans and African Americans. Students will explore how writers, musicians, performers, and scholars excavate the ongoing intimacies between the continent and the African diaspora, in a post-Civil Rights U.S. and in contemporary Africa.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Contemporary Asian Black Relations
courses.description: Divides between these groups, as well as areas of positive cross-cultural collaboration. Historical analysis of reparations, the 1992 Los Angeles riots, and affirmative action. Cross-racial exchange in youth expressions, popular culture, hip-hop. BLK_ST 310-0 and ASIAN_AM 310-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.

courses.title: Religion in the Black Atlantic
courses.description: Afro-Atlantic religions since the 1400s; traditions of Orisa devotion and monotheisms; religion and revolution in African slave religion; racialization and empire; theories of religion, materialities, and diaspora.

courses.title: Black Political Thought
courses.description: Black protest, Black radical politics and Black-led demonstrations are nothing new. How should we understand the motivations and ideas involved in Black radical politics? This course seeks to introduce students to the historical and political underpinnings of issues and questions raised by what Cedric Robinson famously referred to as the "Black Radical Tradition."
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Social Meaning of Race
courses.description: Race as a social concept and recurrent cause of differentiation in multiracial societies. Impact of race on social, cultural, economic, and political institutions. Discussion of prejudice, racism, and discrimination.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Education for Black Liberation
courses.description: This class considers what it means to conceptualize, articulate, and actualize a liberatory Black educational project within U.S. public schools structured by anti-Blackness. The course treats historical and contemporary manifestations of anti-Blackness in schools, as well as the ways Black students, educators, administrators, community and family members, and scholars have worked towards liberation.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Politics of Black Popular Culture
courses.description: Examination of the debates within Black communities about the proper role and function of Black art and artists in relation to Black politics.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Black Women in the 20th Century
courses.description: Experiences and leadership of African American women in major events in recent history, including anti-lynching, women's suffrage, civil rights movements, and World War II.

courses.title: The African American Novel
courses.description: Readings in classic Black American fiction. The author as creator and participant. Works of Wright, Ellison, Baldwin, and others.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: sophomore standing.

courses.title: Gender and Black Masculinity
courses.description: Perceptions and constructions of Black masculinity within African American and "American" cultures in the United States; readings in gender and sexuality studies, feminist theory, African American studies, and cultural studies.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Race and Literature in 19th Century America
courses.description: Examination of the evolution and persistence of the notion of "race" in 19th century America, with attention to the origins of the idea of race in the West. Focus on the multiracial character of 19th century America.

courses.title: Unsettling Whiteness
courses.description: Making the historical, political, and cultural formation of whiteness in Western modernity visible and narratable for commentary and analysis. Particular reference to contemporary culture.

courses.title: Comparative Slavery
courses.description: Traces slavery across historical epochs and geographic contexts, with an emphasis on Latin America, the Caribbean, and the United States.

courses.title: Afro-Latin America
courses.description: Exploration of Afro-Latin communities, cultures, and identities throughout Latin America and the Hispanic diaspora after 1800. Emergence of race and nation in modern Latin America, migration, gender, Afro-Latin spiritual systems and religion, family, and politics.

courses.title: Theorizing Blackness
courses.description: Advanced introduction to critical theories of race and racialization. Investigation of Blackness as a category of critical analysis for analyzing Afro-diasporic formations. Consideration of how Blackness is shaped by gender, class, sexuality, and nationality.
distroTags: U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Diaspora Studies
courses.description: Interdisciplinary examination of the significance of diasporas, their histories, and common dynamics, illustrated with examples drawn from a wide range of cases.

courses.title: Major Authors
courses.description: In-depth examination of a selected author's body of work. Choice of author varies. May be repeated for credit with change of author.
distroTags: U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Racism in Western Modernity
courses.description: Impact of racism in the formation of Western modernity. Critical conceptual and historical analyses of the social formation of "race" and the historical implications of racism in the contemporary West.

courses.title: Black Chicago
courses.description: Surveys the social, cultural, and political history of African Americans in Chicago, including the Great Migration, the Black political machine, Black Chicago music, racial segregation, internal class stratification, and the role of Black churches.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Political Blackness Across the Americas
courses.description: Development of critical approaches to African American studies from the perspectives of postcolonial analysis. In particular, examination of the meaning of the colonial in the formation of African American experiences and the significance of modernity, race, and Black politics in the historical contexts of the United States, Latin America, and the Caribbean.

courses.title: Harlem Renaissance
courses.description: African American political and social movements and cultural production in theater, music, visual arts, and literature from 1915 to 1930.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: BLK_ST 210-0 or another African American literature course.

prereqSegments.value: or another African American literature course.

courses.title: Black Women Writers
courses.description: Intensive, multi-genre examination of the contribution of Black women to African American, women's, and American literature, with consideration of the factors and figures that have influenced the reception of Black women's writings across time.

courses.title: Topics in African-American Studies
courses.description: Advanced work on social, cultural, or historical topics. May be repeated for credit with different topic.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: advanced student or senior standing.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: advanced student or senior standing.

courses.title: Topics in Transnational Black Studies
courses.description: Examination of texts such as novels, poetry, film, drama, slave narratives, political manifestos, and historical texts in order to compare how people from across the African diaspora have approached issues of identity, culture, and community.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: advanced student or senior standing.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: advanced student or senior standing.

courses.title: Research Seminar
courses.description: Methods of researching the Black experience. Identification of research problems; location, selection, and critique of relevant literature; data gathering and analysis; report writing. Topics vary.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: advanced student or senior standing. .

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: advanced student or senior standing. .

courses.title: Internship in Black Studies
courses.description: Analysis of social and cultural institutions through field study and participant observation. Entails a final submission overseen by a Northwestern faculty member.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: advanced student or senior standing.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: advanced student or senior standing.

courses.title: Independent Study
courses.description: Open to advanced students with consent of instructor.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: advanced student or senior standing.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: advanced student or senior standing.

courses.title: Introduction to Biomedical Engineering
courses.description: Information to 1) help students determine if BME is the right major for them and 2) learn how to make the most of their undergraduate experience. The field of biomedical engineering, career and research opportunities, ethics.

courses.title: BME Lab: Experimental Design
courses.description: A laboratory course focusing on quantitative physiological measurements and analyses, instrument characterization, statistical design of experiments, and training in preparation and organization of laboratory notes and reports.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: BMD_ENG 220-0 or IEMS 303-0 or MECH_ENG 359-0.

courses.title: Introduction to Biomedical Statistics
courses.description: Basic statistical concepts presented with emphasis on their relevance to biological and medical investigations.

courses.title: Thermodynamics
courses.description: Physical and chemical principles as applied to biological systems and medical devices. Topics include material balances, thermodynamics, solution chemistry, electrochemistry, surface chemistry, transport, and kinetics.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: MATH 228-1; CHEM 132-0, CHEM 152-0, or CHEM 172-0.

courses.title: Fluid Mechanics
courses.description: Fundamentals of fluid mechanics and their applications to biological systems.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: BMD_ENG 250 or BMD_ENG 271; GEN_ENG 205-4; MATH 228-2.

courses.title: Introduction to Biomechanics
courses.description: Analysis of stresses and deformations in solids. Problems in biomechanics, with emphasis on assumptions appropriate to modeling biological materials including bone, skin, muscle, and cell membranes.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: GEN_ENG 205-2.

courses.title: Biomedical Signals and Circuits
courses.description: Time and frequency domain analysis: convolution representation, Fourier series, Fourier transforms, frequency response, filtering, sampling.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PHYSICS 135-2 or consent of instructor; BMD_ENG 207-0 (can be taken concurrently).

courses.title: Biomedical Systems Analysis
courses.description: Introduction to linear systems analysis. Time and frequency domain techniques for analyzing linear systems, emphasizing their applications to biomedical systems. Python-based problem sets and a summative instrumentation and analysis lab project illustrate topics covered in class.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: BMD_ENG 207-0; BMD_ENG 308-0; BMD_ENG 220-0; GEN_ENG 205-4.

courses.title: Transport Fundamentals
courses.description: Fundamental and biomedical applications of diffusive and convective heat and mass transfer.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: BMD_ENG 270-0; MATH 228-1; BMD_ENG 377-0 recommended.

courses.title: Biomedical Engineering Design
courses.description: Open-ended team-designed projects in the medical devices arena. Systems approach requiring design strategy and concepts, including reliability, safety, ethics, economic analysis, marketing, FDA regulations, and patents. Written and oral reports.

courses.title: Biomedical Engineering Design
courses.description: Development of a design project initiated during the previous quarter.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: BMD_ENG 390-1.

courses.title: Topics in Biomedical Engineering
courses.description: Special Topics in Biomedical Engineering.

courses.title: Special Topics in Biomedical Engineering
courses.description: Special Topics in Biomedical Engineering, Laboratory emphasis.

courses.title: Projects
courses.description: SEE DEPT FOR SECTION AND PERMISSION NUMBERS.

courses.title: Accounting
courses.description: Introduction to both financial and managerial accounting. Use of organizations' financial statements for making decisions.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: ECON 201-0 and ECON 202-0.

courses.title: Marketing Management
courses.description: Basic principles and applications of marketing management. Marketing segmentation, target marketing, brand positioning, consumer behavior, channels strategy, pricing, advertising and promotion.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: ECON 201-0 and ECON 202-0.

courses.title: Leadership in Organizations
courses.description: Social science tools for solving organizational problems and influencing individuals, groups, and organizations. Competitive decision making, reward system design, team building, strategic negotiation, political dynamics, corporate culture, and strategic organizational design.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: ECON 201-0 and ECON 202-0.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Corporate Finance
courses.description: Effects of time and uncertainty on valuation and decision making. Discounting techniques, stock and bond valuation, capital budgeting, firm valuation, capital asset pricing model, financial options. May not receive credit for both this course and ECON 360-1. Not for students who have previously taken KELLG_FE 310-0.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: ECON 201-0 and ECON 202-0; STAT 210-0 or equivalent; MATH 218-1 or MATH 220-1; and BUS_INST 301-0.

courses.title: Business and Economic Institutions in Historical Perspective
courses.description: Factors affecting economic growth and challenges to achieving economic success. Organization of firms and financial markets; corporate governance; innovation; financial crises; income inequality; race and gender.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: ECON 201-0 and ECON 202-0; STAT 210-0 or equivalent; and MATH 218-1 or MATH 220-1.

courses.title: Real Estate Finance & Investment
courses.description: An introduction to the fundamental concepts, principles, analytical methods and tools used for making investing and financing decisions regarding income producing properties. Topics covered include leases, cash flow-based real estate evaluation, property financing (debt), real estate private equity, and property taxation.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: ECON 201-0, ECON 202-0, STAT 210-0 or equivalent, BUS_INST 304-0 or equivalent.

courses.title: Professional Linkage Seminar
courses.description: Content varies. Possible topics include business history, financial markets, investing, leadership communication, nonprofit management, and sports business. Prerequisites vary.

courses.title: Analysis of Field Experience
courses.description: Asynchronous online course for students who intern 10-40 hours a week. Requires students to read, reflect upon, and discuss the structure and purpose of organizations as well as various workplace topics. Application and acceptance to CFS required. May be subject to internship course-credit limits; students should consult their academic adviser before applying.

courses.title: Field Studies in the Environment & Sustainability
courses.description: Explores theoretical, geographic, and historical stakes of sustainability approaches and environmental professions. Students intern 10-40 hours a week. Application and acceptance to CFS required. May be subject to internship course-credit limits; students should consult their academic adviser before applying.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Field Studies in Business Culture
courses.description: Online course for students who intern 10-40 hours a week in organizations throughout the world. Focuses on critical and historical issues shaping business culture. Application and acceptance to CFS required. May be subject to internship course-credit limits; students should consult their academic adviser before applying.

courses.title: Field Studies in Social Justice
courses.description: Theories of justice and approaches to social change. Students intern 10-40 hours a week in advocacy, policy, or related organizations. Application and acceptance to CFS required. May be subject to internship course-credit limits; students should consult their academic adviser before applying.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Field Studies in Public Health
courses.description: Explores foundational concepts and current issues in Public Health. Students earn 10-40 hours a week in health- and medicine-related organizations. Application and acceptance to CFS required. May be subject to internship course-credit limits; students should consult their academic adviser before applying.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Field Studies in the Modern Workplace
courses.description: Culture, theory, and history of modern workplaces and labor issues. Students intern 10-40 hours a week in various fields. Application and acceptance to CFS required. May be subject to internship course-credit limits; students should consult their academic adviser before applying. Credits available may vary by quarter.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Legal Field Studies
courses.description: Contemporary issues and workplace culture in the legal field. Students intern 10-40 hours a week in legal organizations. Application and acceptance to CFS required. May be subject to internship course-credit limits; students should consult their academic adviser before applying. Credits available may vary by quarter.

courses.title: Business Field Studies
courses.description: Contemporary issues in business professions. Students intern 10-40 hours a week in business (primarily finance, consulting, or marketing). Application and acceptance to CFS required. May be subject to internship course-credit limits; students should consult their academic adviser before applying. Credits available may vary by quarter.

courses.title: Field Studies in Civic Engagement
courses.description: Forms of civic engagement with emphasis on local issues and approaches. Students intern 10-40 hours a week in civic, educational, legal, governmental, nonprofit, or community-based organizations. Application and acceptance to CFS required. May be subject to internship course-credit limits; students should consult their academic adviser before applying.

courses.title: Field Studies in Humanities
courses.description: Labor issues through arts and cultural history, with emphasis on humanities, arts, and media in the public sphere. Students intern 10-40 hours a week in fields including but not limited to arts and cultural organizations. Application and acceptance to CFS required. May be subject to internship course-credit limits; students should consult their academic adviser before applying.

courses.title: Introduction to Calculus and Chemistry
courses.description: For participants in Bio&ChemEXCEL summer program. Introduction to calculus and general chemistry. Taken with BIOL_SCI 100-0.

courses.title: Introduction to Problem Solving in Chemistry
courses.description: For participants in the Warren Summer Bridge program. Developing facility with quantitative tools to solve problems in chemistry.

courses.title: College Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: First-Year Writing Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: Quantitative Problem Solving in Chemistry
courses.description: Solution strategies for traditional word problems and their application to basic chemistry quantitative problems: dimensional analysis, chemical equations, stoichiometry, limiting reagents.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: permission of department. Students cannot recieve credit for CHEM 110-0, CHEM 101-0, and CHEM 1X0.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: permission of department. Students cannot recieve credit for

courses.title: Mentored Study Program
courses.description: Study sessions facilitated by undergraduate peer leaders, for students enrolled in CHEM 110-0. Meets weekly in small groups to collaboratively review material, solve practice problems, clarify concepts, and enhance study strategies. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U.

courses.title: Peer-Guided Study Group: Quantitative Problem Solving in Chemistry
courses.description: Peer-guided study group for students enrolled in CHEM 110-0. Meets weekly in small groups, along with a peer facilitator, to collaboratively review material, work through practice problems, and clarify course concepts. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Chemistry I
courses.description: Quantum mechanics, electronic structure, periodic properties of elements, chemical bonding, thermodynamics, intermolecular forces, properties of solids and liquids, solutions and colligative properties.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: CHEM 110-0 (C- or better).

courses.title: Mentored Study Program
courses.description: Study sessions facilitated by undergraduate peer leaders, for students enrolled in CHEM 131-0. Meets weekly in small groups to collaboratively review material, solve practice problems, clarify concepts, and enhance study strategies. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U.

courses.title: Peer-Guided Study Group: Fundamentals of Chemistry I
courses.description: Peer-guided study group for students enrolled in CHEM 131-0. Meets weekly in small groups, along with a peer facilitator, to collaboratively review material, work through practice problems, and clarify course concepts. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Chemistry II
courses.description: Chemical equilibrium, aqueous solution equilibria, chemical kinetics, metals in chemistry and biology, oxidation-reduction reactions and electrochemistry.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM 131-0, CHEM 141-0 (C- or better).

courses.title: Mentored Study Program
courses.description: Study sessions facilitated by undergraduate peer leaders, for students enrolled in CHEM 132-0. Meets weekly in small groups to collaboratively review material, solve practice problems, clarify concepts, and enhance study strategies. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U.

courses.title: Peer-Guided Study Group: Fundamentals of Chemistry II
courses.description: Peer-guided study group for students enrolled in CHEM 132-0. Meets weekly in small groups, along with a peer facilitator, to collaboratively review material, work through practice problems, and clarify course concepts. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Chemistry Laboratory I
courses.description: Chemical analysis of real samples using basic laboratory techniques including titration, colorimetric analysis, density measurements, and atomic spectroscopy. Planning, data collection, interpretation, and reporting on experiments. Must be taken concurrently with CHEM 131-0.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: CHEM 110-0 (C- or better).

courses.title: Fundamentals of Chemistry Laboratory II
courses.description: SUMMER OFFERING - Lab fee. Must be taken concurrently with Chem 132-0.

courses.title: General Chemistry I
courses.description: Quantum mechanics, electronic structure, periodic properties of elements, chemical bonding, thermodynamics, gas laws, intermolecular forces, properties of solids and liquids, solutions and colligative properties.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: permission of department by placement exam.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: permission of department by placement exam.

courses.title: Peer-Guided Study Group: General Chemistry I
courses.description: Peer-guided study group for students enrolled in CHEM 151-0. Meets weekly in small groups, along with a peer facilitator, to collaboratively review material, work through practice problems, and clarify course concepts. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U.

courses.title: General Chemistry II
courses.description: Chemical equilibrium, aqueous solution equilibria, chemical kinetics, metals in chemistry and biology, oxidation-reduction reactions and electrochemistry.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM 151-0, CHEM 161-0 (C- or better).

courses.title: Peer-Guided Study Group: General Chemistry II
courses.description: Peer-guided study group for students enrolled in CHEM 152-0. Meets weekly in small groups, along with a peer facilitator, to collaboratively review material, work through practice problems, and clarify course concepts. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U.

courses.title: General Chemistry Laboratory I
courses.description: Chemical analysis of real samples using basic laboratory techniques including titration, colorimetric analysis, density measurements, and atomic spectroscopy. Planning, data collection, interpretation, and reporting on experiments. Must be taken concurrently with CHEM 151-0.

courses.title: General Chemistry Laboratory II
courses.description: Chemistry laboratory techniques applied to materials science and nanotechnology, acid-base chemistry, and chemical kinetics. Planning, data collection, interpretation, and reporting on experiments. Must be taken concurrently with CHEM 152-0.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM 151-0, CHEM 161-0 (C- or better).

courses.title: Advanced General Inorganic Chemistry
courses.description: Review of mole problems and stoichiometry; descriptive chemistry, elements, compounds, and inorganic reactions; gas laws; phase equilibria and colligative properties; chemical equilibrium; aqueous equilibria; topics in chemical bonding and molecular structure. Must be taken concurrently with CHEM 181-0.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: department placement exam.

courses.title: Advanced General Physical Chemistry
courses.description: Thermodynamics and equilibrium; chemical kinetics and mechanism; electrochemistry; electronic structure of the atom and quantum theory; advanced topics in chemical bonding; coordination compounds; solid-state chemistry; nuclear chemistry. Must be taken concurrently with CHEM 182-0.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM 171-0, CHEM 181-0 (C- or better); MATH 220-1.

courses.title: Advanced General Inorganic Chemistry Laboratory
courses.description: Laboratory techniques for studying chemical analysis and chemical reactions relevant to environmental or materials research. Planning, data collection, interpretation, and reporting on experiments. Must be taken concurrently with CHEM 171-0.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: department placement exam.

courses.title: Advanced General Physical Chemistry Laboratory
courses.description: Study of physical chemistry (acid base chemistry, thermodynamics, etc.) in the laboratory. Planning, data collection, interpretation, and reporting on these experiments. Must be taken concurrently with CHEM 172-0.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: CHEM 171-0, CHEM 181-0 (C- or better).

courses.title: Chemistry of Nature and Culture
courses.description: Chemistry for the nonscientist. Chemicals commonly encountered in everyday life.

courses.title: Organic Chemistry I
courses.description: Foundational concepts in organic chemistry will be introduced. Topics include structure and properties of common functional groups, acidity/basicity, conformational analysis, stereochemistry, and reactivity of organic compounds.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM 132-0 and CHEM 142-0, or CHEM 152-0 and CHEM 162-0, or CHEM 172-0 and CHEM 182-0 (C- or better); or qualifying score on the Chemistry Placement Exam. Must be taken concurrently with CHEM 235-1.

prereqSegments.value: (C- or better); or qualifying score on the Chemistry Placement Exam. Must be taken concurrently with

courses.title: Organic Chemistry II
courses.description: Fundamental concepts in organic chemistry will be covered. The topics will include important functional groups and will include: nomenclature, structure, properties, and multistep synthesis. Reaction mechanisms for organic transformations will be presented, and synthesis strategies will be covered.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Grade of C- or better in CHEM 215-1 and CHEM 235-1. Must be taken concurrently with CHEM 235-2.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: Grade of C- or better in

courses.title: Organic Chemistry III
courses.description: Advanced concepts in modern organic chemistry will be introduced. The material will focus on recent developments in synthetic organic chemistry, including: concerted/pericyclic reactions, catalysis, green/environmental chemistry, automated synthesis, and combinatorial/screening methods.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Grade of C- or better in CHEM 215-2 and in CHEM 235-2. Must be taken concurrently with CHEM 235-3.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: Grade of C- or better in

courses.description: Study sessions facilitated by undergraduate peer leaders, for students enrolled in CHEM 215-2. Meets weekly in small groups to collaboratively review material, solve practice problems, clarify concepts, and enhance study strategies. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U.

courses.title: Peer-Guided Study Group: Organic Chemistry I
courses.description: Peer-guided study group for students enrolled in CHEM 215-1. Meets weekly in small groups, along with a peer facilitator, to collaboratively review material, work through practice problems, and clarify course concepts. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U.

courses.title: Peer-Guided Study Group: Organic Chemistry II
courses.description: Peer-guided study group for students enrolled in CHEM 215-2. Meets weekly in small groups, along with a peer facilitator, to collaboratively review material, work through practice problems, and clarify course concepts. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U.

courses.title: Peer-Guided Study Group: Organic Chemistry III
courses.description: Peer-guided study group for students enrolled in CHEM 215-3. Meets weekly in small groups, along with a peer facilitator, to collaboratively review material, work through practice problems, and clarify course concepts. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U.

courses.title: Accelerated Organic Chemistry I
courses.description: Primarily for chemistry majors and students in ISP. Basic concepts of structure, stereochemistry, and reactivity of organic compounds. The chemistry of hydrocarbons and alcohols. No P/N registration.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM 132-0 and CHEM 142-0, or CHEM 152-0 and CHEM 162-0, or CHEM 172-0 and CHEM 182-0 (C- or better), or department placement. Must be taken concurrently with CHEM 237-1. Students may not receive credit for both CHEM 217-1 and 212-1.

prereqSegments.value: (C- or better), or department placement. Must be taken concurrently with
. Students may not receive credit for both

courses.title: Accelerated Organic Chemistry II
courses.description: Primarily for chemistry majors and students in ISP. The chemistry of aromatic, carbonyl, and nitrogen compounds; characterization of organic substances by chemical and spectral methods; reaction mechanisms. No P/N registration.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM 217-1 and CHEM 237-1 (C- or better). Must be taken concurrently with CHEM 237-2. Students may not receive credit for both CHEM 217-2 and 212-2.

prereqSegments.value: (C- or better). Must be taken concurrently with
. Students may not receive credit for both

courses.title: Accelerated Organic Chemistry III
courses.description: Primarily for chemistry majors and students in ISP. The chemistry of poly-functional compounds of biological and medicinal interest. Modern organic syn-thesis, bioorganic chemistry, and recent developments in organic chemistry. No P/N registration.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM 217-2 and CHEM 237-2 (C- or better). Must be taken concurrently with CHEM 235-3. Students may not receive credit for both CHEM 217-3 and 212-3.

prereqSegments.value: (C- or better). Must be taken concurrently with
. Students may not receive credit for both

courses.title: Introductory Instrumental Analysis
courses.description: Introduction to basic laboratory techniques in analytical chemistry and spectroscopy. Topics include infrared and UV-visible spectroscopy, gas and liquid chromatography, elemental and thermal analysis, simple x-ray diffraction, error analysis, and literature searching techniques.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM 132-0 and CHEM 142-0, or CHEM 152-0 and CHEM 162-0, or CHEM 172-0 and CHEM 182-0 (C- or better), or equivalent.

courses.title: Organic Chemistry Lab I
courses.description: Standard laboratory techniques in organic chemistry will be covered. Techniques will focus on the isolation and purification of organic compounds as well as the use of spectroscopic methods to determine identity and purity.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: CHEM 132-0 and CHEM 142-0, or CHEM 152-0 and CHEM 162-0, or CHEM 172-0 and CHEM 182-0 (C- or better); or qualifying score on the Chemistry Placement Exam. Must be taken concurrently with CHEM 215-1.

prereqSegments.value: (C- or better); or qualifying score on the Chemistry Placement Exam. Must be taken concurrently with

courses.title: Organic Chemistry Lab II
courses.description: Complete laboratory experiments focusing on standard synthetic organic chemistry will be conducted each week. Students will complete a prelab worksheet including stoichiometric calculations, prediction of reaction outcome, and identification of safety protocols.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Grade of C- or better in CHEM 215-1 and CHEM 235-1. Must be taken concurrently with CHEM 215-2.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: Grade of C- or better in

courses.title: Organic Chemistry Lab III
courses.description: Current laboratory practices for organic synthesis will be introduced. Reactions will include mechanistically complex multi-step process for the preparation of compounds related to topical themes from academic research and industrial chemistry. Synthetic targets will include complex small molecules, polymers, and molecules of biological relevance.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Grade of C- or better in CHEM 215-2 and in CHEM 235-2. Must be taken concurrently with CHEM 215-3 or CHEM 217-3.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: Grade of C- or better in

courses.title: Accelerated Organic Chemistry Laboratory I
courses.description: Primarily for chemistry majors and students in ISP. Molecular modeling, unknown identification by spectroscopic methods, and experimental techniques of modern chemistry emphasizing reactions of alkanes, alkenes, alkyl halides, alcohols, and carbonyls.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM 132-0 and CHEM 142-0, or CHEM 152-0 and CHEM 162-0, or CHEM 172-0 and CHEM 182-0 (C- or better), or equivalent. Must be taken concurrently with CHEM 217-1. Students may not receive credit for both CHEM 237-1 and 232-1.

prereqSegments.value: (C- or better), or equivalent. Must be taken concurrently with
. Students may not receive credit for both

courses.title: Accelerated Organic Chemistry Laboratory II
courses.description: Primarily for chemistry majors and students in ISP. Techniques of modern organic chemistry including NMR spectroscopy and reactions such as electrophilic aromatic substitution, esterification, Grignard reaction, aldol condensation, Robinson annulation, and Diels-Alder reaction.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM 217-1 and CHEM 237-1 (C- or better). Must be taken concurrently with CHEM 217-2. Students may not receive credit for both CHEM 237-2 and 232-2.

prereqSegments.value: (C- or better). Must be taken concurrently with
. Students may not receive credit for both

courses.title: Principles of Inorganic Chemistry
courses.description: Topics in advanced inorganic chemistry. CHEM 302-0 and CHEM 402-0 are taught together.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: CHEM 333-0 or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Principles of Physical Chemistry
courses.description: An overview of advanced topics in physical chemistry. CHEM 303-0 and CHEM 403-0 are taught together.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM 342-1 and CHEM 342-2 and CHEM 342-3.

courses.title: Chemistry of Life Processes
courses.description: Topics in the chemistry and biochemistry of life processes. Taught with CHEM 405-0.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM 215-3 or CHEM 212-3 or 217-3 (C- or better); and 1 biochemistry course; or consent of instructor.

prereqSegments.value: (C- or better); and 1 biochemistry course; or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Environmental Chemistry
courses.description: Topics in the physical chemistry of the environment. Taught with CHEM 406-0.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM 215-2 or CHEM 212-3 or 217-3 (C- or better); MATH 230-2; PHYSICS 135-1 and PHYSICS 135-2; or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Supramolecular Design of Materials and Nanostructures
courses.description: Introduction to frontier research at the interface of chemistry and materials science. CHEM 307-0 and CHEM 407-0 are taught together.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM 215-3 or CHEM 212-3 or 217-3 (C- or better).

courses.title: Design, Synthesis, and Applications of Nanomaterials
courses.description: Fabrication, chemical synthesis, assembly, and characterization of controlled-dimensionality materials, including metals, semiconductors, oxides, polymers, and mesoporous scaffolds. Interfacial phenomena and particle stability, nano forms of carbon, and material design. Taught with CHEM 408-0.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: 1 quarter of physical chemistry or consent of instructor.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: 1 quarter of physical chemistry or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Polymer Chemistry
courses.description: This course will cover the design and synthesis of polymers, including reaction mechanisms, characterization, and structure-property relationships. CHEM 309-0 is taught with CHEM 409-0. Prerequisites (for undergraduates only): CHEM 215-3 or CHEM 212-3 or 217-3 (C- or better); and one of the following courses: CHEM 307-0, CHEM 313-0, CHEM 319-0, CHEM 412-0, or CHEM 415-0.

courses.title: Physical Organic Chemistry I
courses.description: An introduction to the concepts and methods of physical organic chemistry, including: molecular orbital theory, orbital symmetry and reactivity, conformational analysis and stereochemistry, stereoelectronic effects, intermolecular forces and solvation, transition state theory, free energy relationships and kinetic isotope effects, and reactive intermediates.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: Students must have completed CHEM 215-3 or CHEM 217-3 or equivalent to enroll.

courses.title: Physical Organic Chemistry II
courses.description: Expansion on the concepts and practices of physical organic chemistry including: orbital symmetry and reactivity, energy surfaces and Marcus theory, single-electron transfer reactions, solvent effects, nucleophilic and electrophilic reactivity, photochemistry and photocatalysis, organic electronic materials.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM 215-3 or CHEM 217-3 or equivalent. CHEM 310-1 strongly recommended.

courses.title: Advanced Organic Chemistry 1. Advanced concepts of organic reactivity and selectivity in synthesis.
courses.description: Strategies and tactics involved in complex target synthesis. Modern reaction classes as applied to chemical synthesis, coupled to in-depth discussion of the underlying key principles of synthesis design and execution, are covered in the class. Students will gain experience in problem solving, creative thinking, structural analysis and presentation skills.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM 215-3 or CHEM 212-3 or 217-3 (C- or better).

courses.title: Principles of Chemical Biology
courses.description: Introduction to using chemical principles in biology and medicine. Experimental techniques and experiments in chemical biology. Suitable for students in chemistry, engineering, and biology. Taught with CHEM 415-0.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM 215-3 or CHEM 212-3 or 217-3 (C- or better); and 1 quarter of biology; or consent of instructor.

prereqSegments.value: (C- or better); and 1 quarter of biology; or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Medicinal Chemistry: the Organic Chemistry of Drug Design and Action
courses.description: Introduction to principles of drug design and mechanisms of drug action from a chemical viewpoint. Historical introduction, drug design and development, receptors, enzymes and enzyme inhibitors, DNA, drug metabolism, and prodrugs.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM 215-3 or CHEM 212-3 (C- or better); or consent of instructor.

prereqSegments.value: or CHEM 212-3 (C- or better); or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Advanced Organic Synthesis - Concepts and Applications
courses.description: Synthesis of natural products and other medicinally relevant organic compounds. Retrosynthetic analysis, substructure keying, and pattern recognition. Classic and modern organic reactions. Terpenes, alkaloids, polyketides, steroids, proteins, and pharmaceuticals.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM 215-3 or CHEM 212-3 or 217-3 (C- or better).

courses.title: Inorganic Chemistry
courses.description: Descriptive chemistry of some important elements. Current concepts and models of chemical bonding.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: 2 200-or 300-level chemistry courses.

courses.title: Thermodynamics
courses.description: Laws of applications of thermodynamics. Thermochemistry, chemical potentials, solution thermodynamics, nonideal gases.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM 132-0 or CHEM 152-0 or CHEM 172-0 or CHEM 215-3 or CHEM 212-3 or CHEM 217-3 (C- or better); and MATH 230-1.

courses.title: Quantum Mechanics and Spectroscopy
courses.description: Quantum mechanics with emphasis on atomic and molecular electronic structure. Electronic, vibrational, rotational, and magnetic resonance spectroscopy.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: MATH 230-2; PHYSICS 135-1 and 135-2; and CHEM 342-1.

courses.title: Kinetics and Statistical Thermodynamics
courses.description: Chemical kinetics, including experimental techniques and theories of rate processes. Statistical mechanics, including Boltzmann distribution, partition functions, and applications to thermodynamics.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM 342-1 and CHEM 342-2 (C- or better).

courses.title: Physical Chemistry for ISP
courses.description: Gas laws and properties; kinetic theory; first, second, and third laws; phase equilibria; mixtures, phase diagrams, statistical thermodynamics, kinetics.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: ISP enrollment; CHEM 172-0 and CHEM 182-0 (C- or better); MATH 281-1, MATH 281-2, MATH 281-3; or consent of department.

courses.title: Advanced Laboratory 1
courses.description: Advanced laboratory techniques in synthetic and analytical chemistry and spectroscopy: mass spectrometry, chromatography, NMR spectroscopy, and organic synthesis techniques.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM 220-0; and CHEM 215-3 or CHEM 212-3, and CHEM 235-3 (C- or better); or equivalent.

courses.title: Advanced Laboratory 2
courses.description: Advanced laboratory techniques in synthetic and analytical chemistry and spectroscopy, polymer characterization methods, electrochemistry, x-ray crystallography, atomic spectroscopy, and inorganic synthesis techniques.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM 333-0 and CHEM 350-1 (C- or better) or equivalent.

courses.title: Advanced Laboratory 3
courses.description: Advanced laboratory techniques in synthetic and analytical chemistry and spectroscopy: infrared and Raman spectroscopy, electronic spectroscopy, fast kinetics, organic and inorganic synthesis techniques in a self-guided project.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM 342-2 or equivalent and CHEM 350-2 (C- or better).

courses.title: Green Chemistry
courses.description: Practices of environmentally benign chemistry as applied to the chemical industry. Introduction to the concept and discipline of green chemistry; growth and expansion of the discipline in historical context from its origins in the early 1990s to the present.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM 215-2 or CHEM 217-2 (C- or better).

courses.title: Undergraduate Seminar
courses.description: Advanced work for superior students through supervised reading, research, and discussion.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: consent of department.

courses.title: Independent Study
courses.description: Faculty-directed research. Must be taken P/N for first 2 quarters.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: consent of department.

courses.title: Getting to Know Chemical Engineering
courses.description: This survey course is an overview of the discipline of chemical engineering, and its undergraduate program at Northwestern. It is primarily targeted to first-years who are considering the major. We will discuss the most recent areas of research, career paths after graduation, and engineering ethics. Most course sessions will involve external speakers.

courses.title: Engineering of Chemical and Biological Processes
courses.description: Survey of engineering principles as they are applied to processes involving chemical and biological transformations. Examples from the chemical, pharmaceutical, biotechnology, food processing, electronics, and other industries. Impact of economics, ethics, and other nontechnical constraints.

courses.title: Analysis of Chemical Process Systems
courses.description: Introduction to process systems. Material balances and stoichiometry. Analysis of process system flow sheets. Introduction to departmental computing facilities. Basic numerical analysis.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM 132-0, CHEM 152-0, or CHEM 172-0; GEN_ENG 205-3 (may be taken concurrently).

courses.title: Thermodynamics
courses.description: The first and second laws of thermodynamics. Entropy and equilibrium. Material and energy balances. Equations of state and properties of fluids. Solutions, phase equilibria, and chemical reactions.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: CHEM_ENG 210-0.

courses.title: Phase Equilibrium and Staged Separations
courses.description: Thermodynamic models of mixtures and phase equilibrium. Analysis and design of staged separation processes such as distillation, absorption, stripping, and extraction.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CHEM_ENG 210-0, CHEM_ENG 211-0.

courses.title: Molecular & Cell Biology for Engineers
courses.description: Introduction to cell and molecular biology concepts that provide the foundation for modern biotechnology and bioengineering.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: CHEM 132-0, CHEM 152-0, or CHEM 172-0.

courses.title: Projects
courses.description: Supervised investigation of a chemical engineering problem with submission of a final report.

courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: grade of at least C- in CHINESE 121-1 or equivalent.
