courses.title: Sound and Communication Health
courses.description: Introduction to communication sciences and disorders. Role of sound in basic human communication; hearing, speech, language, and learning mechanisms required to process and produce sound; assessment and treatment of disorders caused by a breakdown in sound processing. May not be taken with or after CSD 318-0, CSD 320-0, or CSD 373-0.

courses.title: Introduction to Hearing and Speech Acoustics
courses.description: Introduction to acoustics, measurement of hearing, and the acoustical properties of speech sounds. Sound waves; standards of measuring magnitude; audiograms; source-filter theory; spectrograms.

courses.title: The Scientific Exploration of Communication
courses.description: Introduction to biology and physics of human communication. Basic properties of speech sounds and how they are produced and received; relation between human anatomical structures involved in sound production, modulation, and reception; brain mechanisms of processing speech sounds.

courses.title: Neurobiology of Communication
courses.description: Human anatomy, physiology, and neurology in relation to communicative behavior. Sensory, perceptual, cognitive, and motor processes.

courses.title: Study of Learning and Learning Problems in the Classroom
courses.description: Study of children's learning in classroom settings. Field placement, using informal assessments of social, cognitive, and communication functioning, for children with and without exceptionalities.

courses.title: Seminar in Communication Sciences & Disorders
courses.description: Throughout this course, students will consider what it means to be “bilingual,” and explore how bilingualism shapes human communication, including language development and learning, the production and perception of speech, cognitive function, and neural activity, as well as the perceived benefits and pitfalls of bilingualism from a cognitive and educational perspective.

courses.title: Anatomy and Physiology of the Vocal Mechanism
courses.description: Anatomical and physiological mechanisms of breathing, phonation, and articulation. Laboratories include dissection and participation in physiological research.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: sophomore standing or above.

courses.title: Brain and Cognition
courses.description: Neural bases of cognitive processing with emphases on neuroimaging approaches in the areas of encoding, perception, attention, memory, language, reading, motor control, and executive functioning. Taught with Psych 327-0; students may not earn credit for both courses.

courses.title: Statistics in Communication Sciences and Disorders
courses.description: Introduction to research design and data analysis in communication sciences and disorders; statistical inference.

courses.title: Phonetics
courses.description: Training in transcription of English speech sounds. Introduction to phonological analysis, dynamics of articulation, and dialect variations.

courses.title: Culture, Language and Learning
courses.description: Language and culture; transmission of culture through language; effects of cultural variety on perception, cognition, and learning; implications of cultural and linguistic diversity in communicative disorders.

courses.title: Introduction to Audiology
courses.description: Introduction to the measurement of hearing in humans. Basic anatomy of the ear, measurement of hearing, potential disorders of hearing.

courses.title: Aural Rehabilitation
courses.description: Principles and practices in rehabilitation of children and adults, including use of sensory aids, counseling, communication remediation (emphasizing speech reading), and auditory training techniques.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: CSD 318-0.

courses.title: Introduction to Speech, Language, Learning, and Their Disorders
courses.description: Overview of normal and disordered communication. Speech, language, hearing, and cognitive development disorders and their psychosocial effects, across the age continuum according to etiology, clinical manifestations, and intervention. Anatomy and physiology of speech, language, and hearing. Service-delivery settings; ethical and legal considerations; professional issues.

courses.title: Clinical Assisting in Speech and Language Pathology
courses.description: Introduction to clinical practice, the dynamics of the client-clinician relationship and general clinical protocol, and the development and execution of therapy goals and procedures.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: CSD 392-0 and CSD 305-0, or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Language and Cognition in Atypical Development
courses.description: Description and theory relevant to the cognitive, linguistic, and social development of individuals with different developmental disorders throughout the lifespan.

courses.title: Introduction to Learning Disabilities
courses.description: Psychological, neurological, and linguistic theories of language and learning as related to learning disabilities.

courses.title: Introduction to Clinical Procedures in Learning Disabilities
courses.description: Practicum experience in clinical settings. Learning processes and application of instructional approaches. Field studies, reading, and weekly seminars.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: CSD 376-0.

courses.title: Autism Spectrum Disorder
courses.description: Overview of autism, focusing on its clinical presentation and potential causes, diagnosis, assessments for characterizing autistic features in research, evaluation (based on behavior, cognition, neuroimaging, and genetics) of theories of autism's causes, and controversies (changing prevalence, myths about causation).

courses.title: Language Development and Usage
courses.description: Development of spoken and written language as it relates to child development; includes phonological, morphological, syntactic, semantic, and pragmatic components. Cultural and individual linguistic diversity.

courses.title: Cognitive Neuroscience of Human Communication
courses.description: In-depth study of cognitive neuroscience methods (MRI, EEG, etc.) and what they have revealed about human communication and its disorders. The focus is on reading and critiquing research papers. Students also work as a team to design, execute hands-on, and analyze data from their own EEG experiment.

courses.title: Research Practicum in Communication Sciences and Disorders
courses.description: Working with a faculty member on design, execution, and presentation of a research project. Activities may include a review of literature, design of an experiment, data collection, coding, analysis, and spoken or written presentation of experimental results.

courses.title: Independent Study
courses.description: Prerequisite: consent of undergraduate dean after submission of petition.

courses.title: Introduction to Dance Studies
courses.description: A curricular foundation in critical dance studies: the significance of corporeality/embodiment, critical theories as applied to dance practices, cultural and historical contextual knowledges, movement description and dance analysis.

courses.title: Dance as Creative Collaboration
courses.description: Students are introduced to the different ways dance performance materializes: choreography, improvisation, costume, lighting, production design, historical, political and artistic contexts. It will engage in creative and critical problem solving through choreographic thinking, improvisation, and collaborative embodied practice.

courses.title: Movement for the Stage
courses.description: Movement and body awareness. Improvisational techniques using time, space, weight, and effort as the instrument of expression.

courses.title: Music Theatre Dance I
courses.description: Music theatre styles, explored through the study of jazz, tap, and modern repertoire. Taken during junior year.

courses.title: Basic Theatrical Dance I
courses.description: A foundation dance class for theatre, musical theatre and beginning movement students on theatrical dance: alignment, tempo, rhythm, spatial awareness. This course builds progressively with more complex movement phrases and technical specificity. Geared especially to MTCP and Theatre, Performance Studies and Music student who have 0-3 years’ experience in dance.

courses.title: Basic Theatrical Dance II
courses.description: A foundation dance class for theatre, musical theatre and beginning movement students on theatrical dance: alignment, tempo, rhythm, spatial awareness. This course builds progressively with more complex movement phrases and technical specificity. Geared especially to MTCP and Theatre, Performance Studies and Music student who have 0-3 years’ experience in dance.

courses.title: Basic Theatrical Dance III
courses.description: A foundation dance class for theatre, musical theatre and beginning movement students on theatrical dance: alignment, tempo, rhythm, spatial awareness. This course builds progressively with more complex movement phrases and technical specificity. Geared especially to MTCP and Theatre, Performance Studies and Music student who have 0-3 years’ experience in dance.

courses.title: Modern/Contemporary I
courses.description: Offered at levels I, II, and III each quarter to develop skills pertaining to modern/contemporary techniques. Higher levels progress more rapidly with a greater level of complexity, as class work focuses on a wider range of qualities and aesthetics. Style of modern technique varies with each instructor.

courses.title: Dances of the African Diaspora I
courses.description: Offered at levels I, II, and III to expose students to a variety of techniques that come from Black diasporic practices which may include: jazz, tap, hip-hop, West African, Afro-contemporary. Style of technique varies with each instructor.

courses.title: Jump Rhythm Technique 1
courses.description: Offered at levels I and II. Dancing rhythmically-using jazz rhythms and the syncopated rhythms of funk, hip-hop, and other rock-based music to generate all dance movement.

courses.title: Classical Dance I
courses.description: Offered at levels I, II, and III to cover classical dances from basic principles through advanced skills. Terminology and movements are based on class level. Classical dance can encompass ballet but also dance forms from across the globe (e.g., India, Cambodia, China, Korea, West Africa). Classical dance style varies with instructor.

courses.title: Funk Jazz
courses.description: Foundations in multiple stage jazz dance techniques for MTCP or Dance Majors/Minors who are looking to expand their physical repertoire.

courses.title: Theatre Jazz
courses.description: Foundations in multiple stage jazz dance techniques for MTCP or Dance Majors/Minors who are looking to expand their physical repertoire. Theatre jazz would encompass Fosse technique.

courses.title: Street Jazz
courses.description: This class provides foundations in multiple stage jazz dance techniques for MTCP or Dance Majors/Minors who are looking to expand their physical repertoire. Street jazz could encompass current trends such as femme vogue or heels.

courses.title: Jump Rhythm Tap 1
courses.description: Offered at levels I and II. Using not only the feet but other parts of the body as well to "play" the syncopated rhythms of swinging jazz, Latin jazz, rhythm and blues, funk, and hip-hop music.

courses.title: Dance in/as Culture
courses.description: A cultural approaches understanding to dance. Politics, identity, feminism, semiotics, and critical race theories are among the ideas that intersect with dances and choreographies to help understand how dance is both in and functions as culture.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: DANCE 101-1 or approval of instructor.

courses.title: Anatomy
courses.description: The language and analysis of anatomy; heightening of bodily awareness using kinesthetic sensation and imagery. Combines theory and practice to achieve both intellectual and experiential awareness of the kinesthetics of anatomy.

courses.title: Dance Histories
courses.description: A global approach to histories of dance. Readings, discussion, video screenings, movement analysis, embodied workshops, and research.

courses.title: Dance Composition
courses.description: Fundamental choreographic elements: time, space, shape, form, dynamics, and design. Choreographic exploration of the basic principles of dance composition.

courses.title: Music Theatre Dance II
courses.description: Advanced class focusing on a range of Broadway choreography, dance styles, specialty forms, and audition technique. Taken during junior or senior year.

courses.title: Choreography for Musical Theatre
courses.description: How to manipulate space, time, and energy in short movement studies; creating a movement study in dramatic action that relies on those manipulations; choreographing a short dance using the previous movement studies as guideposts.

courses.title: Modern/Contemporary II
courses.description: Offered at levels I, II, and III each quarter to develop skills pertaining to modern/contemporary techniques. Higher levels progress more rapidly with a greater level of complexity, as class work focuses on a wider range of qualities and aesthetics. Style of modern technique varies with each instructor.

courses.title: Dances of the African Diaspora II
courses.description: Offered at levels I, II, and III to expose students to a variety of techniques that come from Black diasporic practices which may include: jazz, tap, hip hop, West African, Afro-contemporary. As class advances, students learn more advanced rhythmic phrases, more complex body-part isolations, and quicker direction changes in space. Style varies with each instructor.

courses.title: Jump Rhythm Technique II
courses.description: Offered at levels I and II. Dancing rhythmically¿using jazz rhythms and the syncopated rhythms of funk, hip-hop, and other rock- based music to generate all dance movement.

courses.title: Classical Dance II
courses.description: Offered at levels I, II, and III to cover classical dances from basic principles through advanced skills. Terminology and movements are based on class level. Classical dance can encompass ballet but also dance forms from across the globe (e.g., India, Cambodia, China, Korea, West Africa). Classical dance style varies with instructor.

courses.title: Jump Rhythm Tap II
courses.description: Offered at levels I and II. Using not only the feet but other parts of the body as well to "play" the syncopated rhythms of swinging jazz, Latin jazz, rhythm and blues, funk, and hip-hop music. Tap technique. One level is offered each quarter, started at the beginning level. The fundamentals of tap are developed through each level, and rhythmic awareness is expanded.

courses.title: Advanced Choreographic Study
courses.description: This class offers an intermediate-level exploration of choreographic elements for experimental dance-making: time, flow, rhythm, musicality, spatial awareness, shape, form, dynamics, and design. Focus is on trio and group work in addition to other experimental performance modalities.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: DANCE 225-0 or consent of instructor.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: DANCE 225-0 or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Special Topics in Dance Research
courses.description: Research methodologies, dance scholarship, criticism, and historical reconstruction. Critical issues and contemporary problems. Content varies.

courses.title: Modern/Contemporary III
courses.description: Offered at levels I, II, and III each quarter to develop skills pertaining to modern/contemporary techniques. Higher levels progress more rapidly with a greater level of complexity, as class work focuses on a wider range of qualities and aesthetics. Style of modern technique varies with each instructor.

courses.title: Dances of the African Diaspora III
courses.description: Offered at levels I, II, and III to expose students to a variety of techniques that come from Black diasporic practices which may include: jazz, tap, hip hop, West African, Afro-contemporary. As class advances, students learn more advanced rhythmic phrases, more complex body-part isolations, and quicker direction changes in space. Style of jazz technique varies with each instructor.

courses.title: Classical Dance III
courses.description: Offered at levels I, II, and III to cover classical dances from basic principles through advanced skills. Terminology and movements are based on class level. Classical dance can encompass ballet but also dance forms from across the globe (e.g., India, Cambodia, China, Korea, West Africa). Classical dance style varies with instructor.

courses.title: Summer Dance Institute
courses.description: One-week summer workshop exploring various forms of dance with guest artists.

courses.title: Tap lll
courses.description: Tap technique. One level is offered each quarter, starting at beginning level. The fundamentals of tap are developed through each level, and rhythmic awareness is expanded.

courses.title: Independent Study
courses.description: Prerequisite: consent of undergraduate dean after submission of petition.

courses.title: Foundations of Data Science
courses.description: Foundations of Data Science will cover the fundamentals of data science and the context within which this field operates. This course will introduce the steps of the data science lifecycle and the associated data tools and techniques, through implementation in languages such as Python.

courses.title: Data Engineering Studio
courses.description: Data Engineering Studio teaches how to build a sustainable data science lifecycle. Students will analyze data in multiple contexts (e.g., SQL, building machine learning models), share the findings with peers, and practice iteratively refining the analysis based on feedback. They will become acquainted with the common pitfalls in applying data analytics to real-world datasets. Several modern data engineering tools, such as docker containers, Spark, Airflow, and MLFlow, will be covered.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: DATA_ENG 200-0 and 1 unit from each of the following core areas; Statistics Foundations, Intermediate Programming/Algorithmic Skills, and Applied Machine Learning.Prereq for DATA_ENG 300.

prereqSegments.value: and 1 unit from each of the following core areas; Statistics Foundations, Intermediate Programming/Algorithmic Skills, and Applied Machine Learning.Prereq for DATA_ENG 300.

courses.title: Design Thinking and Communication
courses.description: Integrated introduction to the user-centered design process and technical communication. Students will address challenges proposed by project partners by identifying unmet needs, conducting research, generating and evaluating potential solutions, and finally, presenting a final design concept with supporting documentation. Students also enhance their abilities in equitable teamwork, project management, fabrication skills, and producing written, oral, graphical, and interpersonal communication. One lecture, two section meetings, and lab. Co-registration with ENGLISH 106-1 required. Primarily intended for first-year engineering students.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Reserved for McCormick students only.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: Reserved for McCormick students only.

courses.title: Design Thinking and Communication
courses.description: Integrated iteration on the user-centered design process and technical communication. This course will build on the learning objectives from DTC-1 while adding more focus on ethics in design and communication, equitable distribution of teamwork, project management, documenting and communicating progress, and exploring a wider variety of project topics. One lecture, two section meetings, and lab. Co-registration with ENGLISH 106-2 required. Primarily intended for first-year engineering students.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: DSGN 106-1.

courses.title: Design Thinking and Doing
courses.description: Project-based introduction to design, structured as a hands-on studio course. Students learn methods of design innovation and work in teams, exploring ideas, prototyping solutions, and interacting with users. This class is intended for first-year students, sophomores, and juniors.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Reserved for First-Year Students, Sophomores, and Juniors. Non-McCormick students only.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: Reserved for First-Year Students, Sophomores, and Juniors. Non-McCormick students only.

courses.title: Introduction to Design Sketching
courses.description: Design sketching to increase one's skills as a basic but essential form of communication. It is the medium for preliminary ideation. Basic rules and skills in a design studio setting.

courses.title: Machining Basics
courses.description: The intent of this class is to introduce students to a range of basic manufacturing techniques. Students will learn machine tool technology theory and gain familiarity through hands-on practice with manual machine tools and other shop equipment. All the skills taught will culminate in a final project which each student is expected to manufacture on their own. All students will be expected to complete safety training modules.

courses.title: Introduction to Solid Modeling: Solidworks
courses.description: Solid modeling by creating three-dimensional shapes through two-dimensional sketches. Assemblies of individual parts. CAD modeling theory; modeling objects using different approaches for creating identical features. Lecture balanced with hands-on use of SolidWorks.

courses.title: Wireframing and App Design Basics
courses.description: Translate your app ideas into real pixels. Whether you're interested in becoming a product designer, product manager, or just have an app idea— together we'll shape ideas into tangible mockups. This beginner class will take you from sketching on pen and paper to generating interfaces and flows in industry tools like Miro and Figma. You'll see how typography, hierarchy and unity play a role in the product design process, and continue on testing your app before handing it off to product teams or engineers.

courses.title: Visual Thinking for Design
courses.description: Complements the traditional design research and ideation process using visual stimuli. Students will work with visual thinking methods to help expand their understanding of a problem space resulting in a greater generation of ideas and a more tangible organization of our thoughts. DSGN 220-0 and/or some experience with Adobe Photoshop is helpful but not necessary.

courses.title: Introductory Topics in Design
courses.description: Topics suggested by students or faculty members and approved by the institute; taught at an intermediate level.

courses.title: Intermediate Topics in Engineering Design
courses.description: Topics suggested by students and faculty and approved by the institute.

courses.title: Designing Your Life
courses.description: Considers an approach to life as a series of design projects to help students craft a total life. Includes seminar-style discussions, role-playing, short writing assignments, hands-on making, guest speakers, and individual mentoring and coaching.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Reserved for Juniors and Seniors.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: Reserved for Juniors and Seniors.

courses.title: Human-Centered Service Design
courses.description: Project-based, human-centered design approach to service design, with a focus on the design of new or improved services that tap deeply into people's needs for connectedness, belonging, and autonomy. Project outcomes may include organizational structures, service designs, and designed products.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: DSGN 106-1 or DSGN 208-0.

courses.title: User Experience Design
courses.description: Project-based studio course covering the full range of user experience design, from screen-based experience to interaction with physical products to end-to-end environment design.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: DSGN 106-1 or DSGN 208-0.

courses.title: Human-Centered Product Design
courses.description: Project-based course focusing on user needs: observational methods, brainstorming, prototyping, business models, and the social and engineering concerns for product design.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: DSGN 106-1 or DSGN 208-0.

courses.title: Introduction to Industrial Design Methods
courses.description: Introduces the process of product development from an industrial design perspective, with a focus on exploring form through design sketching.

courses.title: Advanced Solid Modeling
courses.description: Provides advanced instruction on the use of CAD modeling using Solidworks software.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: DSGN 240-0 or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Rendering
courses.description: Provides an introduction to Keyshot software, a photorealistic rendering package.

courses.title: Performance and Technology: Composition Workshop
courses.description: In this course students will use basic mechatronics to create compelling movement-based performances. The course will involve workshop exploration of technologies embedded in performance: robots, media, computer interface. Students will create performance projects and discuss theoretical and historical implications of technologies in performance. Hands-on making and engineering workshops will be incorporated to develop skills in technological crafts such as circuit design and fabrication, toward technologically enhanced performance.

courses.title: Computer-Aided Manufacturing
courses.description: Teaches the NX manufacturing environment to program machining operations for CNC milling, as well as the operation of CNC milling machines. Teaches the complete path from part design to manufacture, including operations, tools, and geometry in NX, manufacturing setup and g-code generation, proper machine setup, operation, troubleshooting and optimization for both 2.5D machining and full CNC machining.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: DSGN 106-1 and DSGN 106-2 and one of the following: DSGN 240-0 or MECH_ENG 240-0.

courses.title: Manufacturing Methods for Product Design
courses.description: An introduction to manufacturing processes including casting, injection molding, additive manufacturing, extrusion, machining, joining, and forming, using materials commonly found in modern consumer and industrial products. In weekly hands-on labs, students apply theory of manufacturing processes to the design of parts and process tooling for various fabrication methods. Focus will be placed on adjusting design and process parameters to obtain an optimal outcome for a given manufacturing process.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: DSGN 106-1 and 106-2, and MECH_ENG 240 or instructor approval of previous CAD experience. MaDE students may petition for this course to count in place of the of the ME 340-1 degree requirement.

prereqSegments.value: , and MECH_ENG 240 or instructor approval of previous CAD experience. MaDE students may petition for this course to count in place of the of the ME 340-1 degree requirement.

courses.title: Rapid Prototyping
courses.description: A survey of additive manufacturing methods and hands-on training in the operation of all equipment in the Northwestern Rapid Prototyping lab. Students review the fundamentals and theory behind rapid prototyping methods, materials, applications of RP technology, advanced CAD modeling, and reverse engineering methods. Includes lab work, where students will learn the operation of additive manufacturing machines, laser scanners, and CAD surfacing software.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: DSGN 240-0 or MECH_ENG 240-0, or approval of instructor.

courses.title: Bay Area Design Practicum
courses.description: For Bay Area Immersion Program Participants. This course is an opportunity to immerse in a critical area of need and make the most of our opportunity to engage with people in the Bay Area, joining them as visionaries, thought leaders and activists concerned with shaping our communities and world. This is a dynamic, hands-on studio course requiring project-based work and team collaboration. In this 300-level class, you are expected to be able to rapidly engage in the design process, working with multidisciplinary teams on uncovering opportunities, exploring ideas, prototyping solutions (whether physical, digital or interpersonal), and interacting with users.

courses.title: Portfolio Development & Presentation
courses.description: Creation of an individual portfolio that showcase design work and further career goals. The portfolio physically presents a story that embodies its creator's goals.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Reserved for Juniors and Seniors.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: Reserved for Juniors and Seniors.

courses.title: Industrial Design Projects I
courses.description: Design thinking, user-centric principles of design and DFM. Development of an industrial design project for your personal portfolio. Concept ideation and sketching; use of discovery research and data visualization; problem framing and prototyping; design for manufacturing, Keyshot rendering, rapid prototyping. Pt 1 in two-course sequence; sequence must be taken in consecutive quarters.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: DSGN 243-0 and either DSGN 240-0 or MECH_ENG 240-0 and either DSGN 220-0 or DSGN 320-0; or consent of the instructor. Reserved for Juniors and Seniors.

prereqSegments.value: ; or consent of the instructor. Reserved for Juniors and Seniors.

courses.title: Industrial Design Projects II
courses.description: Design thinking, user-centric principles of design and DFM. Development of an industrial design project for your personal portfolio. Concept ideation and sketching; use of discovery research and data visualization; problem framing and prototyping; design for manufacturing, Keyshot rendering, rapid prototyping.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: DSGN 380-1.

courses.title: Digital Product Development
courses.description: "In this digital product development course, students will experience the full end-to-end product development lifecycle through ideation, build, and continuous improvement. Students will have an opportunity to explore and apply design thinking and agile/scrum methodologies to a client sponsored project. This class is by application only.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Students must have taken DSGN 106 or 208 and be in junior or senior standing.".

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: Students must have taken DSGN 106 or 208 and be in junior or senior standing.".

courses.title: Service Design Studio I
courses.description: Explores the human centered approach to the design of services. Students will apply design thinking to client sponsored projects and synthesize both user and client needs to the design of tangible consumer touch points. This includes experience plans, digital interface designs, communication models, organizational designs, systems and/or brand tonality deliverables. Pt 1 in two-course sequence; sequence must be taken in consecutive quarters.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: DSGN 305-0 or DSGN 306-0 or DSGN 308-0; or consent of instructor. Reserved for Juniors and Seniors.

prereqSegments.value: ; or consent of instructor. Reserved for Juniors and Seniors.

courses.title: Service Design Studio II
courses.description: Explores the human centered approach to the design of services. Students will apply design thinking to client sponsored projects and synthesize both user and client needs to the design of tangible consumer touch points. This includes experience plans, digital interface designs, communication models, organizational designs, systems and/or brand tonality deliverables.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: DSGN 382-1.

courses.title: Interdisciplinary Product Design Projects I
courses.description: Open-ended, team-based product or system design projects in real-world settings. Project research, concept development, professional communication, advanced topics in design. One of DSGN 305-0, DSGN 306-0, or DSGN 308-0 is recommended before taking this course. Pt 1 in two-course sequence; sequence must be taken in consecutive quarters.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: DSGN 106-1 or DSGN 208-0. Reserved for Juniors and Seniors.

courses.title: Interdisciplinary Product Design Projects II
courses.description: Open-ended, team-based product or system design projects in real-world settings. Implementation, evaluation, communication, documentation.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: DSGN 384-1.

courses.title: Manufacturing Engineering Design
courses.description: A hands-on, team-based survey of lean manufacturing concepts such as DFA (design for assembly), DFM (design for manufacturing), automation, quality control, process planning, tooling design, concurrent engineering, and continuous improvement. Students are given the components of an existing product and are challenged to design the manufacturing specifications and process. Design strategy, manufacturing modeling and optimization, engineering documentation, quality control, manufacturing costing and product manufacturing productivity skills are put into practice in a final pilot run of the manufacturing process by each team.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Any IEMS 300 level, MECH_ENG 340-1, DSGN 308, or consent of instructor. Reserved for Juniors and Seniors.

prereqSegments.value: , DSGN 308, or consent of instructor. Reserved for Juniors and Seniors.

courses.title: MaDE Capstone Sequence I
courses.description: The first quarter of a three-quarter, year-long product design and development experience for students in the MaDE program. Senior standing and instructor permission are required.

courses.title: MaDE Capstone Sequence II
courses.description: The second quarterr of a three-quarter, year-long product design and development experience for students in the MaDE program. Senior standing and instructor permission are required.

courses.title: MaDE Capstone Sequence III
courses.description: The third and final quarter of a three-quarter, year-long product design and development experience for students in the MaDE program. Senior standing and instructor permission are required.

courses.title: Advanced Topics in Design
courses.description: Topics suggested by students and faculty and approved by the institute.

courses.title: Independent Study Project
courses.description: Independent study on a design topic supervised by a faculty member.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: consent of instructor.

courses.title: Earth Science for the 21st Century
courses.description: Introduction to earth science through topical issues facing contemporary society. Evolution of the earth, geologic hazards, natural resources, peak oil, climate change, the water cycle, nuclear fuel cycle, geology of US national parks.

courses.title: College Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: First-Year Writing Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: Climate Catastrophes in Earth History
courses.description: Introduction to fundamental components of the earth system that control climate. Exploration of present-day climate change and how climate has changed (sometimes catastrophically) in the geologic past.

courses.title: The Ocean, the Atmosphere & Our Climate
courses.description: The role of the world's oceans in the earth's climate system. Properties of the oceans and marine life. Interaction of oceans, atmosphere, and land.

courses.title: Evolution and the Scientific Method
courses.description: Review of evolutionary theory and its scientific, philosophical, social, and religious impacts.

courses.title: Fantasy Worlds – How to Build Your Own Planet
courses.description: The formation and evolution of rocky planets. Introduction of physical concepts common in the lives of planets as they are in our everyday lives: gravity, heat transport, magnetism, and others. Students will apply these concepts to build their own unique planet, and will present their creation at a culminating poster presentation.

courses.title: Earth System History
courses.description: Evolution of the earth system and its record through geological time. Interactions among the atmosphere, hydrosphere, sediments, and life on earth. Recommended Background: At least one credit in math, chemistry, biology or physics.

courses.title: Earth Systems Science and Climate Change
courses.description: This course covers Earth systems: atmosphere, biosphere, hydrosphere, and geosphere, and their interactions at local and global scales. Topics include an introduction to systems thinking, how Earth processes formed and modify surface environments and climate, the planetary history of Earth systems, and modern human impacts on Earth and climate. Students may not enroll if they have previously completed EARTH 203-0 or ENVR_SCI 201-0.

courses.title: Data Analysis in Earth and Environmental Sciences
courses.description: Overview of data analysis and modeling approaches used in earth, environmental, and planetary sciences, including data standards, environmental statistics, box modeling, geospatial and geo-temporal analyses. Assessments focus on skill-mastery, and include small group mini-projects. The data sets used span sub-disciplines of earth and environmental sciences, such as population, climate, solid earth, and water science.
distroTags: Empirical and Deductive Reasoning Foundational Dis

courses.title: Ecology & Environmental Change
courses.description: Dimensions of the ecological niche; growth and regulation of populations; interactions among populations; community structure and diversity; conservation. Students may not enroll if they have previously completed ENVR_SCI 202.

courses.title: Decision-Making in the Anthropocene
courses.description: Introduction to human interactions with the environment. Topics may include but are not limited to energy, sustainability, pollution, and climate change. Students may not enroll if they have previously completed ENVR SCI 203.

courses.title: Physical Earth Science
courses.description: Physical Earth Science synthesizes physical geology and historical geology. Physical geology examines the creation, modification, and characterization of rocks and minerals, as well as internal and external processes that shape the planet. Historical geology examines the origin and evolution of Earth and life through time. The course includes required laboratory sections and culminates with a required weekend field trip.

courses.title: Physics of the Earth's Interior
courses.description: The earth as a planet: origin, composition, and evolution of the solar system and the earth; internal structure of the earth; plate tectonics. Recommended Background: At least one credit in math, chemistry, biology or physics.

courses.title: Independent Study
courses.description: Special problems under direct faculty supervision. Comprehensive report required. Consent of instructor required.

courses.title: Introduction to Problem-Solving in Economics
courses.description: For participants in the Warren Summer Bridge program. Developing facility with quantitative tools to solve problems in Economics.

courses.title: College Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: First-Year Writing Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: Introduction to Macroeconomics
courses.description: An introduction to economics with emphasis on macroeconomics. Topics include: scarcity and choice, elements of supply and demand, inflation, unemployment, recessions, booms, fiscal and monetary policy, international balance of payments, and budget deficits.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: basic algebra and graphing.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Mentored Study Program
courses.description: Study sessions facilitated by undergraduate peer leaders, for students enrolled in ECON 201. Meets weekly in small groups to collaboratively review material, solve practice problems, clarify concepts, and enhance study strategies. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U.

courses.title: Peer-Guided Study Group: Introduction to Macroeconomics
courses.description: Peer-guided study group for students enrolled in ECON 201-0. Meets weekly in small groups, along with a peer facilitator, to collaboratively review material, work through practice problems, and clarify course concepts. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U.

courses.title: Introduction to Microeconomics
courses.description: An introductory course on the fundamentals of microeconomics. The behavior of individuals and firms in deciding on prices and allocation of scarce resources. Topics include: consumer preferences, costs of production, equilibrium prices and output, different market types, potential market failures, and the role of government interventions and public policy.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: ECON 201-0.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Mentored Study Program
courses.description: Study sessions facilitated by undergraduate peer leaders, for students enrolled in ECON 202-0. Meets weekly in small groups to collaboratively review material, solve practice problems, clarify concepts, and enhance study strategies. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U.

courses.title: Peer-Guided Study Group: Introduction to Microeconomics
courses.description: Peer-guided study group for students enrolled in ECON 202-0. Meets weekly in small groups, along with a peer facilitator, to collaboratively review material, work through practice problems, and clarify course concepts. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U.

courses.title: Business Strategy
courses.description: Firms' choices of prices, capacity, location, quality, variety, investment and product innovation when navigating complex economic environments shaped by government policy and inter-firm rivalries. (Majors and Minors should not take this course, but should take ECON 349-0 instead. Students may not receive credit if they have completed ECON 349-0).
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: ECON 202-0; MATH 220-1.

courses.title: Introduction to Applied Econometrics
courses.description: An introduction to econometrics. The underlying theory of regression and the practical application of these techniques to data sets. Understanding and diagnosing common statistical problems encountered during estimation. All other substitutions (including AP Statistics) must be cleared through the Director of Undergraduate Studies for Economics.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: ECON 201-0, ECON 202-0, MATH 220-1, STAT 210-0 or higher level statistics class.

courses.title: Mentored Study Program
courses.description: Study sessions facilitated by undergraduate peer leaders, for students enrolled in ECON 310-1. Meets weekly in small groups to collaboratively review material, solve practice problems, clarify concepts, and enhance study strategies. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U.

courses.title: Mentored Study Program
courses.description: Study sessions facilitated by undergraduate peer leaders, for students enrolled in ECON 310-2. Meets weekly in small groups to collaboratively review material, solve practice problems, clarify concepts, and enhance study strategies. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U.

courses.title: Peer-Guided Study Group: Microeconomics I
courses.description: Peer-guided study group for students enrolled in ECON 310-1. Meets weekly in small groups, along with a peer facilitator, to collaboratively review material, work through practice problems, and clarify course concepts. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U.

courses.title: Peer-Guided Study Group: Microeconomics II
courses.description: Peer-guided study group for students enrolled in ECON 310-2. Meets weekly in small groups, along with a peer facilitator, to collaboratively review material, work through practice problems, and clarify course concepts. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U.

courses.title: Senior Seminar
courses.description: For students of superior ability. Original research on a topic of interest to the student, culminating in a senior thesis. By department invitation only. Grade of K given in 398-1.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: ECON 281-0, ECON 310-1, ECON 310-2, ECON 311-0, MATH 220-2, MATH 230-1; at least four 300-level economics electives.

courses.title: Senior Seminar
courses.description: For students of superior ability. Original research on a topic of interest to the student, culminating in a senior thesis. By department invitation only.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: ECON 398-1.

courses.title: Independent Study
courses.description: Advanced work through reading, research, and discussion to build on economics coursework taken by the student. Project to be decided by mutual agreement with a faculty member.

courses.title: Electrons, Photons, and Bits: Adventures in Electrical and Computer Engineering
courses.description: Introduction to contemporary topics in electrical engineering (5 weeks) and computer engineering (5 weeks) via lectures, demonstrations, and lab tours. No exams, but two in-depth term papers are required: the first on an electrical engineering topic reviewed during the first half of the course, and the second on a computer engineering topic reviewed during the second half of the course.

courses.title: Special Topics in Electrical Engineering
courses.description: Topics suggested by students or faculty and approved by the department.

courses.title: Introduction to Electrical Engineering
courses.description: Introduction to fundamental concepts and applications of electrical engineering. Topics include: circuit analysis from dc resistive networks to networks of impedances operating in the sinusoidal steady-state; circuit simplification and the Thevenin equivalent circuit; complex numbers and phasors; series and parallel inductor-capacitor resonance; simple analog filters; power transfer and impedance matching; op amps realizing active filters; signal spectra and the Fourier transform; signal sampling and aliasing; bandwidth and channel capacity; simple feedback and control systems; semiconductor electronics and devices including diodes, transistors, light-emitting diodes, and lasers.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Circuits
courses.description: Fundamental concepts in electrical circuits; circuit analysis and network theorems; linearity and superposition; series/parallel combinations of R, L, and C circuits; sinusoidal forcing; complex frequency and Bode plots; mutual inductance and transformers; two port networks.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: ELEC_ENG 202-0.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Signals & Systems
courses.description: Comprehensive introduction to analysis of continuous and discrete-time signals and systems. Linear time-invariant systems, convolution. Fourier series representations of periodic signals. Continuous and discrete-time Fourier transforms. Laplace transform; z-transform.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: ELEC_ENG 202-0.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Solid State Engineering
courses.description: Crystalline state of matter; quantum phenomena and quantum mechanics; electrons in atoms, atoms in crystals, electrons in crystals; semiconductors; thermal properties of crystals, electrical properties of crystals and semiconductors; pn junction.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: ELEC_ENG 202-0; PHYSICS 135-3; MATH 228-2.

courses.title: Fund of Electromagnetics & Photonics
courses.description: Introduction to electromagnetic waves in electrical engineering. Topics include: analysis of transmission lines in the time domain and the sinusoidal steady-state; fundamentals of electrostatics and magnetostatics; Maxwell's equations for time-varying electromagnetic fields; plane electromagnetic wave propagation, reflection, and transmission at material interfaces; Poynting's theorem; introduction to fiber optics and photonics.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: (ELEC_ENG 202-0 and ELEC_ENG 221-0 and PHYSICS 135-2 and MATH 228-2) or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Electronics
courses.description: Diode, BJT, and FET circuits; design using ideal operational amplifiers; feedback; frequency response; biasing; current sources and mirrors; small-signal analysis; design of operational amplifiers.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: ELEC_ENG 221-0.

courses.title: Physical Electronics and Devices
courses.description: The physical basis of electronic and optoelectronic devices and their application in analog and digital systems. Diodes, transistors, LEDs, photodetectors, and lasers are described, and their properties explored.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: ELEC_ENG 221-0; PHYSICS 135-2.

courses.title: Special Topics in Electrical Engineering
courses.description: Topics suggested by students or faculty and approved by the department.

courses.title: Information Theory & Learning
courses.description: This course gives students analytical tools to quantify information, perform inference, and study the relationship of information and learning. The course covers information measures, the source and the channel coding theorems, statistical inference, and learning with neural networks. In particular, the course explores a common set of models and tools used by both machine learning and state-of-the-art data compression and error-control codes. This course is aimed at undergraduate students in engineering, science, mathematics, and computing. It expects familiarity with undergraduate-level calculus, probability theory, and linear algebra.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Basic probability theory (ELEC_ENG 302-0 or equivalent).

courses.title: Deep Learning Foundations from Scratch
courses.description: The course covers the fundamentals of deep learning and numerical optimization, with many application examples.

courses.title: Deep Reinforcement Learning
courses.description: Fundamentals of Deep Reinforcement Learning starting from its roots in dynamic programming and optimal control, and ending with some of the most popular applications in practice today; basic Q-Learning algorithm and its extensions; deep Q-Learning. Through exercises and a final course project students will gain significant hands-on experience coding up and testing reinforcement systems on a variety of interesting problems.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: ELEC_ENG 375-0 and ELEC_ENG 335-0.

courses.title: Machine Learning: Foundations, Applications, and Algorithms
courses.description: The course covers the fundamentals of machine learning and numerical optimization, with many application examples.

courses.title: Electrical Engineering Design
courses.description: Design of electrical and electronic devices, circuits, and systems by the application of the engineering sciences, economics, and Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers or other national standards.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: senior standing.

courses.title: Projects
courses.description: Seminar and projects for advanced undergraduates on subjects of current interest in electrical and computer engineering.

courses.title: Summer Academic Workshop in Writing
courses.description: An introduction to college writing. Students learn how to use a process of planning, drafting, revising, and editing to write papers that are clear, concise, interesting, and persuasive.

courses.title: College Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: First-Year Writing Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: Expository Writing
courses.description: Emphasizes all phases of the composition process, research methods, and critical thinking. Careful review of student papers and reports. May be repeated for credit with different topic.

courses.title: College Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: First-Year Writing Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: Writing in Special Contexts
courses.description: Introduction to expository writing similar to ENGLISH 105-0 but paired with a course in another discipline.

courses.title: Writing in Special Contexts
courses.description: Introduction to expository writing similar to ENGLISH 105-0 but paired with a course in another discipline.

courses.title: Literary Histories
courses.description: Content varies, but all versions of this course engage with at least 200 years of literature from a particular tradition, genre, or theme, with an emphasis on literary history and narratives of continuity and change over time. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.

courses.title: Introduction to Creative Writing
courses.description: Forms and techniques of poetry, fiction, and creative nonfiction.

courses.title: Intermediate Composition
courses.description: Expository writing at an intermediate level. Emphasis on techniques for writing clearly, precisely, and persuasively. May be repeated for credit with different topic.

courses.title: Reading & Writing Poetry
courses.description: Forms and techniques of verse. May not be taken earlier than winter quarter of the first year. Seniors may enroll only with department consent.

courses.title: Reading and Writing Fiction
courses.description: Forms and techniques of fiction.

courses.title: Reading & Writing Creative Non-Fiction
courses.description: Forms and techniques of creative nonfiction.

courses.title: Topics in Genre Writing
courses.description: Forms and techniques of genre writing (e.g., screenwriting, young adult fiction, adaptation, memoir). May be repeated for credit with different topic.

courses.title: British Literary Traditions
courses.description: Chronological survey of British literature in its cultural contexts from Beowulf to the 18th century. Suitable for majors and nonmajors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.

courses.title: British Literary Traditions
courses.description: Chronological survey of British literature in its cultural contexts from the late 18th century to the present day. Suitable for majors and nonmajors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.

courses.title: Introduction to Poetry
courses.description: Introduction to poetic analysis across a wide range of authors and periods, with emphasis on poetry’s formal elements, genre conventions, and the historical, material, and cultural conditions that shape them. Suitable for majors and nonmajors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.

courses.title: Introduction to Drama
courses.description: Introduction to the fundamental elements of drama as perceived in performance, exploring how dramatic works communicate from text to stage to audience. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.

courses.title: Introduction to Fiction
courses.description: Introduction to the analysis of prose fiction from the 18th century to the present day, with special attention to narrative strategies and their historical and cultural contexts. Suitable for majors and nonmajors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.

courses.title: Introduction to Film and Its Literatures
courses.description: Introduction to the theory and practice of formal film analysis. Students will gain a critical overview of multiple forms of film-related writing, including historical scholarship, film theory, popular reviews, legal documents, manifestos, and movie-inspired fiction. Suitable for majors and nonmajors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.

courses.title: Topics in Literature, Film, and Media
courses.description: In contrast to ENGLISH 214-0, which introduces students to a wider mix of literary, cinematic, and media traditions, this course explores specific topics, forms, periods, or genres in greater depth. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.

courses.title: The Bible as Literature
courses.description: Selected books of the Hebrew bible and New Testament studied from a literary perspective. Students will consider issues of plot, character, genre, narrative strategy, and theories of interpretation. Suitable for majors and nonmajors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected. Students may not receive credit if they took the same class as the former COMP_LIT 210-0.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Introduction to Shakespeare
courses.description: Introduction to representative Shakespearean plays, including comedies, tragedies, and romances. Suitable for majors and nonmajors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.

courses.title: Introduction to Postcolonial Literature
courses.description: Introduces the field of postcolonial literary studies, including a critical overview of key terms and major debates. Students will read selected postcolonial literary works, paying particular attention to their formal properties, alongside theoretical and historical texts on colonialism and its aftermath. Suitable for majors and nonmajors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Introduction to African American Literature
courses.description: Literature of Black people in the United States from slavery to freedom. Works of major writers and significant but unsung bards of the past. ENGLISH 266-0 and BLK_ST 210-0 are taught together; may receive credit for only 1 of these courses. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.
distroTags: U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Topics in African American Literature
courses.description: Content varies. In contrast to ENGLISH 266-0, which offers a broad survey of the field, this course explores particular topics in African American literature in greater depth. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.
distroTags: U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: American Literary Traditions
courses.description: Representative writers and works of American literature in cultural context. Works discussed range from culture contact to the Civil War. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.

courses.title: American Literary Traditions
courses.description: Representative 19th- and 20th-century works of American literature in cultural context. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.

courses.title: Introduction to 20th-Century American Literature
courses.description: Representative works of American literature since World War I. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.

courses.title: Introduction to Native American and Indigenous Literatures
courses.description: Survey of Native American and Indigenous literatures from pre-contact periods to the present. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.
distroTags: U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Introduction to Asian American Literature
courses.description: Survey of Asian American literature from the early 20th century to the present, covering a range of genres and ethnicities. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected. Taught with ASIAN_AM 275-0; may receive credit for only 1 of these courses.
distroTags: U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Topics in Asian American Literature
courses.description: Content varies. In contrast to ENGLISH 275-0, which offers a broad survey of the field, this course explores particular topics in Asian American literature in greater depth. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected. Taught with ASIAN_AM 276-0. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.
distroTags: U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Introduction to Latinx Literature
courses.description: Surveys major writers and movements from the Spanish colonial era to the present, covering a range of genres and ethnicities. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected. Taught with LATINO 277-0 and SPANISH 277-0; may receive credit for only 1 of these courses.
distroTags: U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Topics in Multiethnic Literature
courses.description: Although content varies depending on the specific course topic, all versions of this course examine the ways in which American ethnic identity has been constructed and discussed through literature. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.
distroTags: U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Topics in Postcolonial & Comparative Literatures
courses.description: Content varies. In contrast to ENGLISH 280-0, which offers a broad survey of the field, this course explores particular topics in postcolonial and comparative literatures in greater depth. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Writing and Speaking in Business
courses.description: Emphasizes writing and speaking to inform and persuade audiences in business contexts to achieve business goals. Attention to clear, compelling, and well-organized written and oral communication.

courses.title: Introduction to Literature and the Environment
courses.description: Studies in literature and other media oriented by ecological thinking. An introduction to the ways in which language, literature, and aesthetic production shape ideas about nature, varying widely across historical and cultural contexts. Suitable for majors and nonmajors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected. ENGLISH 283-0 taught with ENVR_POL 283-0; may not receive credit for both.

courses.title: Topics in Literature and the Environment
courses.description: Content varies. In contrast to ENGLISH 283-0, which offers a broad survey of the field, this course explores particular topics in greater depth. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected. May be repeated for credit with a different topic. ENGLISH 284-0 and ENVR_POL 284-0 are taught together.

courses.title: Topics in Literature and Culture
courses.description: Although content varies depending on the course topic, all versions of this course explore literary texts in their cultural contexts. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.

courses.title: Topics in Global Literatures
courses.description: Although content varies depending on the course topic, all versions of this course explore literary texts written outside of Britain and the United States. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Topics in Literature and Ethics
courses.description: Although content varies depending on the course topic, all versions of this course explore literary texts that emerge from, and speak back to, ethical and/or religious traditions. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Seminar in Reading and Interpretation
courses.description: Close reading of literary works in the light of various critical methodologies in literary study, with an emphasis on developing skills in qualitative analysis and oral and written expression. Required for English literature majors and minors and Creative Writing majors; recommended for Creative Writing minors. Offers good preparation for higher-level literary study. May be taken only once.

courses.title: Practical Rhetoric
courses.description: The theory of writing and skills that underlie good writing; primarily for teachers in secondary schools and universities.

courses.title: Advanced Composition
courses.description: This course is for undergraduate students in their second year or above who want to develop their abilities to write in different styles and for different audiences.

courses.title: Advanced Poetry Writing
courses.description: Content varies. May be repeated for credit with different topic.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: ENGLISH 206-0, ENGLISH 207-0, ENGLISH 208-0, or Department consent.

courses.title: Advanced Fiction Writing
courses.description: Content varies. May be repeated for credit with different topic.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: ENGLISH 206-0, ENGLISH 207-0, ENGLISH 208-0, or Department consent.

courses.title: Advanced Creative Nonfiction Writing
courses.description: Content varies. May be repeated for credit with different topic.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: ENGLISH 206-0, ENGLISH 207-0, ENGLISH 208-0, or department consent.

courses.title: Advanced Creative Cross-Genre Writing
courses.description: Content varies. May be repeated twice for credit with a different topic.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: ENGLISH 206-0, ENGLISH 207-0, ENGLISH 208-0, or department consent.

courses.title: Studies in Asian American Literature
courses.description: Although content varies depending on the course topic, all versions of this course explore Asian American literature in its cultural contexts. Selected texts range from the early 20th century to the present and engage with a range of genres and ethnicities. Recommended for students with prior coursework in English, Asian American Studies, or a related discipline. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.
distroTags: U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Topics in Latinx Literature
courses.description: Although content varies depending on the course topic, all versions of this course examine Latinx literature in its cultural contexts. Selected texts range from the Spanish colonial era to the present and engage with a range of genres and ethnicities. Recommended for students with prior coursework in English, Latinx Studies, or a related discipline. May be repeated for credit with a different topic. ENGLISH 377-0 taught with LATINO 377-0.
distroTags: U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Literature & Medicine
courses.description: Although content varies depending on the course topic, all versions of this course explore medicine through a variety of literary media. Potential subjects of study include illness, ageing, medical treatment, wellness culture, and doctor-patient relationships. Recommended for students with prior coursework in English, pre-health fields, or related disciplines. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.

courses.title: Literature and Law
courses.description: Although content varies depending on the course topic, all versions of this course explore the representation of law in literature and culture, and how law uses language. Students may perform literary analyses of legal discourse alongside literary texts. Recommended for students with prior coursework in English, Legal Studies, or a related discipline. May be repeated for credit with different topic.

courses.title: Studies in Literature and Ethics
courses.description: Although content varies depending on the course topic, all versions of this course explore literary texts that emerge from, and speak back to, ethical and/or religious traditions. Recommended for students with prior coursework in English, Religious Studies, Philosophy, or related disciplines. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: The Situation of Writing
courses.description: The sociology of writers, writing, publication, dissemination of literature, and reading.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: admission to writing major.

courses.title: Theory and Practice of Poetry
courses.description: Tenets of poetry in English, including prosody, form, metaphor, voice, experimentation; involves intensive writing practice and culminates in the production of a long poem.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: admission to creative writing sequence.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: admission to creative writing sequence.

courses.description: Tenets of fictional realism and its substitutes; practice in different applications of plot, narrative technique, point of view; culminates in the writing of a novella.
