courses.title: Honors Sequence
courses.description: The first course in a two-course sequence for seniors preparing an honors essay in the literature major. Students pursue individual research topics under the direction of the departmental honors coordinator and a faculty advisor. Admission by application only. Students will receive a K grade pending completion of the honors thesis.

courses.title: Honors Sequence
courses.description: The second course in a two-course sequence for seniors preparing an honors essay in the literature major. Students pursue individual research topics under the direction of the departmental honors coordinator and a faculty advisor. Admission by application only. Students will receive a K grade pending completion of the honors thesis.

courses.title: Independent Study
courses.description: Individual projects with faculty guidance. Open to majors with junior or senior standing and to senior minors. May be elected two times, but only 1 unit at a time.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: consent of department.

courses.title: Principles of Entrepreneurship
courses.description: This course serves as the foundational course for the undergraduate minor in entrepreneurship. This survey-style course is a good choice for students who wish to familiarize themselves with the world of entrepreneurship. Students will learn to think like entrepreneurs and will be introduced to key topics relevant to entrepreneurship, including customer discovery, finance, and marketing. They will also learn how to understand and perform analyses of a broad range of entrepreneurial activities related to different applications of entrepreneurship. Taught with IEMS 225-0; may not receive credit for both courses.

courses.title: Financing Entrepreneurial Ventures
courses.description: Topics selected from work of current interest in entrepreneurship and innovation. May be repeated for credit.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: ENTREP 225-0 and ENTREP 325-0 recommended.

courses.title: Leadership, Ethics, and You
courses.description: This class satisfies a core requirement for the undergraduate minor in entrepreneurship. The class is designed for students who want to explore how ethics and integrity are tied to leadership, and how leadership skills can contribute to future success in both entrepreneurial endeavors and traditional career paths.

courses.title: Tech Ethics and Business Integrity
courses.description: This course will explore the unique ethical challenges that face the technology industry, utilizing recent and historical case studies. Students will be challenged to think about what it means to run a business with integrity by examining common issues within the workplace as well as the responsibility that businesses have to customers. Data privacy abuses, sexual misconduct, and corporate greed, among many other topics, will be addressed.

courses.title: Special Topics
courses.description: Topics selected from work of current interest in entrepreneurship and innovation. May be repeated for credit.

courses.title: College Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: First-Year Writing Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: Food and Society: An Introduction
courses.description: Overview of past and present food systems from a sociological perspective, examining the roles of culture, government policy, and social movements in shaping such systems and future alternatives. ENVR_POL 211-0 and SOCIOL 211-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Environment and Society
courses.description: Key environmental problems, such as climate change and oil spills; how they are shaped by the market, government regulations, and social movements; possible solutions. SOCIOL 212-0 and ENVR_POL 212-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: The Politics of Disaster: A Global Environmental History
courses.description: A global survey of natural disasters over the last several centuries. Key themes include: inequality, social vulnerability, environmental racism, historical memory and forgetting, and preventability. Using disaster history to better understand and meet the present and future challenges of global climate change. HISTORY 251-0 and ENVR_POL 251-0 are taught together; students may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Introduction to Literature and the Environment
courses.description: Studies in literature and other media oriented by ecological thinking. An introduction to the ways in which language, literature, and aesthetic production shape ideas about nature, varying widely across historical and cultural contexts. Suitable for majors and nonmajors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected. ENGLISH 283-0 taught with ENVR_POL 283-0; may not receive credit for both.

courses.title: Topics in Literature and the Environment
courses.description: Content varies. In contrast to ENGLISH 283-0, which offers a broad survey of the field, this course explores particular topics in greater depth. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected. May be repeated for credit with a different topic. ENGLISH 284-0 and ENVR_POL 284-0 are taught together.

courses.title: Special Topics in Environmental Policy and Culture
courses.description: Lecture course on environmental issues of current interest to students and faculty. May be repeated for credit with different topic.

courses.title: American Environmental History
courses.description: American history from precontact to the present, focusing on the role of the natural world in human history and the role of human thought and action in natural history. ENVR_POL 309-0 and HISTORY 309-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.
distroTags: U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Environmental Art and Advocacy
courses.description: This course examines the use of visual and media arts for public advocacy regarding environmental concerns. We focus on advocacy groups using artistic works, practices, or techniques, and on artists shaping their work to inform and persuade the public. COMM_ST 373-0 taught with ENVR_POL 335-0; students may not receive credit for both.

courses.title: Hazard, Disaster and Society
courses.description: This course examines how socioeconomic and environmental factors work together to cause hazards and disasters in human society. In this course, we learn the main concepts about disasters, such as preparedness, vulnerability, resilience, response, mitigation, etc. We learn that a disaster does not have the same effect on everyone, and factors of social inequality such as race, ethnicity, class, and gender make people more vulnerable to the impacts of disasters. ENVR_POL 337-0 and GBL_HLTH 337-0 are taught together; students may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Environmental Justice
courses.description: This course examines how environmental problems reflect and exacerbate social inequality. In this course, we learn the definition of environmental (in)justice, the history of environmental justice and discuss examples of environmental justice. We will learn about environmental movements and local resistance to protect natural resources. ENVR_POL 338-0 and GBL_HLTH 338-0 are taught together; students may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Silent but Loud: Negotiating Health in a Cultural, Food, Poverty, Environ. Caste
courses.description: This course explores how (un)health as a language is connected to certain bodies. Students will analyze the origins of bio-measurements used to justify "Othering" (not fitting within the norms of a dominant social group) and learn to formulate counternarratives to dominant ideological language that silently forms stereotypes, controlling images, hypervisibility, and invisibility that echo loudly as contributing to health inequities. GBL_HLTH 339-0 and ENVR_POL 339-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Global Environments and World History
courses.description: The planet’s life support systems are at risk. This introductory course explores the recent histories of big environmental problems around the world, including industrialization, toxic contaminants, climate change, extractive economies, intercontinental warfare, and energy regimes. ENVR_POL 340-0 and HISTORY 376-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Land, Identity and the Sacred
courses.description: Study of Native American sacred sites and cultural landscapes and their relationships to land, ceremony, history, and tribal/ethnic identity.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Introduction to Ethnobiology: Theories of Human, Plant and Animal Interactions
courses.description: The scientific study of dynamic relationships among peoples, biota, and environments.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Introduction to Cultural Resource Management and Environmental Policy
courses.description: Understanding what makes places, landscapes, structures and intangible practices culturally significant and the laws and policies that govern cultural resources.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Animal Law
courses.description: Survey of laws, regulations, and cultural norms regarding nonhuman animals and animal ownership in the United States. History of animal protection movement, wildlife regulation, hunting and fishing rights, livestock care and slaughter, animal experimentation, anti-cruelty legislation, and companion animal law. ENVR_POL 360-0 and LEGAL_ST 360-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: LEGAL_ST 206-0 or POLI_SCI 230-0, or instructor approval.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Climate Change Law and Policy
courses.description: Examination of the potential role of the law in confronting climate change from an institutional and policy perspective. Topics include the science associated with climate change, the role of key international climate treaty regimes, and national, state and local responses to climate change in the United States.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Ocean Law and Policy
courses.description: Study of primary threats posed to ocean ecosystems and the role of international law in addressing these threats. Focus is on the role of international law, including treaties and customary international law in addressing these threats.

courses.title: International Wildlife Law and Policy
courses.description: Exploration of the role of international law in addressing the biodiversity crisis and efforts to protect wildlife species. Provides students with a foundation in international law, including skills in analyzing treaty provisions.

courses.title: Contemporary Issues In Energy
courses.description: Charts the development of primary energy sources through the history of the United States, as well as the current status of energy sources, technologies, consumption patterns, conservation and energy policies. Emphasis on the environmental effects of various choices made at each step of the energy cycle, and an examination of those choices from technological, legal, and socioeconomic perspectives.

courses.title: Independent Study
courses.description: Independent project in student's area of interest. Readings and conferences. Comprehensive term paper required.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: consent of program director.

courses.title: Special Topics in Environmental Sciences
courses.description: Lecture course on environmental science topics of interest to students and faculty. May be repeated for credit with different topic.

courses.title: Independent Study
courses.description: Independent research on special problems under direct supervision of a faculty adviser. Comprehensive report required.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: consent of program director.

courses.title: Elementary Applied Linear Algebra
courses.description: Basic linear algebra methods including basic matrix/vector operations, solution of linear systems of equations, eigenvalues, and singular values. Focus will be on applications of the methods on a range of engineering topics including: least squares and data fitting, game theory, graph theory, principal component analysis, linear programming, and other related engineering topics.

courses.title: Honors Calculus for Engineers
courses.description: Calculus sequence for the mathematically interested students who want to dig deeper, cover more mathematical material, and see more applications than the standard calculus sequence offers. It also provides an introduction to computation (no previous experience required). Satisfies the same requirements as Math 228-1,2.

courses.title: Honors Calculus for Engineers

It also provides an introduction to computation (no previous experience required). Satisfies the same requirements as Math 228-1,2.

courses.description: Special studies to be carried out under faculty direction. Credit to be arranged.

courses.title: Elementary French
courses.description: The first course of a three-quarter sequence for true beginners. This course covers grammar, vocabulary, reading, writing, conversation and culture. The aim of the course is to learn and develop skills in speaking, understanding, reading, writing and cultural competence. Class will be conducted exclusively in French.

courses.title: Culture and Society
courses.description: Development of fluency, accuracy, and creativity in speaking, comprehension, reading, and writing French; introduction to social, cultural, and literary topics.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Grade of at least C- in FRENCH 121-3 or Department placement.

courses.title: Writing Workshop: Cultural Encounters in Contemporary France
courses.description: Practical study of French grammar and structure; students develop and improve writing skills through practice in preparing short compositions.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 125-3, FRENCH 201-0, or Department placement.

courses.title: Oral Workshop: Individual and Society in France Today
courses.description: Practical course to increase listening comprehension, build vocabulary and idiom use, and enhance communication skills.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 125-3, FRENCH 201-0 or Department placement.

courses.title: Acting French
courses.description: Use of dramatic scenes, dialogues, songs and performance to help students improve their language skills and develop their interpretive, interpersonal and intercultural competence at the Intermediate Mid/High level.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 121-3 or FRENCH 125-3 or FRENCH 201-0 or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Reading Literatures in French
courses.description: Introduction to texts in various genres such as essay, poetry, drama, novel, and autobiography, from at least two periods from the Middle Ages to the present.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 202-0, AP score of 5, or consent of instructor.

prereqSegments.value: , AP score of 5, or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Reading Cultures in French
courses.description: Introduction to French and/or francophone cultures through texts and media from at least two periods; major themes, issues, and debates.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 202-0, AP score of 5, or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Introducing the Novel
courses.description: Textual interpretation and analysis of French novels from different periods, with special attention to formal issues.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 210-0 or FRENCH 211-0, or consent of instructor.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Introducing Theatre
courses.description: Textual interpretation and analysis of French plays from different periods, with special attention to formal issues. Principles of tragedy and comedy; contemporary developments.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 210-0 or FRENCH 211-0, or consent of instructor. Credit not allowed for both FRENCH 272-0 and FRENCH 279-0.

prereqSegments.value: , or consent of instructor. Credit not allowed for both

courses.title: Introducing Poetry
courses.description: Textual interpretation and analysis of French poetry from different periods, with special attention to formal issues. Overview of major poetic movements.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 210-0 or FRENCH 211-0, or consent of instructor.

courses.title: French Existentialism
courses.description: Existentialism in its literary, philosophical, and cultural manifestations.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Theater in Translation
courses.description: Representative French plays from the 17th through 20th centuries; basic concepts of genre; social and historical context. Credit not allowed for both FRENCH 279-0 and FRENCH 272-0.

courses.title: Major Topics in French and Francophone Studies
courses.description: An introduction to major themes or problems in the study of French and Francophone studies for a wide audience. Potential topics include: literary approaches to scientific language, representing the 19th century in film, the French novel in translation, laughing in French, etc. Taught in English.

courses.title: Language and Culture
courses.description: Study of French language and culture in Paris. Restricted to students in Northwestern's Paris programs. Students completing this course must take a placement exam before continuing French at Northwestern.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Fulfillment of Weinberg College French language proficiency via AP Score of 5; French 121-3, 125-3 or 201; or validation of proficiency.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: Fulfillment of Weinberg College French language proficiency via AP Score of 5; French 121-3, 125-3 or 201; or validation of proficiency.

courses.title: French Phonetics
courses.description: Development of near-native spoken French through practice in correct pronunciation. Phonetic system of contemporary French; introduction to basic issues of theoretical phonetics.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 202-0, FRENCH 203-0, or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Advanced Language in Context: Society and Popular Culture
courses.description: Practical study of structure, syntax, and usage of French through contemporary media, cinema, theater, and popular culture.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 202-0 or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Advanced Writing: Finding Your Voice in French
courses.description: Development of written expression for different communicative needs and functions based on the study of French writing styles and techniques.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 202-0 or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Advanced Conversation: Debating Contemporary France
courses.description: Development of advanced proficiency and confidence in spoken French through practice of speech and discussion of issues in current French media and culture. Emphasis on culturally appropriate usage.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 202-0, FRENCH 203-0, or consent of instructor.

courses.title: French For Professions
courses.description: French language as used in professional contexts. May include study of a specific field and differences from its American counterpart. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 202-0 or consent of instructor.

courses.title: The Middle Ages & Renaissance
courses.description: Study of literary texts of the French Middle Ages and Renaissance with emphasis on their historical and literary-historical contexts.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 271-0, FRENCH 272-0, or FRENCH 273-0, or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Medieval French Narratives
courses.description: Major narrative works of the French Middle Ages in historical context. Content varies; may include epics such as the Song of Roland, romances such as Chrétien de Troyes's Perceval, and narratives of childhood. Texts read in modern French versions.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 271-0, FRENCH 272-0, or FRENCH 273-0, or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Topics in Renaissance Literature
courses.description: Study of literary and other texts of the French Renaissance with emphasis on their literary, historical, and political contexts. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 271-0, FRENCH 272-0, or FRENCH 273-0, or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Montaigne and Modernity
courses.description: In-depth study of the work of Michel de Montaigne and his models within the context of Renaissance history, politics and philosophy. All readings and discussion in French.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 271-0, FRENCH 272-0, or FRENCH 273-0, or consent of instructor.

courses.title: 17th Century Literature
courses.description: Topics and issues related to the literature and culture of 17th century France. Content varies; topics covered previously include theater and its social and political contexts, the rise of rational thought, and the development of fiction and poetry. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 271-0, FRENCH 272-0, or FRENCH 273-0, or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Sexual Politics and the Ancien Regime
courses.description: Literary, intellectual, and political role of women in view of the debates generated by the issues of women's power in the public sphere before the French Revolution.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 271-0, FRENCH 272-0, or FRENCH 273-0, or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Rousseau and the French Revolution
courses.description: Analysis of Rousseau's political thought and major literary works and their impact on Revolutionary ideology and culture.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 271-0, FRENCH 272-0, or FRENCH 273-0, or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Studies in the Enlightenment
courses.description: Authors such as Rousseau, Diderot, Montesquieu, Voltaire, and Graffigny in relation to Enlightenment debates about science, religion, political authority, human nature, colonialism, gender, and slavery.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 271-0, FRENCH 272-0, or FRENCH 273-0, or consent of instructor.

courses.title: The Novel in French
courses.description: Content varies; may include the novel of the ancien régime, the psychological novel, and the Bildungsroman in France.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 271-0, FRENCH 272-0, or FRENCH 273-0, or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Topics in Modern and Contemporary French Literature and Culture
courses.description: Transhistorical study of literary and other texts of the modern and contemporary periods (1800–present) with emphasis on their literary, historical, and political contexts. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 271-0, FRENCH 272-0, or FRENCH 273-0, or consent of instructor.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: From Modernism to Postmodernism
courses.description: Crises and reinventions of French prose from the modernist moment of the early 20th century to the ambiguities of "engaged" literature of the 1930s to postmodernism.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 271-0, FRENCH 272-0, or FRENCH 273-0, or consent of instructor.

courses.title: African Literatures and Cultures
courses.description: Major issues, trends, and authors from francophone Africa. Content varies; may include Shahrazade, narratives of gender relations, law and literature, violence, and writing.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 271-0, FRENCH 272-0, or FRENCH 273-0, or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Caribbean Literatures and Cultures
courses.description: Major issues, trends, and authors from the francophone Caribbean and its diasporas. Content varies; may include Caribbean women writers; slavery, history, and memory; Caribbean identities.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 271-0, FRENCH 272-0, or FRENCH 273-0, or consent of instructor.

courses.title: The Maghreb and the Middle East
courses.description: Major issues in the literatures and cultures of North Africa and the Middle East. Content varies. May include exile in writing; politics of language and translation.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 271-0, FRENCH 272-0, or FRENCH 273-0, or consent of instructor.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: France and East Asia
courses.description: Interdisciplinary approaches to the history of French-East Asian relations, including French representations of East Asia. May include translation, japonisme, cinema, literary and philosophical avant-gardes, and culture and globalization.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 271-0, FRENCH 272-0, or FRENCH 273-0, or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Transnational Francophone Studies
courses.description: Exploration of cultural production in various genres from the French-speaking world, with an emphasis on themes, ideas, and/or forms that traverse national and/or cultural boundaries.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 271-0, FRENCH 272-0, or FRENCH 273-0, or consent of instructor. May be repeated for credit when content changes.

prereqSegments.value: , or consent of instructor. May be repeated for credit when content changes.

courses.title: Giants, Cannibals, and Critique
courses.description: Analysis of works of Rabelais and Montaigne and their techniques of satire and social critique. Readings include related selections from Erasmus, More, La Boétie, and others.

courses.title: Proust
courses.description: Introduces the works of Marcel Proust, a central figure of European literature and thought.

courses.title: French Film
courses.description: Topics in French cinema: for example, French classical cinema, the New Wave, postcolonial French film, the cinema of Marguerite Duras.

courses.title: Gender & Sexuality
courses.description: Major trends and perspectives in gender and sexuality studies such as first and second wave feminisms, lesbian writers, AIDS literature, queer theory, gender and orientalism, cross-cultural feminism.

courses.title: Contemporary Theory
courses.description: Introduction to some major trends in contemporary French theory and the way they have influenced literary studies in the United States.

courses.title: Topics in French Literature and Culture
courses.description: Advanced exploration of special topics in French studies. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: consent of instructor.

courses.title: Political & Social Thought in France
courses.description: Major political and social trends in France from the ancien régime to the 20th century. Content varies. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 271-0, FRENCH 272-0, or FRENCH 273-0, or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Women Writing in French
courses.description: Analysis of texts by women authors with regard to their respective social, cultural, political, and historical contexts.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 271-0, FRENCH 272-0, or FRENCH 273-0, or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Gender & Writing
courses.description: Issues of gender and sexuality in the production of literary and other creative texts in various historical periods. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 271-0, FRENCH 272-0, or FRENCH 273-0, or consent of instructor.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Topics in Literature and Culture
courses.description: Topics, issues, and questions in French and francophone culture. Content varies; may include French and francophone cinema, the intellectual in France. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 271-0, FRENCH 272-0, or FRENCH 273-0, or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Theory and Practice of Translation
courses.description: Intercultural communication through analysis of translation theories and translated works; translation exercises. Content varies; genres may include prose, poetry, graphic novels, and theater.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: FRENCH 301-0, FRENCH 302-0, study abroad, or consent of instructor.

prereqSegments.value: , study abroad, or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Foreign Language Teaching: Theory and Practice
courses.description: Theoretical foundation and practical applications of second-language acquisition and applied linguistics. Analysis and design of pedagogical materials. Self-reflection and analysis of teaching style and teaching philosophy.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: senior status or consent of instructor.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: senior status or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Advanced Studies in Culture and Thought
courses.description: Theoretical perspectives and paradigms for understanding culture through in-depth study of a historical, cultural, or theoretical issue or of a literary or artistic work. Independent term paper.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: senior status or consent of undergraduate advisor.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: senior status or consent of undergraduate advisor.

distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Independent Study
courses.description: Independent reading and research. Topics arranged through consultation with an instructor and approval of the department.

courses.title: Introduction to Global Health
courses.description: Introduces students to pressing disease and health care problems worldwide. The course identifies the main actors, institutions, practices, and forms of knowledge production characteristic of what we call "global health" today, and explores the factors that shape patterns and experiences of illness and healthcare across societies.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Beyond Porn: Sexuality, Health and Pleasure
courses.description: This lecture course goes beyond how sex and pleasure are depicted in pornography and popular culture, to equip students with information leading to more satisfying and healthy sexual experiences across their lifespan, regardless of how they identify, or who or what they like. The course familiarizes students with a wide spectrum of human identities, practices, and attitudes towards sex and sexuality. GBL_HLTH 221-0 and GNDR_ST 221-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: The Social Determinants of Health
courses.description: Advanced introduction to socioeconomic, political, and cultural determinants of health disparities between social groups and categories. Case studies from the United States, South Africa, and Brazil.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: (Re)mixing Qualitative Methods
courses.description: This course covers traditional and alternative data collection methods for public health research, with a focus on self-determination approaches used by Black/African American individuals. It promotes inclusivity in data collection and explores how individuals living in Black bodies navigate life and exercise agency in the face of systemic oppression.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Biomedicine and Culture
courses.description: Comparative exploration of biomedical cultures in different contexts, both in the U.S. and other countries. This course covers how biomedicine intersects with technology, consumerism, politics, economics, culture, power, and place. It further investigates the possibilities and limitations of biomedicine across the globe, particularly regarding how biomedical cultures affect and are affected by patients and health care workers.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: International Perspectives on Mental Health
courses.description: Cross-cultural and international perspectives on mental health issues and examination of the impact of psychological illness on the global burden of disease.

courses.title: Biomedicine and World History
courses.description: Course covers four centuries, focusing especially on the power of disease and the limits of global health governance. It explains how different medical professions became dominant, why drug industries secured monopolies, and what effects these changes had on other medical cultures. GBL_HLTH 309-0 and HISTORY 379-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Supervised Global Health Research
courses.description: Majors and minors are encouraged to do supervised public health research on campus and abroad. Students receive elective credit for this course only when taught abroad, however.

courses.title: Supervised Global Health Research
courses.description: Majors and minors are encouraged to do supervised public health research on campus and abroad. Students receive elective credit for this course only when taught abroad, however.

courses.title: Health Care Systems in Europe and the United States
courses.description: Provides students with an understanding of the various ways in which health care systems are organized in European countries, the problems they face, and the reforms implemented or proposed at the national and EU levels. Restricted to students in Northwestern's Paris program.

courses.title: Public Health in Europe: Issues and Policies
courses.description: Examines issues and debates on health policy in France and the EU, including primary health issues, health insurance, health inequalities, HIV/AIDS, SARS, elderly care, and genetically modified organisms. Lectures are supplemented by visits to relevant sites. Restricted to students in Northwestern's Paris program.

courses.title: International Organizations and Health: A Research Seminar
courses.description: Students design team research projects, learn about research methodology, discuss their research progress, and present findings. Restricted to students in Northwestern's Paris program.

courses.title: Health and Community Development in South Africa
courses.description: Health-related issues confronting South Africa, their social and economic impact, efforts to address them. Apartheid and post-transition policies. Demographics, prevention, and treatment of both infectious and chronic non-communicable diseases. Restricted to students in Northwestern's South Africa program.

courses.title: Public Health in South Africa
courses.description: Context of and responses to public health issues in South Africa, including HIV/AIDS, tuberculosis, malnutrition and poverty, psychosocial rehabilitation, and environmental and occupational health. Lectures are supplemented by visits to relevant sites. Restricted to students in Northwestern's South Africa program.

courses.title: Development Perspectives on Health in South Africa Through Community Engagement
courses.description: Reflection on service-learning experiences at community organizations in relation to theories of international development and global health. Focus on how health-related issues, including HIV/ AIDS, malnutrition, poverty, the environment, occupational health, and gender, impact development. Restricted to students in Northwestern's South Africa program.

courses.title: Native American Health Research & Prevention
courses.description: This course introduces students to the benefits and barriers to various approaches meant to address negative health outcomes among Native American individuals, groups and communities. This course also demonstrates how harnessing positive social determinants of health can affect broader health status among Native Americans. GBL_HLTH 317-0 and NAIS 317-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Community-based Participatory Research Course
courses.description: Oftentimes we hear of research done on communities. Community-based participatory research (CBPR) is a research paradigm that challenge researchers to conducted research with communities. In this reading intense discussion-based course, we will learn the historical and theoretical foundations, and the key principles of CBPR.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Trauma and its Afterlives
courses.description: This course draws on perspectives from anthropology, related social scientific fields, and the humanities to provide a critical introduction to psychological trauma and its increasingly significant place in contemporary global health discourses and agendas.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Qualitative Research Methods in Global Health
courses.description: This course emphasizes research skills acquisition in a practical manner. Topics include how to design, conduct, analyze and write up qualitative ethnographic research. The course covers research methodologies, ethics and Institutional Review Boards, and ethnographic field methods such as participant observation, qualitative interviewing, life histories, focus groups, data coding and analysis. Students acquire research skills while receiving significant feedback throughout.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: War and Public Health
courses.description: Comparative overview of the impact of armed conflict on public health and healthcare delivery worldwide. Historical and contemporary case studies. Specific health needs of refugees and vulnerable populations.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Global Health from Policy to Practice
courses.description: Examines global health and development policy, from the politics of policy-making to the impacts of policy on global health practice and local realities. This course highlights the histories and material, political, and social realities of policy and its application to real-world contexts.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Volunteerism and the Ethics of Help
courses.description: Explores the ethics of altruism through discourses and practices making up volunteering, from perspectives of volunteers, hosts, and others promoting, complicating, or critiquing volunteerism. What motivates people to help at home vs. among strangers or in unfamiliar contexts? What are the implications for volunteers, and for communities and institutions where volunteering takes place? How can we do good better?
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci
Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: History of Reproductive Health
courses.description: This course concerns the history of human reproduction from multiple vantage points. Since reproduction is often about power, the course is framed around the distribution of power in matters of reproduction; as such we will pay particular attention during this class to struggles over matters of reproduction as we explore historical changes and continuities in reproduction globally since 1900.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Native Nations, Healthcare Systems, & U.S. Policy
courses.description: In what is currently the USA, healthcare for Native populations is often experienced as a tension between settler colonial domination and Native nations upholding their Indigenous sovereignty. This reading-intensive, discussion-based seminar provides students with a complex and in-depth understanding of the historical and contemporary policies and systems created for, by, and in collaboration with Native nations. GBL_HLTH 326-0 taught with NAIS 326-0; may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Infectious Disease Eradication
courses.description: Despite efforts across several diseases spanning decades and billions of dollars, global health actors have only been able to eradicate one infectious human disease: smallpox. Why? This course attempts to answer this question by examining failed and continuing disease eradication efforts through a multidisciplinary lens. Case studies include smallpox, malaria, polio, guinea worm, measles, and emerging infectious diseases.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Human Circulations and Human Values: Global Mobilities of People and Products
courses.description: Human beings and human products are on the move across the globe, shaped by inequities that drive poor health outcomes for many involved even as these movements should, ideally, provide benefits for most. Human migration, cadaver procuration, organ donation, cell extractions, surrogacy, and other topics will be examined in relation to concepts of equity, beneficence, human dignity and common good.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci
Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Public Health in China
courses.description: Examination of China's public health system. Focus on role of government, emerging environmental problems, food safety, and prevalent communicable and non-communicable diseases. Restricted to students in Northwestern's China program.

courses.title: Traditional Chinese Medicine
courses.description: Introduces traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) principles and methods as they relate to Chinese culture and philosophy, including TCM diagnostics and therapies. Compares and discusses integration of TCM and Western medicine. Restricted to students in Northwestern's China program.

courses.title: Hazard, Disaster and Society
courses.description: This course examines how socioeconomic and environmental factors work together to cause hazards and disasters in human society. In this course, we learn the main concepts about disasters, such as preparedness, vulnerability, resilience, response, mitigation, etc. We learn that a disaster does not have the same effect on everyone, and factors of social inequality such as race, ethnicity, class, and gender make people more vulnerable to the impacts of disasters. ENVR_POL 337-0 and GBL_HLTH 337-0 are taught together; students may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Environmental Justice
courses.description: This course examines how environmental problems reflect and exacerbate social inequality. In this course, we learn the definition of environmental (in)justice, the history of environmental justice and discuss examples of environmental justice. We will learn about environmental movements and local resistance to protect natural resources. ENVR_POL 338-0 and GBL_HLTH 338-0 are taught together; students may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Silent but Loud: Negotiating Health in a Cultural, Food, Poverty, Environ. Caste
courses.description: This course explores how (un)health as a language is connected to certain bodies. Students will analyze the origins of bio-measurements used to justify "Othering" (not fitting within the norms of a dominant social group) and learn to formulate counternarratives to dominant ideological language that silently forms stereotypes, controlling images, hypervisibility, and invisibility that echo loudly as contributing to health inequities. GBL_HLTH 339-0 and ENVR_POL 339-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Mental Health and the Arts
courses.description: This course explores the role of the arts in shaping experiences of mental health and illness around the world. We will consider forms of storytelling across eras and cultures, tracking shifts in perspectives on normality and their consequences. What role can the arts play in healing? Where is the line between positive and exploitative representation of trauma and mental illness?
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Biocultural Perspectives on Water Insecurity
courses.description: Explore many ways that water impacts humans around the world through the lens of anthropology, religion, biology, environment, and politics. Why do individual’s experiences with water differ? How does water insecurity manifest? How do we measure it? How do we solve it? ANTHRO 357-0 and GBL_HLTH 357-0 are taught together; students may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: College Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: First-Year Writing Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: Sexual Subjects: Introduction to Sexuality Studies
courses.description: Survey of sexuality studies across a range of disciplines. Introduction to major theoretical and methodological approaches. Epistemology, morphology, history, subjectivity/identity, race formation, gender, social organization, and regulation.

courses.title: Beyond Porn: Sexuality, Health and Pleasure
courses.description: This lecture course goes beyond how sex and pleasure are depicted in pornography and popular culture, to equip students with information leading to more satisfying and healthy sexual experiences across their lifespan, regardless of how they identify, or who or what they like. The course familiarizes students with a wide spectrum of human identities, practices, and attitudes towards sex and sexuality. GBL_HLTH 221-0 and GNDR_ST 221-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Traditions in Feminist Thought
courses.description: Introduction to milestone texts in European and American feminist thought, with particular attention to emerging arguments and strategies around issues of gender and sexuality.
distroTags: U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Gender, Sexuality, and Representation
courses.description: Representations in art and literature within their historical, social, and political contexts. Theories of spectatorship, resistance, and revision.

courses.title: Sexuality and Society
courses.description: Examination of the role of sexuality in the cultural, economic, political, and social organization of the United States. Sex work, sex tourism, sexual migration, LGBT social movements, and moral panics. SOCIOL 232-0 and GNDR_ST 232-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.

courses.title: Gender, Politics, and Philosophy
courses.description: Role of gender difference in the main political-philosophical traditions: social contract, liberalism, republicanism, socialism-Marxism, critical theory. Classics of feminist and political thought (Wollstonecraft, Mill, Taylor, Engels) and contemporary debates. PHIL 221-0 and GNDR_ST 233-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci
Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Language & Gender
courses.description: Exploration of socially significant differences in the language used by/about/to men and women, focusing on the role of language in constructing gender as part of local communities of practice. LING 223-0 and GNDR_ST 234-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.

courses.title: Beyond the Binary
courses.description: Exploration of transgender history, identity, and expression, with a focus on the intersection of gender and race.
distroTags: U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Gender Issues in Science and Health
courses.description: Aspects of gender in the cultures of science and medicine.

courses.title: Critical Fat Studies
courses.description: Explorations of the construction the body through gender, race, class, size, sexuality, ability and/or culture. Emphasis on science, health and/or medicine through the lens of cultural studies.

courses.title: Gender, Sexuality, and History
courses.description: Historical considerations of gender and/or sexuality. Topics may cover different historical time periods. Content varies by quarter; may be repeated for credit with different topics.

courses.title: Language & Sexuality
courses.description: The use of language to construct sexual identity/orientation, focusing on the language of and about members of the LGBTQ+ community. Topics include heteronormativity, identity labels and categories, gender vs. sex vs. sexuality, and cross-cultural sexual diversity. Taught with GNDR_ST 327-0; may not receive credit for both courses.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: consent of instructor.

courses.title: Sociology of Gender and Sexuality
courses.description: Examination of gender and sexuality issues integral to social reproduction and social change. Content varies by quarter; may be repeated for credit with different topics.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Gender, Sexuality, and Health
courses.description: Health-related topics concerning gender and/or sexuality. Topics include reproductive health, sexual health, HIV/AIDS, women's health movements, trans* health and activism and disability studies. Content varies by quarter; may be repeated for credit with different topics.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Gender, Sexuality, and the Law
courses.description: Examination of the changing role of law in governing gender and sexual relations in America. Legal definitions of gender and sexuality in the household, the marketplace, and the state. GNDR_ST 340-0 and LEGAL_ST 340-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.description: Studies of legal systems and public policy. Specific topics may include domestic violence and abortion legislation. Content varies by quarter; may be repeated for credit with different topics.

courses.title: Gender, Sexuality, and Political Theory
courses.description: Studies in political theory relating to gender and sexuality. Content varies by quarter; may be repeated for credit with different topics.

courses.description: Examinations of political participation and counter-public spheres informed by feminist activism and feminist and gender theory.

courses.title: Gender, Sexuality, and Literature
courses.description: Studies of literary texts in the context of gender theory, feminism, or sexuality studies. Content varies by quarter; may be repeated for credit with different topics.

courses.description: Studies in gender and/or sexuality in the context of theater and drama in or across historical periods. Content varies by quarter; may be repeated for credit with different topics.

courses.title: Postcolonial Studies and Gender and Sexuality
courses.description: Postcolonial approaches to gender and sexuality studies, including orientalism and diaspora theory. Content varies by quarter; may be repeated for credit with different topics.

courses.title: Gender, Sexuality, and Popular Culture
courses.description: Cultural studies perspective on selected topics in popular culture as they relate to gender and/or sexuality. Content varies by quarter; may be repeated for credit with different topics.

courses.title: Gender, Sexuality, and Film
courses.description: Representations of gender and sexuality in film and film theory. Content varies by quarter; may be repeated for credit with different topics.

courses.description: Theories concerning gender and sexuality in digital representations, particularly Internet related. Content varies by quarter; may be repeated for credit with different topics.

courses.description: Field research and practical work experience in activist organizations; biweekly meeting with the instructor and other interns for discussion of internship experiences and common readings.

courses.description: Literature and/or theory concerned primarily with the intersections of race and/or ethnicity and gender and sexuality. Content varies by quarter; may be repeated for credit with different topics.

courses.title: Accelerated Elementary Ancient and Biblical Greek
courses.description: This is the first in an accelerated two-quarter series designed to teach students to read ancient Greek texts, from the biblical New Testament to Homeric poetry and Platonic philosophy. These two quarters will teach all the fundamentals of grammar and vocabulary and lead students directly into a course dedicated to reading the Greek New Testament, and classical Greek texts thereafter. Usually taught in the Winter.

courses.title: Coming of Age and Growing Old in the 21st Century
courses.description: This course introduces students to sociological and life course perspectives on adulthood and aging. It focuses on how sociohistorical context, social location or category, and early life conditions influence individuals' pathways to adulthood and old age.

courses.title: Special Topics in Human Development in Context
courses.description: Foundational work on special topics.

courses.title: Life 101: Literature and Psychology
courses.description: A conversation among disciplines, especially psychology and literary studies. Explores how people tell stories to develop a deeper and more multifaceted understanding of life narrative and human life. SLAVIC 262-0 and HDC 262-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Identity and Motivation
courses.description: Examines the connection between conceptions of the self and goal-oriented motivation, with particular attention to the influence of social, structural, and cultural forces.

courses.title: Emotional Mysteries
courses.description: What is an emotion? What happens in our bodies when an emotion is triggered? How can emotions help us live productive, healthy, and connected lives? And can we ever truly understand what somebody else is feeling? These are some of the mysteries that we will seek to unravel in this course.

courses.title: Team Dynamics
courses.description: In this course, we will explore team dynamics, those forces that influence a team's behavior and performance, and what can enhance or hinder potential for impact. We will analyze the contributors to team functioning and their interrelationships at multiple levels: intrapersonal, interpersonal, group and organizational. Taught with LOC 309-0; may not receive credit for both courses.

courses.title: The Art and Science of Aging
courses.description: What does it feel like to move through the adult years and toward "old age"? How do people's personalities, social relationships, and overall world view change as they grow older? What does psychological and social science have to say about general trends, as well as individual differences, in aging? This discussion-based and writing-intensive seminar is sequentially organized in terms of five cardinal themes: (1) the social/emotional world, (2) generations, (3) memory and the self, (4) loss, and (5) wisdom of the ages.

courses.title: Identities, Intersections, and Organizations
courses.description: Developing a robust understanding of how to identify and deconstruct the dynamic and varying role that identities play in organizations is key to understanding how/why organizations behave, whose needs they serve/who is left out or marginalized, and the possibility of/conditions for change. Combined with HDC 315-0; may not receive credit for both courses. Students may not enroll in they have received credit for HDC 351-0 or LOC 351-0.

courses.title: Adolescent Stress: Sources and Solutions
courses.description: Why are adolescence and early adulthood stressful periods of life? Are they more stressful now than in the past? How do we best define and measure adolescent and young adult stress? This course is an advanced, interactive, undergraduate class in which the instructor and students explore the set of above questions together, through readings, discussions, and through qualitative and quantitative coding and analysis of datasets on adolescent stress.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: (SESP 201-0) and (SESP 210-0 or any 200-level Statistics Equivalent).

courses.title: The Myths and Facts of Adolescence
courses.description: This course delves into the complex and often misunderstood stage of human development, where myths and realities intertwine to shape our understanding of youth while uncovering the scientific truths behind them. This course critically analyzes a broad spectrum of theories, research findings, and real-world applications related to adolescent development.

courses.title: Building Loving and Lasting Relationships: Marriage 101
courses.description: The intricacies and problems of close, committed, interpersonal relationships, especially marriage. Juniors and Seniors only.

courses.title: Mapping and Spatial Analysis for Social Issues
courses.description: The focus of the course is on using the power of geospatial analysis to identify, understand, and make recommendations about addressing social, racial, and other inequities, including access to healthy food, environmental pollution, policing, etc. Students will learn basic use of the ArcGIS online program to support geospatial analysis. Taught with LOC 347-0; may not receive credit for both courses.
