courses.description: A survey of the music industry as it pertains to jazz. Includes discussions on songwriting, music publishing, national and international copyright law, music licensing, artist management, music production, and related topics.

courses.title: Jazz Keyboard I
courses.description: Basic keyboard skills, with an emphasis on jazz voicing, harmonization, and analysis.

courses.title: Jazz Keyboard II
courses.description: Basic keyboard skills, with an emphasis on jazz voicing, harmonization, and analysis.

courses.title: Bridge Program
courses.description: This course will introduce you to the fundamentals of journalistic reporting and writing while simultaneously considering the role news plays in forming narratives around race and difference.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Reporting & Writing News
courses.description: This course emphasizes the critical practices of ethical journalism and deadline reporting and writing and builds a strong foundation for all Medill classes. It introduces students to the essentials of accurate journalism regardless of platform or storytelling format.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Video Journalism
courses.description: This course puts into practice the strong journalism concepts discussed in JOUR 202-0 and creates a base of fundamentals needed for effective, relevant and engaging storytelling for specific audiences using video as the centered platform.

courses.title: Journalism Values, Practice & Trends
courses.description: This course focuses on the evolving relationship between today’s news media landscape and its consumers. Students learn fundamental theories of human behavior and media consumption in order to understand new technologies, content opportunities, and relationship-building with journalism audiences.

courses.title: Journalism in Practice
courses.description: Practice of reporting, editing, and storytelling skills through topical writing and research assignments. Learning to develop diverse sources and incorporate audio, visual, and multimedia elements for news, magazine, and other audiences. Readings, discussion, and experiential opportunities.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: JOUR 201-1, JOUR 201-2 and Medill sophomore standing.

courses.title: Media History
courses.description: This course will explore how media history has influenced the world we live in today. Students will examine who has had the power and ability to tell the stories that shape people’s lives, who has consumed those stories, who has been affected by the stories and whose stories have been neglected or distorted.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: JOUR 201-1, JOUR 201-2 and Medill sophomore standing.

courses.title: Framed: Media and the Marginalized
courses.description: Through discussion of principles of media professionalism and ethics, and an examination of some of the hot topics featured in today’s headlines, this course will set a framework for recognizing and analyzing media narrative framing, as well as the representation of traditionally marginalized groups within that narrative frame. Ultimately, we will develop a deeper appreciation of media responsibility.

courses.title: Global Journalism History
courses.description: This course expands the understanding of journalism beyond your own country’s borders and experiences to examine the historical development, values and practices across regions throughout the world.

courses.title: JOURneys
courses.description: Students will explore the worldwide media landscape with these short-term, faculty-led programs designed to provide a window into global media outlets and fascinating international news stories. Includes required course travel.

courses.title: Audio Storytelling
courses.description: Learn how to gather sound, conduct interviews, script stories, and produce your final pieces. The class will focus on news spots, vox, and news features, but students will also get a brief introduction to podcasting and longer form audio work.

courses.title: Chicago Investigative Prep Course
courses.description: This course is the first part of the Medill Investigative Program, where students will work as a team on an in-depth investigative project over the course of two academic quarters. The program is based in Chicago and investigations are national, with travel possible. This course combines both undergraduate and graduate students; students will work together with Kari and media partners to report, write and produce stories for publication in professional outlets.

courses.title: Media Presentation: Newspaper/Online
courses.description: This course teaches the essentials of editing content for newspaper and for the Web. Students will learn how to edit for grammar and Associated Press style; achieve accuracy, clarity and objectivity; think critically and exercise news judgment; write headlines and captions for print and online; edit photos and graphics; and design pages for newspapers.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Medill sophomore standing.

courses.title: Editing & Producing: Magazine
courses.description: This course is a workshop, drawing on the experience of the best practitioners. It immerses students in a particular magazine, pushing as close as we can in a classroom to the real world experience of being on the job at a magazine. We focus on editing as “curation in the present tense” on a ten-week journey of discovery that centers on the enduring power of magazine-style journalism in a fast-changing domain.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Medill sophomore standing.

courses.title: Editing & Producing: Video
courses.description: This key Medill video class will teach students how to write and produce newscasts, segments and studio interviews in our state-of-the-art control room and studio. The fast-paced course will emphasize the conversational writing styles of broadcast and video productions while continuing to build the journalistic fundamentals of accurate and ethical reporting. You will use material from the day's events to write and video edit stories on deadline.

courses.title: Local & State Political Reporting
courses.description: Capitol Hill and the White House aren’t the only places to thrive as an ambitious political journalist. This class will focus on building practical skills to effectively cover local politics and hold public officials accountable by focusing on the local government of Washington, D.C.

courses.title: Journalism Research Methods
courses.description: The class is designed to accomplish three inter-related goals: 1) to introduce the basic process, concepts, and techniques of journalism research; (2) to provide a framework for exploring questions about journalism and its effects; and (3) to gain hands-on experience in the process of conducting empirical investigations. While no prior experience in research or statistics is required, the class will involve computing and analyzing basic descriptive statistics and regression models.

courses.title: Social Justice Reporting in Chicago
courses.description: his course will focus on social justice reporting through a partnership with the WTTW (PBS) editorial team and Prof. Ava Thompson Greenwell (former WTTW freelance correspondent). Weekly sessions will guide students in producing broadcast-quality stories that will appear online or on one the WTTW broadcasts: Black Voices, Latino Voices and/or Chicago Tonight.

courses.title: Entrepreneurial Approaches to Media Innovation
courses.description: In this course, students will study different approaches to media-focused innovative business. Students will examine media products and services, analyze the choices that are being made by media companies, tech entrepreneurs, and investors; and observe how the general public learns about these businesses. By application.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Medill sophomore standing.

courses.title: Storytelling: Interactive News
courses.description: This course explores how newsrooms are using technology in innovative ways to engage their audiences; and also examines how a digital news presentation evolves across various multimedia platforms. It builds on the foundations of audio, photo, and video storytelling and adds interactivity and engagement techniques -- enabling students to design dynamic, creative digital narratives for news and feature reporting.Prerequisite: JOUR 301-0 or JOUR 301-1.

courses.title: Storytelling: Magazine & Feature Writing
courses.description: This course introduces students to feature and magazine storytelling. Students will read and deconstruct an array of non-fiction stories and will report, write, re-write and edit short pieces and one long-form story. The course aims to inspire students to deliver memorable works of narrative, explanatory and service journalism, with particular emphasis on expanding your reporting methods and sharpening your prose for particular audiences.

courses.title: Storytelling: Video Reporting, Shooting, & Editing
courses.description: This is Medill’s core video reporting and field-producing class. In weekly assignments, students will practice a variety of research, writing and field reporting techniques that will prepare them for the challenges of creating video in a professional newsroom or as a freelancer.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: JOUR 301-0 or JOUR 301-1.

courses.title: Reporting & Producing Social Media Video
courses.description: As social media continues to dominate as a means of delivering news and entertainment, we’ll explore how major media organizations are crafting visual stories and delivering news in video on the various social platforms. We’ll look at how outlets like NPR, Planet Money, NowThis, The Washington Post, Wall Street Journal, New York Times and others reach audiences on apps like Instagram, TikTok, SnapChat, YouTube stories, etc. We’ll learn how they create what they do and develop skills to make similar work ourselves.

courses.title: Advanced Feature Writing
courses.description: This course is designed to extend the lessons of foundational classes in narrative structure, immersion reporting, feature storytelling, and magazine writing.

courses.title: Narrative Structure
courses.description: “Narrative Structure in Storytelling” is an immersive discussion-based course about the art of crafting stories for different media platforms with a pivot toward personal narrative creation in the second half of the quarter.

courses.title: Advanced Photojournalism
courses.description: Advanced Photojournalism is recommended for students who are already familiar with DSLR cameras and have some experience using Lightroom/Photoshop. This class will review basic DSLR usage, how to use Lightroom, composition, photojournalism ethics, but will primarily be a production and editing course centered around the creation of a story-driven character-based photo story.

courses.title: Social Justice Investigative Primer
courses.description: Over two quarters, students admitted to the Medill Investigative Lab will investigate one local, national or international issue, conduct interviews, probe critical data and trends and ultimately produce a groundbreaking investigative story from the ground up. Students will collaborate with each other, the instructor and potentially other professional investigative journalists to produce stories.

courses.title: Covering Asian American Stories
courses.description: This class is for anyone who wants to better understand the role of media and journalists covering communities of color. We will explore issues of identity, race and ethnicity, nationality and class. We will learn to report in and about communities with sensitivity, address cultural and language differences and practice journalism of empathy. We produce stories in a variety of media.

courses.title: Visual Storytelling for the Web
courses.description: Students in this course will learn how to use web technologies to tell good journalistic stories. They will study and critique work that showcases the possibilities and limitations of the web.

courses.title: Reading and Reporting LGBTQ Health
courses.description: In Reading and Reporting LGBTQ+ Health, Medill journalism students will learn how to explore, navigate and report on public health in Chicago by reading LGBTQ+ news media, learning queer writing methods, exploring the city, and doing critical media analysis. LGBTQ+ health will include studying medicine, journalism, science, art, culture, and literature.

courses.title: Sex and the American Empire
courses.description: This course will be an intensive study in understanding the relationship between American journalism and the U.S. military in creating an American empire. By focusing on how the U.S. military has segregated service members by race, sexuality, gender and gender identity—and on how on U.S. media has covered the military—students will study how identity roles have been formed by both the military and the media in American society.

courses.title: Coverage of Gender and Sexual Minorities
courses.description: Examination of social science research on LGBT communities and translating it for specialized and general audiences. Topics include research aims and limitations; reporting on underrepresented groups; finding fresh angles and credible sources; contextualizing stories of local, national, and international reach.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Sophomore standing.

courses.title: Bilingual Reporting in English and Spanish
courses.description: This is a bilingual course that explores the history, current state and future of the English, Spanish and bilingual audiences and the media outlets that seek to reach them. Students will get to know Chicago’s vibrant and diverse LatinX communities and practice research and interviewing, reflection and story production in Spanish and in English. Spanish proficiency required.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Medill sophomore standing.

courses.title: Chicago Investigative Project
courses.description: This course is part of the Medill Investigative Lab-Chicago for students in the MSJ program’s investigative specialization and BSJ students who have been selected based on applications. In this course students will delve deeply into the team investigation, working together on the group project and also producing their individual stories that are part of the package, to be completed by end of the quarter and published.

courses.title: Knight Lab: Studio
courses.description: Each quarter, multidisciplinary teams of students, faculty, and professionals come together to collaborate in the Knight Lab Studio to produce cutting-edge digital work, research, and thought, innovating across every part of the media-making process. By application.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Medill Sophomore Standing.

courses.title: Knight Lab: Artificial Intelligence in Media
courses.description: A mix of media- and computer science-focused students will explore innovative artificial intelligence ideas and solutions related to the creation, consumption and distribution of journalism.

courses.title: Journalism Residency: Argentina
courses.description: By application only.

courses.title: Journalism Residency: Argentina
courses.description: By application only.

courses.title: Journalism Residency
courses.description: By application only.

courses.title: Journalism Residency
courses.description: By application only.

courses.title: Politics, Media and The Republic
courses.description: This seminar will explore such themes as polarization, political image-making and advertising, voting rights and voter suppression, as well as campaign rhetoric and policy issues.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Medill sophomore standing.

courses.title: Dilemmas of American Power
courses.description: This course draws upon an engaging set of case studies and materials to chart one of the most intriguing stretches of international engagement in U.S. history. We will work collaboratively to understand why decisions were made, how policies were implemented and sold, and what it all may mean in the end.

courses.title: Sports and Society
courses.description: This course will focus on sports in an international context for students who wish to cover sports or study them. Topics will include a basic introduction to the definitions of ‘modern’ sport and modern human rights; the global sports media rights market; the work of international sport governing bodies, from the IOC to the CAS; gender and sex issues, particularly regarding women, intersex and transexual athletes; the legacy of colonialism on sports, particularly in Africa and Asia; “sportswashing” and the role of the Arab Gulf states in sponsoring and hosting international sports events.

courses.title: Sports Commentary
courses.description: Students will develop a distinctive voice that stands out from the cacophony of opinions in the sporting world, to create commentary that is informative, thought-provoking and entertaining and to adapt those messages for delivery across multiple media platforms. Students will learn to coalesce their observations, opinions and experiences into compelling arguments.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Medill junior standing.

courses.title: Evolution of Sports Media
courses.description: From the foundational elements in the days of typewriters and telegraphs that are still in use today to the technological innovations that will shape the way we watch sports in the future, Evolution of Sports Media examines the progress of this multibillion-dollar field. We will examine the media successes and failures that brought us to this point where sports is the most consumed content on television and Twitter.

courses.title: Intro to Sports Writing
courses.description: This course is a multi-faceted look into an industry that is ever-changing, always challenging but continues to serve an eager and discerning audience. We will examine both the industry and the craft; the biggest stories and most compelling issues and we will learn how the best reporters are not coincidentally the best writers.

courses.title: Journalism Residency: South Africa
courses.description: By application only.

courses.title: Journalism Residency: South Africa
courses.description: By application only.

courses.title: Native American Environmental Issues and the Media
courses.description: This course introduces students to indigenous issues, such as treaty-based hunting, fishing, and gathering rights; air and water quality issues; mining; land-to-trust issues; and sacred sites. We will focus on how the media cover these issues and how that coverage contributes to the formation of public opinion and public policy.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Medill sophomore standing.

courses.title: Documentary
courses.description: This course will provide students with a comprehensive overview of HD video production specifically geared towards producing short documentaries that tell human stories. Emphasis is put on the use of character, conflict, drama and surprise in telling these documentary stories. Students will learn documentary production with a journalism focus: reporting, camera technique, lighting and sound recording in the field.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: JOUR 301-0 or JOUR 301-1.

courses.title: Media Law & Ethics
courses.description: This course will acquaint you with the ethical and legal principles that govern journalism and all communications media, and provide an opportunity to come to grips with those principles, using reasoning, analysis, critical thinking, and precise expression.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: Medill sophomore standing.

courses.title: Journalism of Empathy
courses.description: Exploration of writing and reporting about people and places neglected and misunderstood by mainstream America.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: JOUR 301-0 or JOUR 301-1.

courses.title: International Journalism: South Africa
courses.description: This course covers contemporary history in South Africa with a special focus on the role of media. It provides an opportunity to hear from African journalists working in one of the world’s newest democracies and to compare and contrast their experience with the increasingly contested role of reporters in the U.S., the world’s oldest constitutional republic. Required for South Africa Journalism Residency.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: JOUR 301-0 or JOUR 301-1.

courses.title: Medill Investigative Lab
courses.description: In this dynamic course grounded in real-time investigative reporting, students will expose a problem of significant national or international importance and chronicle its effects on the lives and livelihoods of those most impacted, particularly those in disenfranchised communities.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Medill junior standing and instructor consent.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: Medill junior standing and instructor consent.

courses.title: Techniques of Investigative Journalism
courses.description: In this course, you’ll examine the issues, ethics and challenges of investigative journalism while you partner with a working journalist to learn how to use reporting tools that will strengthen any type of story you pursue, as well as prepare you to take advanced investigative reporting courses if you choose.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: JOUR 301-0.

courses.title: Media Design
courses.description: In this course students will be introduced to creating and developing a visual response to communication problems, including understanding of hierarchy, typography, aesthetics, composition and the construction of meaningful images.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Medill sophomore standing and JOUR 301-0 or JOUR 301-1.

courses.title: Introduction to Data Journalism
courses.description: Delve into the dynamic world of data journalism with this introductory course, designed to equip you with essential tools and techniques for uncovering and telling compelling data-driven stories. This course builds on foundational knowledge to enable you to create impactful narratives through the practical application of data journalism methodologies.

courses.title: Introduction to Photojournalism
courses.description: This class will provide students with an introduction to photojournalism and digital photography. Emphasis will be put on using images to portray human stories – ones that surprise, break stereotypes, and capture emotion in dramatic and intimate situations. This class will also focus on basic picture editing and how proper picture selection enhances storytelling. Students will gain an understanding of the ethical responsibility and social value of being a photojournalist.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Medill sophomore standing.

courses.title: Storytelling: Mastering the Data Narrative
courses.description: In this course, you will build on your foundational data journalism skills to master the art of finding, acquiring, analyzing, and presenting data with a focus on enterprise reporting.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: JOUR 377-0.

courses.title: Advanced Data Journalism Techniques
courses.description: Unlock the full potential of data journalism with this advanced course designed to enhance your data analysis and reporting skills using powerful statistical computing and graphics languages such as R and Python. This course delves deep into the journalistic practices of data sourcing, analysis, and interpretation, emphasizing the importance of audience and context in storytelling.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: JOUR 377-0, JOUR 379-0.

courses.title: Health and Science Reporting
courses.description: In this combination writing workshop and seminar, students will read some of the best science and health journalism; meet with expert scientists on campus; and meet the editors and writers from leading scientific journals and publications. Students will learn what makes good science writing, how to find sources, how to evaluate information and how to sort out science from pseudo-science.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Medill sophomore standing.

courses.title: Covering Washington, D.C. as a Mobile Journalist
courses.description: This class will give students hands-on experience covering the nation's capital as a mobile journalist on a beat, producing up-to-the-minute political stories for the Medill on the Hill website. The primary focus of the course, instant newsgathering and multi-media reporting, will enhance innovative story-telling and deadline skills.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: By application only.

courses.title: Internship
courses.description: Student-initiated internships in journalism. Supervised by Medill Career Services.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: Sophomore standing and consent of Medill Career Services.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisites: Sophomore standing and consent of Medill Career Services.

courses.title: Special Topics
courses.description: Specialized experimental courses offered from time to time by faculty. Topics may include journalism in a networked world and depth reporting using documents and databases.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: Vary depending on the course.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisites: Vary depending on the course.

courses.title: Global Journalism Residency
courses.description: Global Practicum programs are designed and facilitated by partner universities and program providers in the US and abroad in consultation with Northwestern and the Global Learning Office. This program is part of a custom agreement with API for Northwestern students in the Medill School of Journalism to participate in a full-time internship program and fulfill their journalism residency requirement.

courses.title: Global Journalism Residency
courses.description: Global Practicum programs are designed and facilitated by partner universities and program providers in the US and abroad in consultation with Northwestern and the Global Learning Office. This program is part of a custom agreement with API for Northwestern students in the Medill School of Journalism to participate in a full-time internship program and fulfill their journalism residency requirement.

courses.title: Undergraduate Independent Study
courses.description: Academic work sponsored and supervised by a faculty member working one-on-one with the student. By application.

courses.title: Experiential Learning
courses.description: Journalism Travel Program. By application Only.

courses.title: College Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: First-Year Writing Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: Jewish History I: 750-1492
courses.description: Surveys the development of Jewish culture and civilization in the medieval period, and traces the ways in which Jewish culture, thought, and socio-political life developed in dialogue with contemporaneous Christian and Islamic societies. Goals include grappling with how historians use primary documents to reconstruct Jewish history, and learning to read works of historical interpretation with a careful and critical eye. HISTORY 203-1 taught with JWSH_ST 203-1; may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Jewish History II: Early Modern, 1492-1789
courses.description: Jewish community's economic and cultural reshaping; legalized readmission of Jews to European cities and integration into European society. HISTORY 203-2 taught with JWSH_ST 203-2; may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Jewish History III: 1789-1948
courses.description: Plurality of models of integration, acculturation, and assimilation; multiple identities; split of traditional community; sociocultural behavior; political movements. HISTORY 203-3 taught with JWSH_ST 203-3; may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Jewish Studies: An Overview
courses.description: Introduction to the field of Jewish studies. Methodologies used to research and analyze Jewish culture, history, and religion.

courses.title: Introduction to Hebrew Bible
courses.description: Critical introduction to the Hebrew Bible: its history, content, and cultural influence, as well as the sociopolitical history of ancient Israel. RELIGION 220-0 and JWSH_ST 220-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Introduction to Judaism
courses.description: Main concepts in the theology of Judaism, main rituals and customs, and main institutions. RELIGION 230-0 and JWSH_ST 230-0 taught together; may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Imagining Modern Jewish Culture in Yiddish and German
courses.description: History and character of Yiddish and the development of modern German culture in general and German-Jewish culture in particular. Appreciation of the variety of "Judaisms" imagined and reimagined during modern European history. GERMAN 242-0 and JWSH_ST 242-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.

courses.title: Introduction to Yiddish Culture: Images of the Shtetl
courses.description: Analysis and discussion of the literary, visual, and filmic images of the communal life developed by Eastern European Jews and inseparably associated with them. GERMAN 266-0 and JWSH_ST 266-0 are taught together; students may receive credit for only one of these.

courses.title: Modern Hebrew Literature in Translation
courses.description: History of Hebrew literature.

courses.title: Modern Jewish Literature
courses.description: Modern European, American, and Israeli Jewish literature in historical context.

courses.title: Topics in Israel Studies
courses.description: Topics in Israel Studies: History, culture and society of the modern State of Israel. Content varies. May be repeated for credit with change of topic. Topics taught under this course number may be applied towards Weinberg College distribution requirement Area IV.

courses.title: Topics in Israel Studies
courses.description: Topics in Israel Studies History, culture and society of the modern State of Israel. Content varies. May be repeated for credit with change of topic. Topics taught under this course number may be applied towards Weinberg College distribution requirement Area V.

courses.title: Topics in Israel Studies
courses.description: Topics in Israel Studies: History, culture and society of the modern State of Israel. Content varies. May be repeated for credit with change of topic. Topics taught under this course number may be applied towards Weinberg College distribution requirement Area VI.

courses.title: Topics in Israel Studies
courses.description: Topics in Israel Studies: History, culture and society of the modern State of Israel. Content varies. Course number indicates distribution area towards which course counts. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.

courses.title: Christians and Jews
courses.description: Varieties of historical encounters between Jews and Christians. Origins of the "Jesus movement"; rabbinic attitudes toward Christianity; medieval polemic and engagement; the modern "Judeo-Christian tradition"; Christian Zionism and postwar ecumenicism. HISTORY 347-0 taught with JWSH_ST 347-0; may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Jews in Poland, Ukraine, and Russia
courses.description: Social, political, religious, and cultural interaction of Jews and Slavs over a millennium, 1250-1917. HISTORY 348-1 taught with JWSH_ST 348-1; may not receive credit for both.

courses.title: Jews in Poland, Ukraine, and Russia
courses.description: Jewish encounter with Marxism and communism; social, political, cultural, and artistic aspects of Jewish life; Soviet Jews and the Russian empire: patterns of survival, accommodation, and interaction, 1917-2014. HISTORY 348-2 taught with JWSH_ST 348-2; may not receive credit for both.

courses.title: The Holocaust
courses.description: Origins and development of the genocide of European Jewry during World War II. HISTORY 349-0, GERMAN 349-0, and JWSH_ST 349-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for more than one of these courses.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Representing the Holocaust in Literature and Film
courses.description: Analysis of artistic, ethical, and historical questions about representing the Holocaust in different genres.

courses.title: Yiddish Culture and the Holocaust
courses.description: Analysis of modern Yiddish literature before the Holocaust as well as literary work that emerged from Yiddish-speaking writers who survived the Second World War. GERMAN 366-0 and JWSH_ST 366-0 are taught together; may receive credit for only one course.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: None.

courses.title: Storytelling in American Jewish Literature
courses.description: Modern Jewish writers from diverse national and linguistic backgrounds who have reshaped the oral tradition in Judaism to their individual talents.

courses.title: Topics in Jewish Studies
courses.description: Addresses topics not covered by other course offerings. Content varies.

courses.title: Topics in Modern Jewish Culture
courses.description: Analysis of major texts and figures in 20th and 21st century Jewish literature, with attention to their cultural context and import.

courses.title: Independent Study
courses.description: For majors selected as candidates for departmental honors. Consent of department is required to enroll.

courses.title: Principles of Finance
courses.description: Foundation course for the certificate. Basic principles of finance, focusing on the effects of time and uncertainty on value. First half emphasizes valuation, including discounted cash flows, equity and debt valuation, the term structure of interest rates, portfolio theory, asset pricing, and efficient market theory. Second half examines firms' financing decisions, including capital budgeting, capital structure, and payout policy.

courses.title: Investments
courses.description: Active portfolio strategies in bonds and stocks, optimal portfolio selection from the perspective of individual and institutional investors, and the role of style and performance benchmarks in portfolio management. Performance evaluation, trading costs, and other special topics.

courses.title: Derivatives
courses.description: Use and pricing of forwards and futures, swaps, and options. Strategies for speculation and risk management, no-arbitrage pricing for forward contracts, binomial and Black-Scholes option pricing models, applications of pricing models in other contexts.

courses.title: Topics in Financial Economics
courses.description: In-depth examination of selected issues in finance.

courses.title: Pricing
courses.description: Comparison of the three main ways to set prices-haggling/negotiation, posted price, and auctions. How to choose the best method in a given situation. Customizing the price of the same product or service to different segments, using optimization models to set prices when volume is uncertain, pricing multiple products. Introduction to techniques for gathering information about buyer valuations and demands, including regression, conjoint analysis, and enterprise value creation.

courses.title: Operations and Supply Chain Management
courses.description: Management of business processes - i.e. a firm's recurring activities. Challenges facing operations managers; the language, concepts, insights, and tools needed to gain competitive advantage through operations and supply chains; different strategies for different processes and supply chain structures, and the operational capabilities allowing and supporting them.

courses.title: Topics in Managerial Analytics
courses.description: In-depth examination of selected issues in managerial analytics; topic varies each year.

courses.title: Competitive Strategy and Industrial Structure
courses.description: The course studies the determinants nature of competitive strategy in a variety of industry structures.

courses.title: Korean I
courses.description: First-quarter course of the beginning college-level sequence to develop basic four language skills in Korean.

courses.title: Korean I
courses.description: Second-quarter course of the beginning college-level sequence to develop basic four language skills in Korean.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: grade of at least C- in KOREAN 111-1 or equivalent.

courses.description: Third-quarter course of the beginning college-level sequence to develop basic four language skills in Korean.

courses.description: First-quarter course of the second-year intermediate-level Korean sequence to further develop four language skills in Korean.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: grade of at least C- in KOREAN 111-3 or equivalent.

courses.description: Second-quarter course of the second-year intermediate-level Korean sequence to further develop four language skills in Korean.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: grade of at least C- in KOREAN 121-1 or equivalent.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: grade of at least C- in

courses.title: Korean II
courses.description: Third-quarter course of the second-year intermediate-level Korean sequence to further develop four language skills in Korean.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: grade of at least C- in KOREAN 121-2 or equivalent.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: grade of at least C- in

courses.title: Korean II - Accelerated
courses.description: First-quarter course of the accelerated sequence for students with existing proficiency in speaking/listening skills (beginner-high/intermediate-low) and more limited reading/writing skills (beginner-low/mid) in Korean. It focuses on the development of students’ literacy skills to balance with their oral skills through various literacy and cultural activities. This course condenses the Korean 111-1, 111-2, and 111-3 sequence into one quarter.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Consent of the department.

courses.title: Korean II - Accelerated
courses.description: "Second-quarter course of the accelerated sequence for students with existing proficiency in speaking/listening skills (intermediate-low/mid) and more limited reading/writing skills (beginner-mid/high) in Korean. It focuses on the development of students’ literacy skills to balance with their oral skills through various literacy and cultural activities. This course condenses Korean 121-1 and part of the 121-2 sequence into one quarter.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Grade of at least C- in KOREAN 125-1 or equivalent.".

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: Grade of at least C- in

courses.title: Korean II - Accelerated
courses.description: Third-quarter course of the accelerated sequence for students with existing proficiency in speaking/listening skills (intermediate-mid) and more limited reading/writing skills (intermediate-low) in Korean. It focuses on the development of students’ literacy skills to balance with their oral skills through various literacy and cultural activities. This course condenses part of the Korean 121-2 and 121-3 sequence into one quarter.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Grade of at least C- in KOREAN 125-2 or equivalent.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: Grade of at least C- in

courses.title: Korean III
courses.description: First-quarter course of the intermediate-toward-advanced level sequence to further develop four language skills in Korean.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: grade of at least C- in KOREAN 121-3, or in KOREAN 125-2, or equivalent.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: grade of at least C- in

courses.title: Korean III
courses.description: Second-quarter course of the intermediate-toward-advanced level sequence to further develop four language skills in Korean.

courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: grade of at least C- in KOREAN 211-3 or equivalent.

courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: grade of at least C- in KOREAN 211-3 or equivalent.

courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: grade of at least C- in KOREAN 211-3 or equivalent.

courses.description: Classical Latin vocabulary, grammar, and syntax with graded readings for translation. Course one of three.

courses.title: Elementary Latin
courses.description: Classical Latin vocabulary, grammar, and syntax with graded readings for translation. Course two of three.

courses.title: Elementary Latin
courses.description: Classical Latin vocabulary, grammar, and syntax with graded readings for translation. Course three of three.
