Student Teaching in the Senior High School: Choral and Nonperformance
Student Teaching in the Elementary School: Instrumental
Student Teaching in the Middle School/Junior High School: Instrumental
Student Teaching in the Senior High School: Instrumental and Nonperformance
Selected Topics
courses.description: Topics vary; announced before registration. May be repeated.

courses.title: Native American Health Research & Prevention
courses.description: This course introduces students to the benefits and barriers to various approaches meant to address negative health outcomes among Native American individuals, groups and communities. This course also demonstrates how harnessing positive social determinants of health can affect broader health status among Native Americans. GBL_HLTH 317-0 and NAIS 317-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Native Nations, Healthcare Systems, & U.S. Policy
courses.description: In what is currently the USA, healthcare for Native populations is often experienced as a tension between settler colonial domination and Native nations upholding their Indigenous sovereignty. This reading-intensive, discussion-based seminar provides students with a complex and in-depth understanding of the historical and contemporary policies and systems created for, by, and in collaboration with Native nations. GBL_HLTH 326-0 taught with NAIS 326-0; may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: College Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: First-Year Writing Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: College and the Brain
courses.description: Introduction to the neurobiology of learning and memory with a particular focus on aspects of college life. May not receive credit for both NEUROSCI 102-0 and NEUROSCI 101-6 or NEUROSCI 101-7 if seminar topic was Your Brain on College.

courses.title: Cellular and Molecular Neuroscience
courses.description: Introduction to principles governing nervous system function at the cellular and molecular level. May not receive credit for both NEUROSCI 202-0 and BIOL_SCI 302-0.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: BIOL_SCI 201-0 (or NU Biology Placement Test) or BIOL_SCI 239-0.

courses.title: Systems and Behavioral Neuroscience
courses.description: Introduction to the organization and function of brain systems and their role in generating behavior.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: NEUROSCI 202-0 or NEUROSCI 311-0 or BIOL_SCI 302-0.

courses.title: Neurobiology of Biological Clocks and Sleep
courses.description: General properties of sleep and circadian rhythms; how sleep and the circadian clock regulate a number of diverse activities at the cell, organ, and organism levels.The importance of biological rhythms and sleep for human health and disease will be covered in the course.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: NEUROSCI 202-0 or NEUROSCI 311-0 or BIOL_SCI 302-0.

courses.title: Neurobiology of Learning and Memory
courses.description: This course examines how brain cells and neural circuits process experience to produce lasting changes in behavior. In depth discussion of original research findings, with a focus on the latest molecular, neural physiology, and behavioral studies.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: NEUROSCI 202-0 and NEUROSCI 206-0; or NEUROSCI 311-0 and NEUROSCI 206-0; or BIOL_SCI 302-0.

courses.title: Injury, Regeneration, and Recovery in the Nervous System
courses.description: Why are non-mammalian vertebrates better than mammals at recovering from injury? Learn to delve into the cellular, molecular, and neuronal mechanisms that are involved in spinal cord regeneration. Develop skills to dissect, organize, and compile information from different sources into a coherent and logical story.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: NEUROSCI 202-0 and NEUROSCI 206-0; or NEUROSCI 311-0 and NEUROSCI 206-0; or BIOL_SCI 302-0.

courses.title: Advanced Neurophysiology Laboratory
courses.description: Learn to record electrophysiological signals (action potentials and post synaptic potentials) from living neural systems using amplifiers and recording equipment commonly found in research labs around the world.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: NEUROSCI 202-0 and NEUROSCI 206-0; or NEUROSCI 311-0 and NEUROSCI 206-0; or BIOL_SCI 302-0; and consent of instructor.

courses.title: Neurogenetics of Behavior Laboratory
courses.description: Project-based laboratory investigating the genetic basis of behavior in a simple model system; molecular genetic techniques used in neurobiology.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: NEUROSCI 202-0 and NEUROSCI 206-0; or NEUROSCI 311-0 and NEUROSCI 206-0; or BIOL_SCI 302-0.

courses.title: Neuroanatomy Laboratory
courses.description: Comparative anatomy and dissection to understand the functions of brain regions by comparing their structures across the major vertebrate classes. Includes clinical anatomy and case studies to understand the functions of brain regions by drawing connections between neurological symptoms and the localization of lesions.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: NEUROSCI 202-0 and NEUROSCI 206-0; or NEUROSCI 311-0 and NEUROSCI 206-0; or BIOL_SCI 302-0.

courses.title: Neurotoxicology Laboratory
courses.description: Gain practical lab experience on how to conduct studies using the scientific method, perform experiments to quantify behaviors and monitor neural activity, analyze di erent types of data, and improve communication skills. Come satisfy your curiosity on the connection between commonly used chemicals, neurons, and behavior.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: NEUROSCI 202-0 and NEUROSCI 206-0; or NEUROSCI 311-0 and NEUROSCI 206-0; or BIOL_SCI 302-0.

courses.title: Topics in Neuroscience
courses.description: Special Topics in Neuroscience. Topics to be announced. Prerequisites vary. May be repeated for credit with different topic. May be used to fulfill a Neuroscience Group B elective or serve as a Biology Allied Field course.

courses.title: Senior Thesis Seminar
courses.description: Instruction in writing a scientific thesis, discussion of student projects, instructor and peer feedback on thesis drafts, and continued independent research. Required of seniors pursuing departmental honors, with approval of the director of undergraduate studies.Prerequisites: Students must be a neuroscience major and have departmental consent to take the course.

courses.title: Independent Study in Neuroscience
courses.description: Supervised laboratory or methods research with a faculty member. Research must be related to Neuroscience.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: The research must be neuroscience based and only neuroscience majors may take this course.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: The research must be neuroscience based and only neuroscience majors may take this course.

courses.title: Introduction to Programming for Big Data
courses.description: The skills needed to go from data to knowledge and application, which go under the name of Data Science, are in big demand in industry, government, and academia. This course provides an introduction to the foundational skills needed by data scientists. Prior knowledge of programming is not needed.

courses.title: Project for Introduction to Programming for Big Data
courses.description: The skills needed to go from data to knowledge and application, which go under the name of Data Science, are in big demand in industry, government, and academia. This course provides an opportunity to develop programming skills by working on a data centered project.

courses.title: Metropolitan Chicago Data-science Corps Practicum
courses.description: Students will be assigned to teams to develop and create solutions for community partners/stakeholders with data needs. Students will learn to appropriately scope and solve problems for real stakeholders. Students should expect to spend significant amounts of time coordinating and working with team mates outside of class.

courses.title: Modes of Performance
courses.description: Introduction to performance as a concept, embodied practice, and as a critical methodology. We will cover the various ways performance has been conceived over time; various approaches to acting; various performance styles; and various ways of using performance to analyze literary and non-literary texts. Students will do both solo and group performances each week and write critical essays on performance.

courses.title: Analysis and Performance of Text
courses.description: This course studies texts, broadly defined and situated, through performance. Students explore how performance helps the performer and audience interpret a text, as well as express challenging political and personal themes. They examine the ways performance illuminates and draws out deeper understandings of social relations through embodied praxis.

courses.title: Production Laboratory
courses.description: Registration for performance studies majors fulfilling production crew requirements. Students perform duties for run crews and house crews in connection with department-sponsored productions in the Krause Performance Lab of Annie May Swift Hall.

courses.title: Introduction to Performance Studies
courses.description: Explores fundamental themes and debates that animate the field, introducing a range of ways of theorizing, conceptualizing, studying, and making performance.

courses.title: Performance Culture and Communication
courses.description: Explores how live performance and dramatic forms of communication are methods used to examine social behavior and cultural expressions.

courses.title: Performance of Poetry
courses.description: Introduction to the analysis and performance of poetry.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PERF_ST 103-0 or equivalent.

courses.title: Performance of Narrative Fiction
courses.description: Introduction to the study of narrative performance.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PERF_ST 103-0 or equivalent.

courses.title: Sound Cultures
courses.description: Introduction to ways of thinking culturally and historically about sound and listening. Students learn to describe, contrast, and analyze sound cultures over a wide geographical and chronological range.

courses.title: Black Music Studies
courses.description: This course introduces students to specific topics in the field of Black Music Studies that range from American gospel music and jazz to the question of how Black sound and performance gestate in everyday life, locally and transnationally. Students will engage with the complex socio-political dynamics that have historically shaped music in Black spaces as well as the role of Black and Afro-diasporic musical practices in shaping sono-musical cultures worldwide. No prerequisites. Specific topics and approaches to be determined by individual instructor.
distroTags: U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Food and Performance
courses.description: A critical engagement with food as a performance medium. This course explores food in its embodied, material, symbolic and evocative potential in historical, fictional, ethnographic and journalistic writings, as well as poetry, memoirs, cookbooks, selected films, plays, performances, music, visual and performance art.

courses.title: Topics in Performance Studies
courses.description: Readings, discussion, and creative work in performance studies research and artistic practice. Topics vary. May be repeated for credit.

courses.title: Movement Based Performance
courses.description: Movement laboratory exploring theories and techniques of movement for performance, including dance, physical theatre, and framed quotidian action. Introduction to leading practitioners and practices in movement training, choreography, and composition.

courses.title: Performance and Activism in Digital Culture
courses.description: Exploration of the intersection between performance and digital media as tools for activism. Includes practices of hacktivism, counter-surveillance, locative media activism, and networked protest.

courses.title: Art and Performance in Asian America
courses.description: Introduction to the arts and performance of Asian America, including popular culture, performance art, theatre, and dance. Overview of current practices in Asian American aesthetic criticism.

courses.title: Transnational Flows of Performance
courses.description: Exploration of how transnationalism and globalization challenge the concept of modern nation-states as bounded territories, identities, and cultures by considering how social actors negotiate these processes through performance as an embodied, in situ-cultural practice.

courses.title: Sonic Practices of the Middle East and North Africa
courses.description: Sonic and musical practices and ritual in the Middle East and North Africa in relation to modernity, transnationalism, political economy, and performance.

courses.title: Performance Theory
courses.description: Introduction to theoretical approaches that animate performance studies, including Marxism, psychoanalysis, deconstruction, postcolonial theory, critical race theory, feminist theory, and queer theory.

courses.title: Performance and Race
courses.description: An exploration of the field of performance studies as it interrogates the relationship between race and performance. Anchoring each class in the work of artists who engages the medium of performance, we explore the ways in which race is performed and performative. Performance works by black, brown, Asian, and indigenous artists will anchor understandings of race and racism.

courses.title: Performance in Latin America
courses.description: This course provides an overview of artistic, quotidian, and activist performance practices in Latin America. Students will engage performance practice as an object of study, analytic framework, and communication medium in order to develop their own work in dialogue with the course themes and contexts.

courses.title: Contemporary Middle Eastern Performance
courses.description: This seminar examines embodied cultural practices across the Middle East and North Africa, with particular attention to music, dance, theater, and popular culture. Spanning the late nineteenth century to the Arab Spring, students will better understand a cultural history of the region, its role in shaping global modernity, and the politics of gender, sexuality, and ethnoreligious difference.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Black Performance
courses.description: Exploration of black performance traditions; introduction to various schools of thought regarding black performance.

courses.title: Performing Africa
courses.description: This course invites students to imagine themselves as creators and curators, rather than as passive consumers or critics, of African images. We will engage storytelling, photography, devised theater, and movement as creative strategies with which to probe the idea of Africa; and explore historical and contemporary discourses that underpin ideas about Africa in the West.

courses.title: Yoga: Practice, History, and Politics
courses.description: This course combines yoga practice with critical inquiry to engage the global phenomenon of yoga as performance, industry, philosophy, and culturally contested zone. We will study the historical and social processes that shaped the historic transformation of yoga from a 5,000 year-old South Asian tradition into a modern cultural form.

courses.title: Documentary Theater and Performance
courses.description: This practice-based course focuses on the historical and theoretical foundations of documentary theatre and performance. Through case studies we will explore the poetics and politics of the genre. Students will devise their own documentary pieces based on interviews, personal narratives, and archival records of their choosing.

courses.title: Feminist Performance
courses.description: This seminar focuses on the analytics and practice of intersectional, queer, and trans-feminist performance through a selection of key scholarly, artistic, and activist works. Pursuing what a feminist performance practice is and what it does, we will develop critical readings and hands-on experiments that follow feminist strategies in response to issues like gender-based violence, dissident identities, and collective emancipation.

courses.title: Performing Masculinities
courses.description: This course will examine the ways in which masculinity is represented in popular culture, theoretical discourse, and live performance. Assuming that gender is made and not given, the course will challenge the assumption that only “men” are creators, performers, and producers of masculinity. We will also examine the ways in which race, class, and sexuality alter tropes of masculinity.

courses.title: Queer and Trans of Color Critique
courses.description: This class explores the development of queer of color and trans of color critique, their relationship to each other and to the realm of performance and performance studies. In addition to engaging with the traditions of queer and trans of color critique, the course will explore queer and trans of color performance practice as a site of theoretical praxis.

courses.title: Performance, Sex, and Censorship
courses.description: This seminar pursues the central issues that animated the “culture wars” in the United States since the 1980s, such as artistic expression, censorship, sex and sexuality, gender, race, reproductive choice, and religion. It focuses the history of performance, art, and censorship in the contemporary US, as well as the relevant first amendment law that accompanies much of this history.

courses.title: Museums and Cultural Collections
courses.description: A look at museums as sites and practices of performance. This course focuses on how museums have historically collected, exhibited, and programmed around objects, images, plants, and, at times, human and non-human animals. We will explore the history of collections in libraries, cultural centers, museums and other archives. The course includes significant site visits to Chicago museums and collections.

courses.title: Performing Popular Music
courses.description: This course approaches the study of popular music practices, discourses, and worldmaking from the perspective of performance studies, with its attendant focus on the role of embodiment, social and cultural difference, and practice-based research.

courses.title: Adaptation: Writing and Staging
courses.description: This is a course in the fundamentals of adapting an existing, non-dramatic text (such as a novel, short story, memoir, non-fiction of all kinds, poetry, etc.) into a theatrical event. We will focus on all the issues involved in moving a story from one mode of address to another: direct and indirect discourse, voice, the implied author, compression, how time works differently for the reader vs. the spectator, and narrative and what to do about it; as well as replacing text with image, inventing dialogue, and staging the impossible. Some texts will be assigned, but primarily students will work with print texts of their own choosing. Students will have many small exercises in both writing and staging; and work towards a “big project” – the completion of a significant part of their own adaptation. The course could be of interest to anyone interested in directing or creating plays from non-dramatic source texts.

courses.title: Autobiographical Performance
courses.description: In this process-oriented class, students will spend ten weeks analyzing a variety of autobiographical performances and developing solo performance pieces. Students will learn how to create a performance and move from the personal to the universal by pulling inspiration from personal experiences, family archives, and canonical performance art pieces. Students will also learn and practice methods for giving and receiving thoughtful and consensual critical feedback.

courses.title: Performance and Technology: Composition Workshop
courses.description: In this course students will use basic mechatronics to create compelling movement-based performances. The course will involve workshop exploration of technologies embedded in performance: robots, media, computer interface. Students will create performance projects and discuss theoretical and historical implications of technologies in performance. Hands-on making and engineering workshops will be incorporated to develop skills in technological crafts such as circuit design and fabrication, toward technologically enhanced performance.

courses.title: Independent Study
courses.description: Prerequisite: consent of undergraduate dean after submission of petition.

courses.title: College Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: First-Year Writing Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: Introduction to Philosophy
courses.description: Fundamental problems and methods of philosophy.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Elementary Logic I
courses.description: Introduction to the philosophical study of logical entailment and deduction. Systems of classical propositional and predicate logic; and their bearing on philosophical quandaries. First quarter of PHIL 150-0/PHIL 250-0/PHIL 350-0 sequence.
distroTags: Empirical and Deductive Reasoning Foundational Dis

courses.title: Scientific Reasoning
courses.description: Introduction to probabilistic calculus and its role in science. Topics may include Bayes's theorem, the Dutch Book theorem, hypothesis and confirmation, problems of induction, subjective and objective interpretations of probability, causal reasoning.

courses.title: History of Philosophy - Ancient
courses.description: Classical readings from authors of the Western tradition of ancient philosophy, like Plato, Aristotle, Stoics, Epicureans, Cicero, Seneca.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: History of Philosophy - Medieval
courses.description: Classical readings from authors of the Western tradition of medieval philosophy.

courses.title: History of Philosophy - Early Modern
courses.description: Classical readings from authors of the Western tradition of the early modern age of enlightenment, like Hobbes, Locke, Spinoza, Leibniz, Hume.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Introduction to Pragmatism
courses.description: Introduction to classical and contemporary literature in pragmatist philosophy: Peirce, James, Mead, Dewey, and 20th century neopragmatist authors (Quine, Rorty, Putnam, et al.).
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Introduction to Existentialism
courses.description: Principal sources of existential philosophy: Kierkegaard, Jaspers, Marcel, Nietzsche, Sartre, Heidegger, Merleau-Ponty, and others.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Introduction to Critical Theory
courses.description: Crisis, criticism, and critique in philosophical, political, and cultural contexts. Focus on the philosophical aspects of critical theory with reference to social conditions and art, literary, and/or political forms. COMP_LIT 207-0 and PHIL 220-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci
Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Gender, Politics, & Philosophy
courses.description: Role of gender difference in the main political-philosophical traditions: social contract, liberalism, republicanism, socialism-Marxism, critical theory. The classics of feminist political philosophy (Wollstonecraft, Mill, Taylor, Engels), followed by contemporary debates. Taught with GNDR_ST 233-0; may not receive credit for both courses.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci
Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Introduction to Africana Philosophy
courses.description: Conceptual and historical approaches to philosophical questions, topics, and figures belonging to the African Diaspora including political thought, ethical thought, aesthetics, hermeneutics, philosophy of identity, and epistemologies.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci
Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Philosophy, Race, and Racism
courses.description: Introduction to philosophical discussions of race, race identity, and racism. Readings may be drawn from classical as well as contemporary sources.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Minds and Machines
courses.description: Introductory course addressing philosophical issues arising from the study of intelligence, including the possibility of machine intelligence and its relevance to the study of human intelligence.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Freedom and Responsibility
courses.description: Introduction to philosophy through an examination of major theories of freedom and responsibility, with attention to how these may be affected by the scientific worldview.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Elementary Logic II
courses.description: Introduction to variations of classical logic, and their application to philosophical topics, among them necessity and possibility, obligation and permission, knowledge and truth. Second quarter of PHIL 150-0/PHIL 250-0/PHIL 350-0 sequence.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PHIL 150-0.
distroTags: Empirical and Deductive Reasoning Foundational Dis

courses.title: Introduction to the Philosophy of Language
courses.description: Contemporary themes and theories in the philosophy of language. Topics may include context and semantics, the semantics-pragmatics boundary, implicature, reference, presupposition, speech acts, and the role of language in shaping conceptions of social and natural reality.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Introduction to Philosophy of the Natural Sciences
courses.description: Philosophical and methodological issues in the natural sciences, such as the discovery and testing of hypotheses, explanation, theory selection, the nature of scientific laws, causality, space and time, determinism.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Theory of Knowledge
courses.description: Basic philosophical questions about human knowledge, focusing on skepticism and competing theories of knowledge.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Philosophy of the Universe
courses.description: Methods for and approaches to philosophical inquiry about the ultimate nature of the universe. Topics include: emergence of the macroscopic world, laws of nature, the directionality of time, cosmological arguments for the existence of God, life, consciousness. No Prerequisites.

courses.title: Introduction to Metaphysics
courses.description: Introductory discussion of some debates in contemporary metaphysics. Possible topics include objectivity, time, universals, causations, possible worlds, and material constitution.

courses.title: Introduction to Moral Philosophy
courses.description: Overview of some of the main ideas and most influential writings of moral philosophy.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Introduction to Political Philosophy
courses.description: Overview of some of the main ideas and most influential writings of political philosophy.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Ethical Problems and Public Issues
courses.description: Analysis of such controversial issues as the death penalty, abortion, euthanasia, sexual morality, economic justice and welfare, pornography and censorship, discrimination and preferential treatment, the environment, and world hunger.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Philosophy of Religion
courses.description: Survey of the central issues in the philosophic analysis of religious experience and cultures: the existence of God or divine-like forces, creation, miracles, the claims of faith versus the claims of reason, sin, free will, immortality, revelation, harmony of the universe, community of humanity and universe. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Ethics and the Environment
courses.description: Topics include our relationship to the environment, the obligation to future generations, pollution and population control, food and energy production and distribution, species diversity, and the preservation of wilderness.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Bioethics
courses.description: Ethical analysis of a variety of issues such as the human genome project, genetic therapy, cloning and stem cell transplantation, human and animal research, reproductive technologies, and the allocation of resources.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: The Brady Scholars Program: The Moral Life
courses.description: Course asking questions that allow determining how we are to treat and interact with others.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: The Brady Scholars Program: The Good Life
courses.description: Course asking questions such as what it takes for a human life to go well, the nature of happiness, the role of pleasure, what makes lives meaningful, rich, flourishing, what human dignity consists in.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: The Brady Scholars Program: The Good Society
courses.description: Course investigating domestic and international questions, such as whether democracy is the most legitimate form of government, what obligations national states have to others, the role of peace, international inequality.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Climate Change and Sustainability: Ethical Dimensions
courses.description: Interdisciplinary analysis of ethical issues concerning climate change; resource use, conservation practices, and sustainability. ISEN 230-0 is taught with PHIL 275-0; students may not earn credit for both courses.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci
Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Introduction to the Philosophy of Art
courses.description: Introduction to major themes and theories in the philosophy of art, including questions concerning the nature of taste, beauty, art, and artistic creativity. May also be taught as an introduction to the philosophy and theory of one or several of the arts, e.g. philosophy of film, philosophy of figurative art, etc.

courses.title: Civic Reflections I
courses.description: Students in Brady Program meet four times a quarter to discuss short literature and visual art pieces and begin planning their senior project.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Junior Status in the Brady Scholars Program.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: Junior Status in the Brady Scholars Program.

courses.title: Civic Reflections II
courses.description: Students in Brady Program meet four times a quarter to discuss short literature and visual art pieces and continue planning their senior project.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: Junior Status in the Brady Scholars Program.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisites: Junior Status in the Brady Scholars Program.

courses.title: The Brady Scholars Program: The Civically Engaged Life
courses.description: Students in the Brady Scholars Program meet at least once each week, among themselves and with civic leaders, to plan their community service project, and to bring it to completion.

courses.title: The Brady Scholars Program: The Civically Engaged Life
Issues in Environmental Philosophy
courses.description: Interdisciplinary analysis of a contemporary issue, individual philosopher, or school of thought in environmental philosophy. PHIL 375-0 taught also as ISEN 375-0.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci
Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: HONKOL -- Advanced research methods in philosophy Honors Colloquium
courses.description: This course provides students as they design, research, and write their senior thesis with a group-research experience to refine their methods. Students will engage with each other’s research and the Honors Convener to improve research methods to arrive at professional philosophical work. Each student presents their research project, and upon completion receives critical discussion of their presentation of research.

courses.title: Senior Tutorial
courses.description: Senior thesis. Grade of K given in PHIL 398-1.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: consent of instructor.

courses.title: Senior Tutorial
courses.description: Senior thesis. Grade of K given in PHIL 398-1.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: consent of instructor.

courses.title: Independent Study

courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: Ideas of Physics
courses.description: Topics in modern physics. Content varies-for example, relativity, the physics of music, and the progress of physics through history. Requires only high school mathematics and is designed for non-science majors.

courses.title: General Physics ISP
courses.description: General physics course relying extensively on calculus. Similar to PHYSICS 135-1 but more advanced and intended for ISP students. A concurrent advanced calculus course, MATH 281-1 is offered by the mathematics department.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: first-year standing in ISP or consent of the department and concurrent enrollment in PHYSICS 126-1.

courses.description: Introductory physics laboratory for students taking PHYSICS 125-2 and PHYSICS 140-2, with which it must be taken concurrently.

courses.description: Introductory physics laboratory for students taking PHYSICS 125-3 and PHYSICS 140-3, with which it must be taken concurrently.

Students with credit for a quarter of PHYSICS 135 may not later receive credit for the comparable quarter of PHYSICS 130.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: algebra, trigonometry, and concurrent enrollment in PHYSICS 136-1.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisites: algebra, trigonometry, and concurrent enrollment in

courses.description: Algebra-based physics primarily for premedical students who do not need to take calculus-based physics. Topics covered are similar to those of PHYSICS 135-2. Students with credit for a quarter of PHYSICS 135 may not later receive credit for the comparable quarter of PHYSICS 130.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PHYSICS 130-1, PHYSICS 136-1, and concurrent enrollment in PHYSICS 136-2.

courses.title: College Physics
courses.description: Algebra-based physics primarily for premedical students who do not need to take calculus-based physics. Topics covered are similar to those of PHYSICS 135-3. Students with credit for a quarter of PHYSICS 135 may not later receive credit for the comparable quarter of PHYSICS 130.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PHYSICS 130-2, PHYSICS 136-2, and concurrent enrollment in PHYSICS 136-3.

courses.title: Peer-Guided Study Group: College Physics 1
courses.description: Peer-guided study group for students enrolled in PHYSICS 130-1. Meets weekly in small groups, along with a peer facilitator, to collaboratively review material, work through practice problems, and clarify course concepts. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U.

courses.title: Peer-Guided Study Group: College Physics 2
courses.description: Peer-guided study group for students enrolled in PHYSICS 130-2. Meets weekly in small groups, along with a peer facilitator, to collaboratively review material, work through practice problems, and clarify course concepts. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U.

courses.title: Peer-Guided Study Group: College Physics 3
courses.description: Peer-guided study group for students enrolled in PHYSICS 130-3. Meets weekly in small groups, along with a peer facilitator, to collaboratively review material, work through practice problems, and clarify course concepts. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U.

courses.title: General Physics
courses.description: Calculus-based physics for science and engineering majors and premedical students. Mechanics. Must be taken concurrently with PHYSICS 136-1.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: MATH 220-1, MATH 220-2, and MATH 230-1 (may be taken concurrently).

courses.title: General Physics
courses.description: Calculus-based physics for science and engineering majors and premedical students. Electricity and magnetism. Must be taken concurrently with PHYSICS 136-2.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PHYSICS 135-1, PHYSICS 136-1, MATH 230-1.

courses.title: General Physics
courses.description: Calculus-based physics for science and engineering majors and premedical students. Introduction to modern physics; wave phenomena. Must be taken concurrently with PHYSICS 136-3.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PHYSICS 135-2, PHYSICS 136-2, MATH 230-1.

courses.title: Peer-Guided Study Group: General Physics 1
courses.description: Peer-guided study group for students enrolled in PHYSICS 135-1. Meets weekly in small groups, along with a peer facilitator, to collaboratively review material, work through practice problems, and clarify course concepts. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U.

courses.title: Peer-Guided Study Group: General Physics 2
courses.description: Peer-guided study group for students enrolled in PHYSICS 135-2. Meets weekly in small groups, along with a peer facilitator, to collaboratively review material, work through practice problems, and clarify course concepts. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U.

courses.title: Peer-Guided Study Group: General Physics 3
courses.description: Peer-guided study group for students enrolled in PHYSICS 135-3. Meets weekly in small groups, along with a peer facilitator, to collaboratively review material, work through practice problems, and clarify course concepts. Enrollment optional. Graded S/U.

courses.title: General Physics Laboratory
courses.description: Introductory physics laboratory for students taking PHYSICS 130-1 or PHYSICS 135-1, with which it must be taken concurrently.

courses.title: General Physics Laboratory
courses.description: Introductory physics laboratory for students taking PHYSICS 130-2 or PHYSICS 135-2, with which it must be taken concurrently.

courses.title: General Physics Laboratory
courses.description: Introductory physics laboratory for students taking PHYSICS 130-3 or PHYSICS 135-3, with which it must be taken concurrently.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Physics
courses.description: Introduction to fundamental topics in classical mechanics for physics majors and minors and students with a strong interest in physics.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: MATH 220-1, MATH 220-2; concurrent enrollment in PHYSICS 126-1 and MATH 230-1.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Physics
courses.description: Introduction to fundamental topics in electricity and magnetism for physics majors and minors and students with a strong interest in physics.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PHYSICS 140-1, PHYSICS 126-1; concurrent enrollment in PHYSICS 126-2.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Physics
courses.description: Introduction to fundamental topics in wave phenomena and modern physics for physics majors and minors and students with a strong interest in physics.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PHYSICS 140-2, PHYSICS 126-2; concurrent enrollment in PHYSICS 126-3.

courses.title: Foundations of Modern Physics
courses.description: Principles of waves, probability, quantum theory, and selected topics from special relativity, statistical mechanics, optics, and atomic structure.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PHYSICS 135-1, PHYSICS 135-2, and PHYSICS 135-3 or equivalent; MATH 240-0 and MATH 250-0 or equivalents or concurrent enrollment.

courses.title: Mathematical Tools for the Physical Sciences
courses.description: Introduction to tools for solving physics problems, including integral calculus, complex numbers and complex algebra, matrices and vector spaces, differential equations, and Fourier analysis.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PHYSICS 135-1 and PHYSICS 135-2 or equivalent (concurrent enrollment in PHYSICS 135-2 is sufficient); MATH 230-1.

courses.title: Mathematical Tools for the Physical Sciences
courses.description: Introduction to tools for solving physics problems, including integral calculus, complex numbers and complex algebra, matrices and vector spaces, differential equations, and Fourier analysis.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PHYSICS 135-3 (or concurrent enrollment); PHYSICS 311-1 or equivalent.

courses.title: Scalar and Vector Field Methods in Physics
courses.description: Physically contextualized introduction to the field concept, the associated methods of calculus, and the solution of key physical partial differential equations. Three lectures and one discussion per week.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PHYSICS 311-1, PHYSICS 311-2, or MATH 230-1, MATH 230-2, MATH 240-0, and MATH 250-0, or equivalents.

courses.title: Classical Mech
courses.description: Introduction to classical mechanics and mathematical methods of physics. Newtonian mechanics, oscillations, the Lagrangian and Hamiltonian formalisms, central-force motion.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PHYSICS 135-1 or equivalent and MATH 230-2 and PHYSICS 311-1 and PHYSICS 311-2; or MATH 240-0, MATH 250-0; or equivalent.

courses.title: Classical Mechanics
courses.description: Introduction to classical mechanics and mathematical methods of physics. Motion in a non-inertial reference frame, kinematics of rigid modes, systems with many degrees of freedom.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PHYSICS 330-1 or equivalent.

courses.title: Statistical Mechanics
courses.description: Ideal gas, Boltzmann distribution, transport phenomena, fluctuation theory, Bose-Einstein and Fermi-Dirac statistics.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PHYSICS 135-1, PHYSICS 135-2, and PHYSICS 135-3; MATH 230-2 or equivalent.

courses.title: Advanced Electricity & Magnetism
courses.description: Electrostatics and magnetostatics, multipole expansion, solutions of Laplace's equation, images, analytic functions.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PHYSICS 135-1, PHYSICS 135-2, and PHYSICS 135-3 and MATH 230-2 and PHYSICS 311-1 and PHYSICS 311-2; or MATH 240-0, MATH 250-0; or equivalent.

courses.title: Advanced Electricity & Magnetism
courses.description: Maxwell's equations, electromagnetic equations, electromagnetic wave propagation and radiation, microwave cavities, diffraction.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PHYSICS 333-1, MATH 230-2, and PHYSICS 311-1 and PHYSICS 311-2; or MATH 240-0, MATH 250-0; or equivalent.

courses.title: Physics of Condensed Matter
courses.description: Emergent properties and collective descriptions when simple components of matter are combined into larger systems with varying degrees of order.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PHYSICS 339-1; PHYSICS 332-0 recommended.

courses.title: Quantum Mechanics
courses.description: Introduction to quantum theory. Applications to atomic and molecular systems. The harmonic oscillator, the one-electron atom, the hydrogen molecule, barrier penetration.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: second-year standing in ISP or PHYSICS 135-1, PHYSICS 135-2, and PHYSICS 135-3 or equivalent; PHYSICS 239-0; PHYSICS 330-1; PHYSICS 311-1 or MATH 240-0.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisites: second-year standing in ISP or

courses.title: Quantum Mechanics
courses.description: Introduction to quantum theory. Applications to atomic and molecular systems. The harmonic oscillator, the one-electron atom, the hydrogen molecule, barrier penetration.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PHYSICS 339-1, second-year standing in ISP or PHYSICS 311-2 or MATH 250-0, MATH 351-0.

courses.title: Particle and Nuclear Physics
courses.description: Nuclei and their constituents; nuclear models; alpha and beta decay; nuclear fission and fusion; the strong, electromagnetic, and weak interactions; and the fundamental particles and particle schemes.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PHYSICS 339-1 and PHYSICS 339-2.

courses.title: Introduction to General Relativity
courses.description: Review of special relativity and Newtonian gravity; curved space-time; geodesics and conservation laws; Schwarzschild geometry; tests of general relativity; black holes; linearized gravity and gravitational waves; and big bang cosmology.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PHYSICS 330-1 and PHYSICS 330-2 or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Introduction to Computational Physics
courses.description: Application of computing to physics: Monte Carlo simulation, numerical integration of equations of motion, discrete element methods in electromagnetism.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PHYSICS 135-1, PHYSICS 135-2, and PHYSICS 135-3 or equivalent; MATH 250-0 or equivalent (concurrent enrollment is sufficient); COMP_SCI 110-0 or equivalent prior programming experience.

courses.title: Optics Laboratory
courses.description: Optics/laser lab focusing on optical instruments widely used in medical/biological studies, including optical microscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, tumor detection in optical scattering, and optical fibers in endoscopes.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: consent of instructor.

courses.title: Electronics
courses.description: Introduction to modern electronics, construction of elementary analog and digital circuits.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PHYSICS 333-1 and PHYSICS 333-2 or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Advanced Physics Laboratory
courses.description: Modern experimental techniques and data analysis methods. Both classic and modern experiments in atomic/nuclear physics, electricity and magnetism, optics, condensed matter physics, and nonlinear dynamics. This laboratory emphasizes independent work. This course consists primarily of two four-hour sessions per week, which may have lecture and laboratory periods.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PHYSICS 239-0 or PHYSICS 339-1, and PHYSICS 333-1, or permission of instructor.

courses.title: Classical Optics and Special Relativity
courses.description: Advanced topics following from electrodynamics, including advanced classical optics, Fraunhofer and Fresnel diffraction, radiation from accelerated charges, wave guides and/or antennae, and special relativity, including dynamics.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PHYSICS 333-1 and PHYSICS 333-2.

courses.title: Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos
courses.description: This course covers the mathematics of nonlinear oscillations, fractal geometry, chaotic dynamics, the dynamics of complex systems, and physics applications of these ideas. Projects involving applications of nonlinear dynamics and chaos are integral to this course.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PHYSICS 330-1 and PHYSICS 330-2, and some familiarity with computer programming.

prereqSegments.value: , and some familiarity with computer programming.

courses.title: Independent Thesis Research
courses.description: Individual study under the direction of a faculty member. Open only to advanced students pursuing departmental honors.

courses.title: Independent Study
courses.description: Opportunity to study an advanced subject of interest under the individual direction of a faculty member. Open to all advanced students; consent of instructor required.

courses.title: Technique Class - First Year
courses.description: Introductory piano techniques for first year piano performance majors.

courses.title: Technique Class - Second Year
courses.description: Intermediate piano techniques for second year piano performance majors.

courses.title: Piano Repertoire I
courses.description: Analytical and historical study of piano solo and concerto repertoire from early keyboard literature to the present.

courses.title: Piano Repertoire II
courses.description: Analytical and historical study of piano solo and concerto repertoire from early keyboard literature to the present.

courses.title: Piano Repertoire III
courses.description: Analytical and historical study of piano solo and concerto repertoire from early keyboard literature to the present.

courses.title: Piano Pedagogy I
courses.description: Lecture/demonstration/laboratory course in piano teaching at all levels. Principles and techniques of group and individual instruction; survey of teaching materials. Seniors and graduate students.

courses.title: Piano Pedagogy II
courses.description: Lecture/demonstration/laboratory course in piano teaching at all levels. Principles and techniques of group and individual instruction; survey of teaching materials. Seniors and graduate students.

courses.title: Piano Pedagogy III
courses.description: Lecture/demonstration/laboratory course in piano teaching at all levels. Principles and techniques of group and individual instruction; survey of teaching materials. Seniors and graduate students.

courses.title: Collaborative Piano-Beginning I
courses.description: Piano students work with a singer and instrumentalist in the preparation and performance of mainstream recital repertoire.

courses.title: Collaborative Piano-Beginning II
courses.description: Piano students work with a singer and instrumentalist in the preparation and performance of mainstream recital repertoire.

courses.title: Collaborative Piano-Beginning III
courses.description: Piano students work with a singer and instrumentalist in the preparation and performance of mainstream recital repertoire.

courses.title: Duo Sonata Class
courses.description: Provides an opportunity for pianists and their vocal and/or instrumental partners to receive intensive coaching on works of their choice. A final performance is required.

courses.title: Selected Topics
courses.description: Topics vary; announced before registration. May be repeated.

courses.title: Repertoire Studies
courses.description: Topics vary by quarter.

courses.title: College Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: First-Year Writing Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: Introduction to Political Theory
courses.description: Examination of texts in political theory. Topics vary but often include justice, the Greek polis, the modern state, individualism, representative democracy.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Introduction to Empirical Methods in Political Science
courses.description: Tools political scientists use. How qualitative, quantitative, and experimental research designs help answer difficult descriptive and causal questions.
distroTags: Empirical and Deductive Reasoning Foundational Dis

courses.title: Introduction to Interpretive Methods in Political Science
courses.description: Philosophy of inquiry and interpretive research methods for students of political science. Examines diverse schools of thought on research methods and their relevance for research goals.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Evaluating Evidence
courses.description: Introduction to evaluation of qualitative and quantitative evidence across science, politics, society, health, education, and industry. POLI_SCI 212-0 and COG_SCI 202-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.
distroTags: Empirical and Deductive Reasoning Foundational Dis

courses.title: American Government and Politics
courses.description: The structure and process of American politics from competing perspectives. Analysis of representation, voting, interest groups, parties, leadership, and policymaking institutions. The gateway course for the American politics subfield.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Introduction to Law in the Political Arena
courses.description: Roles of law in society and politics. Police and prisons, law and social change, courts and politics, legal reasoning, Supreme Court decision making, judicial discretion, legal strategies for making change.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Introduction to International Relations
courses.description: Introduction to the major theories, concepts, and problems of contemporary international relations. Security, political economy, and cooperation.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Introduction to Comparative Politics
courses.description: Politics of developing and industrialized countries across the world. Major topics include states and state-making, democracy and dictatorship, conflict and violence, constitutions, civil society, and political economy.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Ancient Political Theory
courses.description: Political thought of Greece and Rome in historical context.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Subjects, Citizens, Revolutionaries: Early Modern Political Thought
courses.description: Political philosophers from the 16th, 17th, and 18th centuries. Topics include sources of power and their impact on justice, equality, and law. No prerequisites, but some knowledge of political theory is desirable.

courses.title: Modernity and Its Discontents
courses.description: Examination of late 19th and early 20th century social and political thought in the works of writers such as Marx, Weber, Mill, Kafka, Darwin, Nietzsche, Freud, and de Beauvoir. No prerequisites, but some knowledge of political theory is desirable.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Human Rights Between East and West
courses.description: In this course, students consider challenges leveled against the declared universalism of human rights. They assess these challenges from the perspective of two non-western traditions: Islam and Confucianism.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci
Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: American Political Thought
courses.description: Advanced introduction to the development of political thought in the United States from the revolutionaries to the 20th century pragmatists.

courses.title: Critical Theory and the Study of Politics
courses.description: Critical theory examines and contests hegemonic thinking about politics and envisages alternate worlds of political possibility. Note: students cannot receive credit for both POLI_SCI 308-0 (Evanston) and POLI_SCI 308-SA (study abroad).

courses.title: Critical Theory and the Study of Politics
courses.description: Critical theory examines and contests hegemonic thinking about politics and envisages alternate worlds of political possibility. This study abroad course is restricted to students in Northwestern's Paris program in critical theory, literature, and media.

courses.title: Political Theories of the Rule of Law
courses.description: Key documents and debates in the development of theories of law and jurisprudence. From Aeschylus to contemporary democratic and legal theories and major court cases on topics ranging from torture to Title IX. Taught with POLI_SCI 309-0 ; may not receive credit for both courses.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Statistical Research Methods
courses.description: Statistics and data science as applied in political science, including research design, data collection and handling, social scientific communication, and transparency practices.
distroTags: Empirical and Deductive Reasoning Foundational Dis

courses.title: Urban Politics
courses.description: Structure of local and regional political power and its relation to the social and economic structure of community.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Race and Public Policy
courses.description: Analysis of how diversity shapes policy in the United States and how policies contribute to racial and ethnic diversity. Immigration reform, school choice, residential segregation, and criminal justice.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Latino Politics
courses.description: Implications of Latino politics including contemporary social and political developments of Latino communities in the United States from a comparative urban framework. Focus on Mexican and Cuban Americans and Puerto Ricans. POLI_SCI 334-0 taught with LATINO 334-0; may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Political Psychology
courses.description: Examination of mental processes that underpin political judgments. Origins of political views, influence of parties and news media, decision-making heuristics and biases. Emphasis on ordinary citizens; some attention to elites.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Immigration Politics and Policy
courses.description: Introduction to immigration politics in the U.S. with a focus on policies, public opinion, participation, and mobilization. Emphasis on relationships between nativity, citizenship status, legal status, and race/ethnicity.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Gender and Politics
courses.description: This class investigates how gender shapes politics and policy and how these in turn shape gender with a focus on the United States in comparative and global context. It analyzes the gendered character of citizenship, political participation and representation, and social and economic rights. It aims to understand gendered politics from both "top down" and "bottom up" perspectives.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Labor in America
courses.description: This advanced seminar examines how capitalism, law, politics, public policy, and issues of race, ethnicity, and gender, have interacted to shape workers' rights and capacities to mobilize in collective action since the U.S. founding. Emphasis on employer power, legacies of slavery, labor's orientation toward immigration, rise and fall of unions, declining quality of low-wage work, emergence of new forms of labor organizing.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: International Relations Theory
courses.description: Examination of the changing role of law in governing gender and sexual relations in America. Legal definitions of gender and sexuality in the household, the marketplace, and the state. Taught with GNDR_ST 340-0 ; may not receive credit for both courses.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: International Political Economy
courses.description: Introduction to the politics of international economic relations. Roots and evolution of the international political economy. Politics of the international rules, institutions, and ideas governing trade, monetary and financial relations, development, and economic statecraft.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: POLI_SCI 240-0 or consent of instructor.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Politics of International Law
courses.description: Non-utopian political science analysis of how law is used to promote collective goals and regulate international relations.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: U.S. Foreign Policy
courses.description: How US foreign policy is formulated, executed, and contested. Topics include democracy promotion, nuclear proliferation, foreign aid, the rise of China, US-Russia relations, terrorism, humanitarian intervention, and cyberwarfare.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Globalization
courses.description: Political foundations of global markets for goods and services, production, finance, and workers. Implications of market globalization for national politics, institutions, and societies in Global Northern and Southern contexts.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: POLI_SCI 240-0 or equivalent or consent of instructor.

prereqSegments.value: or equivalent or consent of instructor.

distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: International Environmental Politics
courses.description: International cooperation and conflict resolution of global and transnational environmental problems such as climate change. Role of political, economic, and normative considerations in the formation of politically feasible solutions to international environmental problems.

courses.title: Social Movements
courses.description: Theory and case studies examining the processes shaping collective challenges to authority. Topics include causes and mechanics of mobilization, the contexts in which movements emerge, repression and violence, strategies, and determinants of movement outcomes.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Politics of the Middle East
courses.description: Survey of politics and political history of the Middle East and North Africa from World War I to the present. Topics include state building, authoritarianism, political economy, the Israeli-Palestinian conflict, and the causes, trajectories, and aftermath of the 2011 Arab uprisings.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Global Development
courses.description: Exploration of the economic and social changes constituting development, focusing on comparison between the historical experience in Europe and more recent processes in Africa, Asia, and Latin America. SOCIOL 317-0 and POLI_SCI 352-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Constitutional Challenges in Comparative Perspective
courses.description: Constitutional controversies and resolutions in liberal democracies. Constitutional traditions and governance, rule of law, legitimacy and authority in diverse societies, human rights, social transformation. POLI_SCI 356-0 and LEGAL_ST 356-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Contemporary South Africa: A Political Economy/Policy Perspective
courses.description: Analysis of the political outcomes of South Africa's transition to democracy, democratic consolidation, the state of the South African political economy, and major policy issues, such as gender equality and HIV/AIDS. Restricted to students in Northwestern's South Africa program.

courses.title: The Political Economy of the European Union
courses.description: The political production, structure, and regulation of economic activity in the EU. Restricted to students in Northwestern's Paris program.

courses.title: France: Politics, Culture, & Society
courses.description: Introduction to French politics in the framework of European integration. Covers French efforts to promote integration and France's role in the international system and adaptation to the EU. Restricted to students in Northwestern's Paris program.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Decision Making in the European Union
courses.description: Analysis, by lecture and simulation, of the EU's complicated institutional structure for political decision making. Restricted to students in Northwestern's Paris program.

courses.title: The Dynamics of Law Making in the European Union
courses.description: Examination of the dynamics of law making in the EU and conflict/balance between domestic and regional law. Restricted to students in Northwestern's Paris program.

courses.title: Political Economy of Development
courses.description: Major analytical perspectives of modern political economy seen through concrete problems of development and underdevelopment in the least developed countries.

courses.title: Politics of Post-Soviet Russia and Ukraine
courses.description: Analysis of Russia's and Ukraine's political and economic revolutions after the collapse of the Soviet Union. Examines key concepts in comparative politics, such as revolution, regime change, market formation, nationalism, and state building.

courses.title: Chinese Foreign Policy
courses.description: Basic dynamics of Chinese foreign policy toward a variety of countries and regions.

courses.title: Politics of Capitalism
courses.description: Effects of politics on the economy and vice versa, especially in advanced industrial economies. The welfare state, varieties of capitalism, and neoliberalism.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Civil Wars
courses.description: Focus on post-Cold War increase in civil wars, including causes and consequences of internal wars, and theories of conflict. Examines recent and contemporary civil wars to illustrate applications of theories and better understand current events.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Drugs and Politics
courses.description: This course studies the links between illegal drugs and politics. From the politics of local communities to international public policy, the production, distribution, and consumption of illicit drugs affect individual behavior, local and national institutions, markets, and international relations. The course focuses on North, Central, and South America.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: America and the World
courses.description: Key debates and developments in the history and politics of American foreign relations. Domestic politics and foreign policy, political culture, interventionism, legal globalization, international institutions.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Political Communication
courses.description: Nature and functions of communication within established political institutions; decision making strategies, deliberative discourse, and electoral campaigns; field study of advocacy and interest groups. POLI_SCI 380-0 and COMM_ST 380-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Religion, Law, & Politics: Politics of Religious Diversity
courses.description: This course teaches students to think critically, comparatively, and globally about the intersections of religion, law, and politics. It considers how religious, legal, and political traditions intersect and interact in modern states and societies. The course is organized around a set of legal cases and supporting materials in the Teaching Law and Religion Case Study Archive. RELIGION 382-0 taught with POLI_SCI 382-0; may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl
U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: War and Change in International Politics
courses.description: Historical and contemporary forms of international order. Western and non-Eurocentric systems; how international order emerges; whether the post-1945 order will change.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: International Responses to Mass Atrocities
courses.description: How the international community can respond to mass atrocities and human rights violations.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Negotiation and Conflict Resolution
courses.description: This highly interactive course explores the principles of negotiation and conflict resolution. Through in-class simulations, students learn about the substance and process of negotiations while crafting creative solutions. Along the way, students learn negotiations theory, including positional and principled negotiation, multi-party and cross-cultural negotiation, and hard bargaining. Applying course concepts, students assess real-world political negotiations from domestic and international politics.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: POLI_SCI 220-0 American Government and Politics, POLI_SCI 240-0 Introduction to International Relations, or POLI_SCI 250-0 Introduction to Comparative Politics.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Institutions and Society
courses.description: Institutions in a broad societal context. How institutional frameworks apply to government, family, education, and the environment; implications of institutions. POLI_SCI 388-0 and SOCIOL 288-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Understanding Genocide
courses.description: Key debates in the comparative study of genocide. Why genocide occurs, why people become killers, how these processes relate to each other. POLI_SCI 389-0 and SOCIOL 379-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.

courses.title: Political Research Seminar
courses.description: Required of all political science majors; ordinarily taken during junior or senior year.

courses.title: Senior Thesis Seminar
courses.description: Two consecutive quarters (fall and winter) during which students work on their senior theses.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: POLI_SCI 395-0 and admission to the honors program.

prereqSegments.value: and admission to the honors program.

courses.title: Senior Thesis Seminar
courses.description: Two consecutive quarters (fall and winter) during which students work on their senior theses.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: POLI_SCI 395-0 and admission to the honors program.

prereqSegments.value: and admission to the honors program.

courses.title: Independent Study
courses.description: Study and research projects carried out under faculty supervision. A written proposal, signed by the professor with whom the student will study, should be submitted to the director of undergraduate studies. Consent of department required.

courses.title: Elementary Polish
courses.description: First course in a three-quarter sequence in elementary Polish. Communicative approaches to reading, writing, listening, and speaking. No prerequisite.

courses.title: Elementary Polish
courses.description: Second course in a three-quarter sequence in elementary polish. Communicative approaches to reading, writing, listening, and speaking.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: POLISH 108-1 (or equivalent).

courses.title: Elementary Polish
courses.description: Third course in a three-quarter sequence in elementary Polish. Communicative approaches to reading, writing, listening, and speaking.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: POLISH 108-2 (or equivalent).

courses.title: Intermediate Polish: Language and Culture
courses.description: First course in a three-quarter sequence in intermediate Polish. Further development of reading, writing, speaking and listening proficiency through communicative approaches. Continued focus on Polish culture and society.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: POLISH 108-3 (or equivalent).

courses.title: Intermediate Polish: Language and Culture
courses.description: Second course in a three-quarter sequence in intermediate Polish. Further development of reading, writing, speaking, and listening proficiency through communicative approaches. Continued focus on topics in Polish culture and society.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: POLISH 208-1 (or equivalent).

courses.title: Intermediate Polish: Language and Culture
courses.description: Third course in a three-quarter sequence in intermediate Polish. Further development of reading, writing, speaking, and listening proficiency through communicative approaches. Continued focus on topics in Polish culture and society.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: POLISH 208-2 (or equivalent).

courses.title: Polish for Advanced and Native Speakers
courses.description: Polish for advanced speakers, including heritage speakers. Stress on advanced levels of reading and writing as well as speaking. Taught entirely in Polish. Content varies; may be repeated for credit.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: POLISH 208-3 (or equivalent).

courses.title: Polish for Advanced and Native Speakers
courses.description: Polish for advanced speakers, including heritage speakers. Stress on advanced levels of reading and writing as well as speaking. Taught entirely in Polish. Content varies; may be repeated for credit.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: POLISH 208-3 (or equivalent).

courses.title: Elementary Portuguese
courses.description: Introduction to grammar and development of listening, speaking, reading, and writing skills in Brazilian Portuguese, as well as the history and culture of Portuguese-speaking countries.

courses.title: Elementary Portuguese
courses.description: Introduction to grammar and development of listening, speaking, reading, and writing skills in Brazilian Portuguese, as well as the history and culture of Portuguese-speaking countries.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PORT 101-1 or sufficient score on placement test.

courses.title: College Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: First-Year Writing Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: Portuguese for Speakers of Spanish and other Romance Languages
courses.description: For students proficient in Spanish, French or Italian. Comparative sociolinguistic and interactive approach to communicative competence emphasizing pronunciation, intonation, sentence structure, and patterns of spoken and written Portuguese.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: AP 4 in Spanish, French or Italian; or equivalent on the Language Placement Exam.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: AP 4 in Spanish, French or Italian; or equivalent on the Language Placement Exam.

courses.title: Portuguese for Speakers of Spanish and other Romance Languages
courses.description: For students proficient in Spanish, French or Italian. Comparative sociolinguistic and interactive approach to communicative competence emphasizing pronunciation, intonation, sentence structure, and patterns of spoken and written Portuguese.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PORT 115-1.This course is equivalent to PORT 121-3.

courses.title: Intermediate Portuguese
courses.description: Port 121 helps students achieve an intermediate level of proficiency through further development of listening, speaking, reading and writing skills. Grammar, vocabulary and pronunciation of Brazilian Portuguese will continue to be developed through meaningful cultural contexts. The course also offers insights into the history and culture of the Portuguese speaking countries in Europe, Africa and America.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PORT 101-3 or placement test.

courses.title: Intermediate Portuguese
courses.description: Port 121 helps students achieve an intermediate level of proficiency through further development of listening, speaking, reading and writing skills. Grammar, vocabulary and pronunciation of Brazilian Portuguese will continue to be developed through meaningful cultural contexts. The course also offers insights into the history and culture of the Portuguese speaking countries in Europe, Africa and America.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PORT 121-1 or sufficient score on the Portuguese Language Placement Exam.

prereqSegments.value: or sufficient score on the Portuguese Language Placement Exam.
