courses.description: Port 121 helps students achieve an intermediate level of proficiency through further development of listening, speaking, reading and writing skills. Grammar, vocabulary and pronunciation of Brazilian Portuguese will continue to be developed through meaningful cultural contexts. The course also offers insights into the history and culture of the Portuguese speaking countries in Europe, Africa and America.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PORT 121-2 or Placement.

courses.title: Reading and Speaking Portuguese
courses.description: This intermediate course is designed to expand mastery in reading and speaking Brazilian Portuguese through select cultural videos, readings of literary cronicas, periodicals, and the Internet.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PORT 115-2, PORT 121-3, or sufficient score on placement examination.

prereqSegments.value: , or sufficient score on placement examination.

courses.title: Reading and Writing Portuguese
courses.description: Instruction in reading and writing expository and narrative prose. Emphasis on vocabulary, linguistic skills, and syntax appropriate to formal written Portuguese.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PORT 115-2, PORT 121-3, or sufficient score on placement examination.

prereqSegments.value: , or sufficient score on placement examination.

courses.title: Icons, Legends, and Myths in Brazil
courses.description: Representations in graphic materials, documentaries, film, theater, folklore, narrative fiction, and popular music of historical, literary, and popular figures in the national imagination. May include English or Portuguese discussion sections. Prerequisite for Portuguese section: PORT 201-0, PORT 202-0, or sufficient score on placement exam. Prerequisite for English section: none.

courses.title: Introductory Topics in Luso-Afro-Brazilian Studies
courses.description: Introduction to the histories and cultures of the Lusophone world (Brazil, Portugal, Lusophone Africa and/or Asia) through literature, graphic novels, documentaries, film, theater, folklore, and/or popular music. Content may span the precolonial/premodern, colonial, and postcolonial periods. May be repeated for credit with different topic.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: Topics in Advanced Portuguese
courses.description: Advanced review of grammar concepts and idiomatic use of spoken and written Portuguese. Deals with a variety of topics in the context of Brazilian and Lusophone culture, history, literature, and current events. May be taken more than once for credit with change of topic.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PORT 202-0 or equivalent.

courses.title: Independent Study
courses.description: Independent study under supervision. Consultation with the director of undergraduate studies required.

courses.title: McCormick First-Year Experience
courses.description: A series of peer-led small group discussions for first-year engineering students, covering topics like higher education culture, time management, strategies for success in courses, and mindset. Attendance is required.

courses.title: McCormick First-Year Experience
courses.description: A series of peer-led small group discussions for first-year engineering students covering topics such as academic strategies, choice of major, exploration of non-major options, and health and well-being. Attendance is required.

courses.title: PATH (Personal Development Studio Lab)
courses.description: PATH explores opportunities to improve performance on many different levels. Students will begin with a values assessment and set short term goals, then explore the role of attention, mindset, learning science, time management and self-compassion in achieving personal, academic, and future professional success. PATH is a prerequisite for the Curious Life Certificate.

courses.title: Designing Your Life
courses.description: Considers an approach to life as a series of design projects to help students craft a total life. Includes seminar-style discussions, role-playing, short writing assignments, hands-on making, guest speakers, and individual mentoring and coaching.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: Reserved for Juniors and Seniors.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisite: Reserved for Juniors and Seniors.

courses.title: Emotional Intelligence 101 - Managing Yourself, Maximizing Your Potential
courses.description: Introduction to emotional intelligence theories and concepts; provides practical tools for building skills in stress management, intrapersonal and interpersonal awareness, peak performance, resilience/adaptability, and general mood.

courses.title: Engineering Improv I: The Art of Allowing
courses.description: Start anywhere, remember you are not the most important person in the scene, and say yes. Through these and other improv techniques, students learn to tackle unexpected obstacles, building skills that can be leveraged in both academic and non-academic contexts to face challenges with resilience.

courses.title: Engineering Improv II: The Art of Application
courses.description: This course builds on Improv I and takes students deeper into concepts and applications.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PRDV 335-1 Engineering Improv I.

courses.title: Whole-Body Thinking: Collaborative Problem Solving through Partner Dancing
courses.description: In this approach to swing dancing, the goal is for two people to join hands and use the rhythms they hear in swinging, jazz-rhythm-based music as a means of connecting with each other.

courses.title: Yoga Foundations: Living a Life of Service, Discipline, and Joy
courses.description: Explore the intersection of yoga principles and personal development with topics such as living your dharma, the guiding principles of yoga, courage before confidence, and the discipline of joy. Learn how to live a more peaceful, meaningful, purpose-driven life based on the tenants of yoga. The course is part lecture, part physical practice.

courses.title: Special Topics in Personal Development
courses.description: Topics suggested by students or faculty and approved by the curriculum committee.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: consent of instructor.

courses.title: Topics in Personal Development
courses.description: Topics of limited scope as suggested by faculty or students and approved by the McCormick Office of Personal Development.

courses.title: Selected Topics in Personal Development
courses.description: Topics of limited scope as suggested by faculty or students and approved by the McCormick Curriculum Committee.

courses.title: College Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: First-Year Writing Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: Indigenous Summer Research Program
courses.description: The Indigenous Summer Research Program (ISRP) is an 8-week residential internship, training the next generation of social and cultural psychologists. Application is open to students with demonstrated prior commitment to Indigenous Studies. Participants work with RISE staff and faculty to develop a research project investigating Indigenous issues. At the program's conclusion, interns present their work at the ISRP Conference. Prerequiste: Admission to program.

courses.title: Introduction to Psychology
courses.description: A survey course reviewing primary psychological research and theories of human behavior. Laboratory experience exposes students to psychology as a research science.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Statistical Methods in Psychology
courses.description: Measurement; descriptive statistics; probability and sampling; T-test, ANOVA, correlation, and regression.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: some college mathematics recommended.
distroTags: Empirical and Deductive Reasoning Foundational Dis

courses.title: Social Psychology
courses.description: Psychological processes underlying social behavior; topics include social cognition, attraction, aggression, prejudice, and behavior in groups.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PSYCH 110-0.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Psychology of Personality
courses.description: Nature of personality and its development. Modern theoretical interpretations. Biological and social bases of individual differences.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PSYCH 110-0.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Introduction to Neuroscience
courses.description: Designed for students with no prior coursework in neuroscience or biology. Neurophysiology and neuroanatomy; neuroscience of perception, emotion, morality, memory, mental illness, and consciousness. Neuroscience majors may not take PSYCH 221-0. Students may not take PSYCH 221-0 after they have taken NEUROSCI 202-0 or NEUROSCI 206-0.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Cognitive Psychology
courses.description: Introduction to research into mental processes such as perception, memory, reasoning, and decision making.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PSYCH 110-0.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Developmental Psychology
courses.description: Development of cognitive, social, and other psychological functions.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PSYCH 110-0.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Health Psychology
courses.description: Overview of research in health psychology. Stress and coping, biological systems affected by stress, social support and health, health behaviors, adjustment to chronic illnesses.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PSYCH 110-0.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Buddhist Psychology
courses.description: Buddhist and scientific psychological views of mind and behavior; meditation techniques.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PSYCH 110-0.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Research Methods in Psychology
courses.description: Methods of psychological research; experimental design; reliability and validity; review and application of statistics; execution and reporting of psychological research.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PSYCH 201-0.
distroTags: Empirical and Deductive Reasoning Foundational Dis

courses.title: Introduction to Clinical Psychology
courses.description: Definition and history of clinical psychology, personality theory in clinical psychology, diagnosis and classification of disorders, assessment, psychotherapy, and ethical issues.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PSYCH 303-0.

courses.title: Cognitive Behavior Therapy
courses.description: Scientific foundations of cognitive behavior therapy for a wide range of disorders. Focus on the rationale for different treatments and evidence of efficacy and process. Comparisons with other scientifically validated treatments.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PSYCH 303-0.

courses.title: Special Topics in Social/Clinical/Personality
courses.description: Topic to be announced. Prerequisites vary. May be repeated for credit with different topic.

courses.title: Psychology of Attitudes
courses.description: Survey of social psychological research on attitudes; focus on the formation of attitudes, the relationship between attitudes and behavior, and attitude change.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PSYCH 213-0.

courses.title: Relationship Science
courses.description: Social-psychological analysis of close relationships, with an emphasis on romantic relationships. Interpersonal processes associated with relationship formation, development, and dissolution.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: The Holocaust: Psychological Themes & Perspectives
courses.description: Exploration of how particular psychological theories and concepts can inform understanding of the Holocaust at group and individual levels. Social and clinical psychology are emphasized.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PSYCH 110-0.
distroTags: Global Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity
Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Brain and Cognition
courses.description: Neural bases of cognitive processing with emphases on neuroimaging approaches in the areas of encoding, perception, attention, memory, language, reading, motor control, and executive functioning. Taught with CSD 303-0; may not receive credit for both courses.

courses.title: Brain Damage and the Mind
courses.description: Survey of human cognition as studied via investigations of brain damage and brain-imaging techniques. Mandatory.
courses.prereqRawText: prerequisites: PSYCH 221-0 or NEUROSCI 206-0.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Special Topics in Cognition/Neuroscience
courses.description: Topic to be announced. Prerequisites vary. May be repeated for credit with different topic.

courses.title: Consciousness
courses.description: Examines how psychologists, neuroscientists, computer scientists, and physicists have tackled fundamental questions about consciousness using empirical and theoretical methods.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: a course in cognition and/or neuroscience, or instructor permission based on a strong background in neurobiology and/or physics; PSYCH 205-0 strongly recommended.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisites: a course in cognition and/or neuroscience, or instructor permission based on a strong background in neurobiology and/or physics; PSYCH 205-0 strongly recommended.

courses.title: Positive Psychology: The Science of Well-Being
courses.description: Key developments in the field of positive psychology. Eudaimonic and hedonic wellbeing, mindfulness and flow, importance of social connections, emotional resilience, positive institutions.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PSYCH 110-0.

courses.title: Psychology of Beauty
courses.description: Theory, methodology, and empirical data related to the psychological impact of human beauty. Emphasis on both cultural and evolutionary perspectives.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PSYCH 110-0.

courses.title: Cultural Psychology
courses.description: Introduction to concepts and empirical methods used to study how culture shapes mind, brain, and behavior over multiple time scales, including over generations and the lifespan and across situational contexts.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PSYCH 110-0.

courses.title: Presenting Ideas & Data
courses.description: Understanding principles of cognitive psychology, data visualization, and graphic design to present ideas and data in an engaging, clear, and memorable manner. PSYCH 345-0 and COG_SCI 345-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.
distroTags: Social and Behavioral Science Foundational Discipl

courses.title: Psychology of Sex and Gender Differences
courses.description: Examination of sex differences and similarities. Evaluation of explanations for differences. Review of how gender affects achievement, relationships, and mental health.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PSYCH 110-0.

courses.title: Personality Research
courses.description: Research in personality, with emphasis on experimental approaches and methods. Basic concepts of test reliability and validity. Students conduct original research.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PSYCH 301-0, PSYCH 215-0.

courses.title: Social and Personality Development
courses.description: Research methods, theories, and facts relating to the development and modification of attitudes and behavior.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PSYCH 301-0; and PSYCH 213-0, PSYCH 215-0, or PSYCH 244-0.

courses.title: Stereotyping & Prejudice
courses.description: Analysis of the causes and consequences of stereotyping and prejudice, as well as methods used to study these issues. Students conduct original research.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PSYCH 301-0, PSYCH 213-0.

courses.title: Child Psychopathology
courses.description: Major forms of psychopathology present during childhood, including disorders exclusive to childhood and those that may appear during any developmental period. Developmental models of the etiology and course of major psychopathologies.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PSYCH 301-0; PSYCH 244-0 or PSYCH 303-0.

courses.title: Psychological Tests & Measures
courses.description: Explores the science of psychological assessment, including its history, test construction and evaluation, and common measures of personality, psychopathology, and ability. Students create and evaluate their own psychological measures.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PSYCH 301-0; PSYCH 213-0, PSYCH 215-0, or PSYCH 303-0.

courses.title: Cognitive Development
courses.description: Cognitive development in infancy, childhood, and adolescence. Focus on theoretical explanations for cognitive change and development in core domains, including language, space, number, time, and social relations.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PSYCH 301-0; PSYCH 244-0 or PSYCH 228-0.

courses.title: Language and Cognition
courses.description: Exposure to original research and theoretical perspectives on language and its relation to thought and behavior. Critical analysis of theories and methods. Topics may vary.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PSYCH 301-0; and PSYCH 228-0 or COG_SCI 211-0.

courses.title: Human Memory
courses.description: Scientific study of human memory, including memory systems of the brain, amnesia, remembering, forgetting, encoding, consolidation, memory suppression, and memory distortion. Emphasizes original research reports in cognitive neuroscience. Prerequisites: PSYCH 301-0 and PSYCH 228, 328, or 336.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: Psych 301-0 AND either Psych 228, Psych 328, or Psych 336 or consent of instructor.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisites: Psych 301-0 AND either Psych 228, Psych 328, or Psych 336 or consent of instructor.

courses.title: Advanced Statistics & Experimental Design
courses.description: Advanced topics in research design and analysis of data. Focus on both theory and applications.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PSYCH 301-0; 2 200-level mathematics courses.

courses.title: Children & the Law
courses.description: Examines research on children's involvement in the legal system as decision makers, witnesses, victims, and perpetrators from a developmental perspective . Taught with LEGAL_ST 381-0; may not receive credit for both courses.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PSYCH 301-0, PSYCH 244-0 strongly recommended.

courses.title: Psychology and Food
courses.description: Social, cultural, cognitive, evolutionary, and biological factors that influence food choice and consumption. Conducting and evaluating research on eating behavior. Students conduct original research.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PSYCH 301-0.

courses.title: Consumer Psychology and Marketing Research
courses.description: Application of psychological theories, findings, and methodologies to marketing research questions and problems. Students conduct a marketing research project for an actual client.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PSYCH 301-0.

courses.title: Advanced Seminar in Cognition or Neuroscience
courses.description: Discussion and critical analysis of research methods and findings in an area of cognitive psychology and/or neuroscience. Topics vary. May be repeated for credit with different topic.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PSYCH 301-0; additional prerequisites may apply.

courses.title: Advanced Seminar in Psychology
courses.description: Discussion and critical analysis of research methods and findings in psychology. Interdisciplinary focus, often spanning natural and social science aspects of psychology. Topics vary. May be repeated for credit with different topic.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: PSYCH 301-0; additional prerequisites may apply.

courses.title: Advanced Supervised Research 1
courses.description: Design, implementation, and reporting of a psychology research project.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PSYCH 301-0 and consent of instructor; PSYCH 397-2 must be taken with the same professor as PSYCH 397-1. Weinberg College limits on 398 and 399 enrollments also apply to 397.

prereqSegments.value: must be taken with the same professor as
. Weinberg College limits on 398 and 399 enrollments also apply to 397.

courses.title: Advanced Supervised Research 2
courses.description: Design, implementation, and reporting of a psychology research project.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: PSYCH 301-0 and consent of instructor; PSYCH 397-2 must be taken with the same professor as PSYCH 397-1. Weinberg College limits on 398 and 399 enrollments also apply to 397.

prereqSegments.value: must be taken with the same professor as
. Weinberg College limits on 398 and 399 enrollments also apply to 397.

courses.description: Open only to students pursuing departmental honors. They must apply for admission in spring quarter of junior year.

courses.description: Open only to students pursuing departmental honors. They must apply for admission in spring quarter of junior year.

courses.title: Senior Thesis Seminar
courses.description: Open only to students pursuing departmental honors. They must apply for admission in spring quarter of junior year.

courses.title: Independent Study
courses.description: Consent of instructor required. Generally limited to juniors and seniors. See department requirements for eligibility.

courses.title: College Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: First-Year Writing Seminar
courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.title: Introduction to the Study of Religion
courses.description: Introduces students to foundational themes, categories, content, and modes of analysis for studying religion.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Introduction to Religion, Media, and Culture
courses.description: Integrating multimedia modes of learning and engaging students in skill-developing mediamaking assignments, this course offers undergraduates an introduction to studying the phenomena of religion in relationship to dynamics of media, society, and culture. No prerequisites required.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Religion, Medicine & Suffering in the West
courses.description: Examination of religious healing ceremonies and Christian perspectives on pain and suffering in light of the meaning of physical pain in people's everyday lives.

courses.title: Introduction to Hinduism
courses.description: Unity and diversity of Hindu mythology, beliefs, and practices from ancient times to the present.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Introduction to Buddhism
courses.description: The Buddha's life and teachings, traditions that developed from these teachings, and systems of meditation, rituals, and ethics.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Introduction to Hebrew Bible
courses.description: Critical introduction to the Hebrew Bible: its history, content, and cultural influence, as well as the sociopolitical history of ancient Israel. RELIGION 220-0 and JWSH_ST 220-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Introduction to the New Testament
courses.description: Beginning, development, and content of the New Testament; its Jewish and Hellenistic environment.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Introduction to Judaism
courses.description: Main concepts in the theology of Judaism, main rituals and customs, and main institutions. RELIGION 230-0 and JWSH_ST 230-0 taught together; may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Introduction to Christianity
courses.description: Doctrine, worship, and institutions in the various branches of Christianity.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Introduction to Islam
courses.description: Principal beliefs and practices of Muslims set against the historic development of the faith.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Topics in Religion, Nature, and Ecology
courses.description: Explores the relationships between religion, nature, and ecology in text and practices across various traditions and cultural contexts. Topics may include environmental ethics, nature mysticism, religious conservation movements, environmental consciousness, and the role of spirituality in ecological activism. May be taken multiple times with different content.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Introduction to Black Religions: The North American Experience
courses.description: Introduces students to the variety of Black religions that developed during and after the Atlantic slave trade up to the present. Explores these traditions as continuities/changes of West African religious cosmologies. Examines the interplay between religion, politics, and the constructions of racial identities within various forms of Christianity, Islam, and other expressive cultures. RELIGION 262-0 and BLK_ST 262-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: U.S. Perspectives on Power, Justice, and Equity

courses.title: American Religious History from 1865 to the Great Depression
courses.description: Topics include urban religion, African American churches, Christians and foreign policy, immigrant religion, the spiritual crisis of the 1920s, and Pentecostalism.

courses.title: American Religious History from World War II to the Present
courses.description: Religion and the making of contemporary America, including Cold War religion, the Black Gods of the Great Migration, the rise of the Christian Right, and modern American Catholicism and Judaism.

courses.title: Introduction to Theology
courses.description: Theology as an academic discipline with a long history of asking fundamental questions about religious experience, texts, practices, and ideas in dialogue with thinkers past and present.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Theology of Love
courses.description: The concept of love from theological, historical, philosophical, and biblical perspectives.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Luther and the West
courses.description: Examination of Luther's work in the context of his life and times. Introduces basic dimensions of Western thought, showing how theology relates to broader cultural, political, social, and aesthetic issues. GERMAN 272-0 and RELIGION 272-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Mysticism and Spirituality
courses.description: Examines mysticism (mystical experience, relationship, prayer, or consciousness) and spirituality (spiritual presence and practice) across religious traditions and outside traditions.

courses.title: Introductory Readings in Sanskrit I
courses.description: This course is the first of a two quarter sequence that provides instruction in the Sanskrit language for beginners. No prior knowledge is required. It begins a comprehensive introduction to the Sanskrit language, while engaging students from the beginning in the practice of translation.

courses.title: Introductory Readings in Sanskrit II
courses.description: This course is the second of a two quarter sequence that provides instruction in the Sanskrit language for beginners. Students continue a comprehensive introduction to the Sanskrit language through study of its forms and through translation.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: RELIGION 305-1.

courses.title: Indian Philosophy
courses.description: This course offers an introduction to ancient Indian philosophical traditions. We will look first at the distinctive features of philosophy in South Asia, including its particular aims and presuppositions. Thereafter we will look in some detail at two major Hindu traditions from the classical period: Sāṁkhya-Yoga and Advaita Vedānta. We also briefly explore the Hindu traditions of Nyāya and Vaiśeṣika. From there, we will turn to look at classical Buddhist and Jain philosophy. Our major topics will include metaphysics, consciousness, ego, meditation and the release from human suffering.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Topics in Hinduism
courses.description: Content varies. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Buddhism in the Contemporary World
courses.description: An exploration of where, why, and how Buddhist practices, ideas, and iconography are spreading and being reinterpreted in the modern world.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.description: Radical changes that emancipation and modernity have brought to the religious expression of Judaism. May be repeated for credit.

courses.description: Historical and contemporary conceptions of sanctity, especially in Roman Catholicism and Eastern Orthodoxy.

courses.title: Islamic Law
courses.description: Evolution of Islamic Law from the Prophet Muhammad to the contemporary world, focusing on the impact of colonialism.

courses.title: Sufism
courses.description: The historical development of Islamic mystical traditions, with special attention to notions of mystical experience, sainthood, and devotion to the Prophet Muhammad.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Topics in Islam
courses.description: Selected topics in Islamic history and thought. May be repeated for credit with different topic.

courses.description: Topics, figures, events, and dynamics in the history of American women and religion.

courses.description: Content varies. Explores expressions and representations of religion, spirituality, moral questions, transcendence, meaning of life, etc. in TV and film.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Religion and Bioethics
courses.description: Analysis of contemporary dilemmas in medicine and the life sciences; responses to these dilemmas from religious perspectives.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Contemporary Religious Thought
courses.description: Content varies (e.g., convergence between religious paths, science and religion, politics and religion). May be repeated for credit with change of topic.

courses.title: Death: Myth and Meaning
courses.description: Ideas, beliefs, and practices pertaining to death from a variety of ancient and modern cultures. Immortality, afterlife, care for the dying, suicide, funerary rituals, transhumanism.

courses.title: Topics in Comparative Religion
courses.description: Content varies. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.title: Global Catholicism in the Contemporary World
courses.description: Historical and contemporary global Catholicism. Topics include the church and political modernity; local saints; controversies over worship styles; Catholics and political revolutions; the Vatican; the pontificate of John Paul II.

courses.title: Religion, Law, & Politics: Politics of Religious Diversity
courses.description: This course teaches students to think critically, comparatively, and globally about the intersections of religion, law, and politics. It considers how religious, legal, and political traditions intersect and interact in modern states and societies. The course is organized around a set of legal cases and supporting materials in the Teaching Law and Religion Case Study Archive. RELIGION 382-0 taught with POLI_SCI 382-0; may not receive credit for both.
distroTags: Ethical and Evaluative Thinking Foundational Disci

courses.description: Historical and contemporary subjects in the study of Catholic culture in the United States. May be taken multiple times with different content.

courses.title: Topics in Latin America Catholicism
courses.description: Historical and contemporary subjects in the study of Catholic culture in Latin America. May be taken multiple times with different content.

courses.title: Theories of Religion
courses.description: Ways of critically analyzing religious experience and its meaning. Phenomenology of religion, history of religions, comparative religions. Designed for majors and minors in Religious Studies but all are welcome.

courses.title: Media Construction
courses.description: Introduces the core components of media-idea, image, sound, and sequence- with the technical fundamentals involved in shooting and editing video. Students work with SLR and digital video cameras and with Photoshop and Final Cut Pro editing software, completing four projects in different genres during the quarter. Prerequisite for all upper-level production courses. Required for majors; typically taken in first year.

courses.title: First-Year Topics Seminar
courses.description: Beginning seminar focused around a special topic of media analysis, history, or theory. Students will learn research, analytic, and writing skills while focusing on issues relevant to film, media and/or digital arts and culture.

courses.title: Analyzing Media Texts
courses.description: Introduction to the study of the moving image. Basic elements of style across media including film, television, and interactive media. Focus on close analysis of texts to find significance. Prerequisite for upper level courses in the department. Required for majors; typically taken in first year.

courses.title: Foundations of Screenwriting
courses.description: Introduction to writing for the screen (film, television, and/or computer). Structure, character, dialogue, format, voice, scope, pace, context. Lecture/workshop. Prerequisite for upper-level writing courses in the department.

courses.title: Studies in Media Topics
courses.description: Theoretical or practical or both; emphasis on evolving trends.

courses.title: Introduction to Acting for the Screen
courses.description: Foundational concerns and practices for screen acting. Scene analysis, rehearsal, staging and camera space, casting, editing for performance. Creating and portraying characters for most effective capture by the camera. Film directing techniques as related to the actor. Required introductory course for the Acting for Screen module sequence.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites : RTVF 190-0 and either THEATRE 171-0 or THEATRE 273-1.

courses.title: Advanced Acting for Screen
courses.description: A retrospective critique of curricular and extracurricular performance work in the Acting for Screen module intended for seniors and other students about to graduate. Course will involve continued advanced scene work, as well as an evaluation of performer’s range and capabilities as they apply to film and television industries, and the student’s professional self-presentation through a self-edited actor reel and resume.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: RTVF 368-1/THEATRE 376 and at least one approved Acting for Screen module elective.

prereqSegments.value: /THEATRE 376 and at least one approved Acting for Screen module elective.

courses.title: Topics in Acting for the Screen
courses.description: Production-based courses on a range of practices and methods in acting for screen. May be repeated as topic varies. Counts as elective for the Acting for Screen module.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: RTVF 190-0.

courses.title: Practicum in RTVF Research
courses.description: Collaboration with a faculty member on design and execution of a media research project. Students learn how to construct and complete a research project and document results. Requires a paper or other form of work product as determined by the faculty member.

courses.title: Advanced Directing I
courses.description: Three-quarter sequence for students creating advanced artistic production, with critique of work throughout the production and post-production process; conceptual resources offered as needed. Students may work in any genre (documentary, narrative, experimental) and any medium. Admission based on portfolio of previous media work and proposal for project, including script and budget.

courses.description: Three-quarter sequence for students creating advanced artistic production, with critique of work throughout the production and post-production process; conceptual resources offered as needed. Students may work in any genre (documentary, narrative, experimental) and any medium. Admission based on portfolio of previous media work and proposal for project, including script and budget.

courses.description: Three-quarter sequence for students creating advanced artistic production, with critique of work throughout the production and post-production process; conceptual resources offered as needed. courses.description: Conversation, listening comprehension, reading, and composition. Exploration of modern Russian language and culture through readings, video, and film. Combined third-and fourth-year multi-skill course.

courses.description: Conversation, listening comprehension, reading, and composition. Exploration of modern Russian language and culture through readings, video, and film. Combined third-and fourth-year multi-skill course.

courses.title: Advanced Contemporary Russian
courses.description: Russian for advanced speakers, including heritage speakers. Stress on skills in speaking, reading, and writing in professional and formal environments. Taught entirely in Russian.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: RUSSIAN 302-3 or RUSSIAN 303-3 (or equivalent).

courses.title: Advanced Contemporary Russian
courses.description: Russian for advanced speakers, including heritage speakers. Stress on skills in speaking, reading, and writing in professional and formal environments.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisite: RUSSIAN 302-3 or RUSSIAN 303-3 (or equivalent).

courses.title: Finding Your Path
courses.description: A continuation of Finding Your Path: Pathways and Future Possibilities (SESP 251), a course that supports second-year students most impacted by the historical and contemporary realities of classism and racism as they map their path forward to career fulfillment. This class is a 0 credit class in which Pathways students conduct a search for a summer internship and then complete said internship.

courses.title: Civic Engagement 1- Participatory Policymaking
courses.description: Policy implementation involves more than just identifying and researching options, policymakers must, consider design program design, costs & sustainability; political feasibility, and building political support. In this class, you will learn how to design policy for implementation through Northwestern’s Participatory Budgeting process.

courses.title: Civic Engagement 2: Participatory Budgeting
courses.description: Democracy gives limited opportunities for citizens to influence decision-making. In this class you will learn to implement open democracy innovations, that are more inclusive, more representative, and lead to better policy outcomes, by implementing a campus-wide participatory budgeting process, where community decides how to spend $1000 to address climate change.

courses.title: Civic Engagement 3: Organizing, Gathering & Policy implementation
courses.description: How do we motivate people to take action? In this class, you will learn the techniques of relational organizing (canvassing, one-on-ones, public narrative), designing civic gatherings (Civic Saturdays) that move people to action to build social movements. You will also oversee policy implementation of community development projects selected in the participatory budgeting process.

courses.title: Understanding Knowledge
courses.description: What does it mean to know something? What are the different types of knowledge and what distinguishes them from one another? What counts as fact vs. opinion vs. belief and so on; who gets to decide and under what conditions? How is knowledge produced and how does it gain traction? How does the source and type of knowledge interact with socio-political-cultural constructs and systems of power and, in turn, how can "knowledge" be used to produce and/or perpetuate power and privilege or to empower those who are marginalized? In this course students will explore these and other questions to gain insight into the social production, distribution, consumption, interpretation, and operationalization of "knowledge."

courses.title: Human Development: Childhood and Adolescence
courses.description: Personal, social, and cognitive development from birth through adolescence. Interplay of biological and experiential factors on linguistic and conceptual development, ego, and personality.

courses.title: Human Development: Adulthood and Aging
courses.description: Psychological, sociological, and biological factors influencing socialization and development from young and middle adulthood through old age. Influences of family, school, and work on the individual.

courses.title: Introduction to Statistics and Research Methodology
courses.description: Definitions and classifications of terms used in quantitative methods; measures of typical and maximum performance, reliability, and validity checks; reporting and displaying data; interpreting results.

courses.title: Leaders Lab
courses.description: Leaders Lab is an interactive, engaging and dynamic fall quarter course that was created and designed for incoming first and second-year students in SESP to reflect, experience and engage in dialogue about the "big" questions in life such as: who am I? What is my purpose in life? How can I be me? What are my responsibilities to the communities I'm a part of? Why do I serve? What is leadership? How can I lead? This course is associated with the SESP Leadership Institute. Only students enrolled in the leadership institute can register for this course.

courses.title: Introduction to Social Science Research
courses.description: This course provides an overview of how social scientists study human behavior and society. It will introduce students to the different stages of the research process: literature review, research question formulation, research design, data collection, analysis, and presentation of findings.

courses.title: Special Topics
courses.description: Foundational work on special topics.

courses.title: Community Based Research Methodologies: Educational Justice
courses.description: This course examines the histories, ideas, practices, relations and possible futures that shape struggles for educational justice and human thriving. The course is unique in that it brings together an intergenerational group of thinkers and learners (high school students, undergraduate students, youth workers, graduate students, professors, high school teachers and community members) to engage in collaborative study, reflection and design.

courses.title: Field Research Methods
courses.description: Guided practice in systematic and participant observation. Observer bias, field notes, unobtrusive measures.

courses.title: Peer-Led Learning: Theory and Practice
courses.description: SESP 291 is the training program for students working as first-time mentors in the Peer Leaders program. It is taken over two academic quarters, with each quarter offering .25 credit (a total of .5 credit).

courses.title: Leadership Studio I
courses.description: Learning organizing requires building a real organization. Students accepted to the certificate program will further develop their organizing skills by taking a leadership role on the executive board of Open Democracy Northwestern (undergraduate club). Leaders will develop strategy, train club members, manage operations. Leaders will receive weekly coaching from instructors. Course is only open to students who have completed the first year sequence of the certificate.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: Prior to 2024-2025 students will need to have completed SESP 195-0 3 times to enroll. 2024-2025 and beyond, students will need to have completed SESP 195-1, 195-2, 195-3 to enroll.

Leaders will develop strategy, train club members, manage operations. Leaders will receive weekly coaching from instructors. courses.title: Student Organized Seminar
courses.description: Courses proposed by students and supervised by faculty sponsors on special topics approved by the SESP undergraduate education director. May be taken only once per quarter; pass/no credit only. Consultation with the SESP student affairs assistant dean advised.

courses.title: Causal Methods for Evaluating Policy
courses.description: This course will provide students with a framework for understanding causal inference and a toolkit for making causal claims using quantitative data.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: Students need to have taken SESP 210-0 or any 200-level STATS course.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisites: Students need to have taken

courses.title: Race and Education
courses.description: Conceptual underpinnings of the construct of race and how conceptions of race have influenced the course of education in the United States.

courses.title: Crafting Child Policy
courses.description: This course is open to undergraduate students interested in the intersection of child development, social policy, and applied research. The course will guide students in how to apply psychological theory and rigorous research methods to the study of child and family policies in real-world settings. Working in groups, students will have the opportunity to answer pressing, current questions posed by the Illinois Governor’s Office of Early Childhood Education and Chicago’s Office of the Mayor. Group projects have the potential to help inform the structure and development of the state’s innovative policies for young children (birth to 8) and their families.

courses.title: Holocaust Memory, Memorial and Museums
courses.description: What is Holocaust memory? How has Holocaust memory changed over time, and how does the Holocaust continue to affect our understanding of trauma, atrocity, and human rights today? This seminar addresses individual memory, including survivor and witness testimony, memory and trauma, and the impact of the Holocaust on survivors' families and communities.

courses.title: Race, Adolescence, and School Discipline
courses.description: In recent years, racial disparities in school discipline have attracted the attention of educators, policymakers, parents, and the general public. Why is it so hard for legal, political, and educational institutions to improve school discipline? How do intersections of race, gender, and social class matter for students' experiences of school discipline? Are there schools that are getting discipline right? What does that look like, and to what extent can other schools learn from their successes? In this course, we will learn about evidence-based policy improvements and imagine how to create schools where race does not predict discipline.

courses.title: Special Topics
courses.description: Advanced work on special topics.

courses.title: Magic Monsters & the Holocaust
courses.description: In this course, we'll explore public learning about the Holocaust through popular film and fiction. We'll question which historical narratives are being told and which are being ignored, and we'll ask why and how genres like fantasy, sci-fi, fairy tales, and time travel are commonly used to bring stories of mass-violence to the public.

courses.title: BSED/BSJ Experiential Learning Community Workshop Series
courses.description: In their second year, students choose 1 experiential education requirement – either the SESP Practicum, Student Teaching, or Medill Journalism Residency for 4 units of credit. The students participate in the Community Workshop Series during the year they complete their experiential education requirement.

courses.title: Research Apprenticeship
courses.description: Opportunity to participate in faculty research projects.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: consent of the faculty member and the SESP assistant dean for student affairs; submission of completed Request for Independent Study/Special Courses Form at registration.

prereqSegments.value: Prerequisites: consent of the faculty member and the SESP assistant dean for student affairs; submission of completed Request for Independent Study/Special Courses Form at registration.

courses.title: Advanced Research Design
courses.description: Overview of research methods that may be used to design and implement the honors thesis.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: SESP 210-0 and SESP 272-0 recommended.

courses.title: Experiential Learning: Practicum
courses.description: The practicum is a substantive, full quarter (four academic credits) Experiential Education requirement typically completed during a SESP undergraduate student’s junior year (inclusive of the summer before/after).

courses.title: Experiential Learning: Practicum Study Abroad
courses.description: SESP students who are interested can fulfill their Experiential Education/Practicum requirement as well as their Global Engagement requirement through several different internship abroad opportunities offered through Northwestern’s Global Learning Office.

courses.title: Senior Thesis Seminar
courses.description: Students develop, design, implement, and evaluate a research project under a faculty advisor's guidance.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: senior status, cumulative GPA by the end winter quarter of the junior year, recommendation for the honors program from SESP 391-0 instructor(s); consent of program director.

courses.title: Independent Study
courses.description: Faculty-supervised study of special topics of the student's own choosing and not covered in regular courses.
courses.prereqRawText: Prerequisites: consent of the supervising faculty member(s) and the SESP assistant dean for student affairs; submission of completed Request for Independent Study/Special Courses Form at registration.

courses.description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

courses.description: For students who wish to qualify for honors by writing a senior thesis.

courses.description: For students who wish to qualify for honors by writing a senior thesis.
